etymology: Initialism of As Fast As Possible. Coined by Professor Sendy as a brisk counterpart to the long-established "ASAP" (as soon as possible). Though formed by initials rather than by the blending of two words, it is presented as honorary vocabulary of the wombo lexicon on the grounds that it readily conjoins with true wombos in everyday speech. The derived form "afapmaxxing" appends the intensifying suffix -maxx(ing), denoting the strenuous pursuit of a quality to its limit; the verbal form "afapped" treats the initialism as a finite verb.

parts.label: informal
parts.forms: AFAP (initialism of as fast as possible)

senses.def: with the greatest possible speed; at the earliest opportunity that swiftness, rather than mere timing, allows.

examples.text: An example is to visit my guyknowballogist AFAP, because last time I appricot to brokly loreain the glazed balledge.

subsenses.def: used as a brisk imperative or request, distinguished from ASAP by its emphasis on raw velocity rather than scheduling.

example.text: Get over here AFAP.

senses.def: the acronym itself, regarded as a unit of speech that, while not a true wombo, readily joins with wombos in conversation.

examples.text: While not a wombo itself, this acronym can easily be used with other wombos.

senses.def: to do something as fast as possible; to apply the principle of maximal speed to an activity.

examples.text: Let's go afapmaxxing this tweekend.

subsenses.def: afapmaxxing: to pursue speed to its utmost limit, with the intensifying suffix -maxxing.

example.text: You could even maxx this out and say let's go afapmaxxing this tweekend.

notes: On-screen black text box gives the headword as AFAP and the etymology box as "As Fast As Possible"; this spelling/expansion is authoritative. IMPORTANT classificatory caveat: in the captions Sendy explicitly states this is an acronym and "not a wombo itself," presenting it instead as a term that pairs readily with wombos. It is recorded here as a Professor Sendy dictionary entry because the video is structurally one of his definition clips. Senses and the derived verb/intensified forms (afapmaxxing, afapped) are drawn directly from his demonstrated usage. The verb sense and the deadpan grammatical labels are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with his examples. The closing line "and it just came Afapped" is rendered as spoken; meaning there is loose and treated as a playful nonce verbing. The shirt name in the description (the "Lowkirkentologic..." merch tee) and the bottom-screen "GUYKNOWBALLOGIST" relate to other wombos and are not the headword.

etymology: Blend of "amazing" and "maxxing" (the slang suffix "-maxxing," denoting the deliberate maximization of a desirable trait, from "max out"). The conventional spelling of the second element, with its doubled "x," is preserved across the seam, yielding "amaxxing" rather than the expected "amaxing."

senses.def: Of a thing or person: amazing to a maximized or superlative degree; so impressive as to seem deliberately optimized for excellence.

subsenses.def: Used as a term of high praise directed at a person, esp. for a skill or quality pushed to its peak.

example.text: You're amaxxing.

subsenses.def: Applied to an ability or performance regarded as reaching its absolute summit.

example.text: Your amaxxing clavaxxing skills are climaxing.

examples.text: Reserve it for the moment something is, as he puts it, level 67.

notes: On-screen black text box confirms the headword spelling "Amaxxing" and the blue etymology boxes confirm "Amazing + Maxxing." Definition is paraphrased from Sendy's spoken cue that the wombo is for "when something's level 67" (an idiom of his for an off-the-charts, maximal degree). Both usage examples ("You're amaxxing"; "Your amaxxing clavaxxing skills are climaxing") are his actual caption lines, lightly cleaned. Sendy demonstrates only the adjectival form, so a single part of speech is given. Enrichment beyond his literal words: the IPA, the sub-sense split (a: praise of a person; b: praise of a skill) which organizes his two examples, the usage label "informal," and the etymological note on the preserved double-x spelling. The "level 67" example is rendered as a usage gloss rather than presented as a stand-alone quotation. Synonyms drawn from neighboring wombos he stacks in the same clip (twincredible, bawesomesauce) as broadly praise-coded terms.

etymology: Blend of analysis and revision, the latter syllable supplying the sense of a redone or corrected version. Coined by Professor Sendy.

senses.def: A revised or corrected redo of an analysis, especially of a piece of so-called peak cinema; a second, improved attempt at explaining something that was delivered badly the first time.

examples.text: Dude, your peanemis was really bad. Could you please just give me an analvision?

subsenses.def: The act or instance of requesting such a redo from someone who has botched their delivery.

example.text: You can even ask him to quiche it out: could you please quichemaxx this analvision?

senses.def: To rework or revise an analysis after a poor first delivery; to produce a corrected second version on request.

examples.text: If he does a really good job of analvisioning it, you can tell him so.

senses.def: One who excels at revising an analysis; a person who delivers an outstanding corrected redo.

examples.text: If he does a really good job of revising it, you can even say, hey, I think you're an analvisionary.

notes: Frame f02 confirms the headword spelling "Analvision" and the etymology boxes "Analysis + Revision"; upload_date 20260328 sets the year to 2026. Sendy frames the word around delivering a "peak cinema analysis" and someone "doing a bad job of quiche-loraining it," i.e. botching the explanation. The core sense is the corrected/redone analysis you request. He explicitly demonstrates three forms: the noun ("give me an analvision"), a verb usage where the related wombo "quichemaxx" is applied to it ("quichemaxx this analvision," i.e. maximize/improve the redo), and the agent noun "analvisionary" for someone who revises it well; the verb and agent-noun parts below are house-style enrichment built directly on his demonstrated forms. The "quiche it out / quichemaxx" and "quiche loraining" references are separate Sendy wombos invoked here, not defined in this entry. The senses for "quiche out" / "quiche loraining" are paraphrased from context. Examples are his actual caption usage lightly cleaned.

etymology: A blend (portmanteau) of ball (in the colloquial sense of athletic competition, esp. as in the vernacular assertion "I know ball") + knowledge, the second element clipped and fused so that the -edge of knowledge is grafted onto ball. Coined by Professor Sendy as the headword of a "Ball Wombos" series exploring the lexical family generated by ball: compare balledgeable (ball + knowledgeable), guyknowballogist and guyknowballogy (guy + knows + ball + -ologist / -ology), ballmost (ball + almost), and ballways (ball + always). On-screen etymology given as Ball + Knowledge = Balledge.

senses.def: A command of the facts, history, and finer points of competitive sport, esp. as professed by one who insists "I know ball"; sporting erudition.

subsenses.def: Such knowledge regarded as a measurable quantity or store, as one might possess much or little of it.

example.text: Ball plus knowledge equals balledge.

subsenses.def: By extension, demonstrable expertise in any narrow pursuit treated with the gravity ordinarily reserved for athletics.

example.text: He brings real balledge to the broadcast booth.

senses.def: In the elevated variant Eliche Balledge (from elite + niche): a rarefied, specialist grade of such knowledge, the province of the few.

examples.text: Elite plus niche plus ball plus knowledge equals Eliche Balledge.

senses.def: Possessed of balledge; well versed in the lore and mechanics of sport.

examples.text: Ball plus knowledgeable equals balledgeable.

senses.def: A guy who knows ball; one who has elevated the possession of balledge into a recognized discipline, the practitioner (guyknowballogist) of the study (guyknowballogy).

examples.text: Guy plus knows plus ball plus ologist equals guyknowballogist.
Guy plus knows plus ball plus ology equals guyknowballogy.

notes: Compilation video titled BALL WOMBOS presenting eight related coinages on the root ball; the central, standalone headword is Balledge (Ball + Knowledge), so the entry is built around it with the demonstrated derived forms (balledgeable; guyknowballogist; guyknowballogy) captured as additional parts of speech in the same family, since Sendy explicitly shows multiple forms. On-screen text is authoritative for spelling: Balledge, Eliche Balledge, Balledgeable, Guyknowballogist, Guyknowballogy. The captions for items 16/21 read "Guyknowballogist"/"Guyknowballogy" and match the frame. Sense built on the well-known "I know ball" meme (claimed deep familiarity with sports); the closing pair "Smart + I Know Ball = I Know Small" is Sendy's deadpan inversion gag and is noted but not given its own sense. Definitions, IPA, and usage examples are original house-style enrichment consistent with the displayed etymology; senses 1b and the figurative shade are tasteful extensions of Sendy's literal meaning.

etymology: A blend (wombo) of ball + almost, coined by Professor Sendy and presented as the "Wombo of the Week." The first element draws on the colloquial sense of ball as instinctive, authentic competence in basketball (as in the phrase "to know ball"); the second supplies the notion of falling just short. The compound thus names the condition of nearly, but not quite, possessing such competence.

senses.def: The state of nearly possessing a genuine, instinctive grasp of basketball (or, by extension, of any pursuit) while falling just short of true competence; the condition of almost, but not quite, knowing ball.

examples.text: When someone almost knows ball, that's ballmost.

subsenses.def: broadly, a near-miss of authentic mastery in any domain, in which understanding stops one increment shy of the real thing.

example.text: His scouting reports are pure ballmost: confident, detailed, and wrong in the one way that matters.

senses.def: a person who almost knows ball; one who exhibits this condition.

examples.text: He's a total ballmost, calls every shot before it clanks off the rim.

senses.def: to come close to demonstrating real understanding of basketball or another field without ever quite arriving; to perform at the threshold of competence.

examples.text: He ballmosted his way through the whole pickup game, looking right up until the ball left his hands.

notes: No spoken captions exist for this video (no SRT file); the entry rests entirely on the clear on-screen text box, which gives the headword Ballmost, the etymology Ball + Almost, and the gloss "When someone almost knows ball." The single noun sense is taken directly from Sendy's on-screen definition. The second noun sense (a person who is ballmost) and the verb part of speech are tasteful house-style enrichments extending his meaning into related forms; Sendy himself demonstrates only the abstract-state noun. Confidence is medium because the meaning is well-supported by the frame but no audio elaboration was available to confirm shades of usage. Example sentences are constructed in the spirit of his on-screen gloss rather than transcribed from his speech.

etymology: A fivefold wombo (Word + Combo) blending ball + Sendy + peak + cinema + analysis. The compound fuses "ball" (sport) with the inventor's own name (Sendy) and the appraising trio of "peak," "cinema," and "analysis," yielding a single term for a top-tier, cinematically delivered work of sports commentary. Coined by Professor Sendy and conventionally preceded by the definite article ("the Balsend Peanemis"). Compare the contemporaneous self-referential coinages of Professor Sendy, in which his own name is folded into the morphology of the word.

senses.def: An exceptionally enthusiastic and knowledgeable piece of sports storytelling or commentary; a virtuoso, almost cinematic feat of athletic analysis regarded as the definitive take on its subject.

examples.text: Please drop the Balsend Peanemis.
When you drop the Balsend Peanemis, everyone is going to be impressed.

subsenses.def: Such a take regarded as a benchmark of expertise, the recognition or non-recognition of which marks the connoisseur from the layman.

example.text: You wouldn't know the Balsend Peanemis if it hit you like a ton of bricks.

subsenses.def: By extension, the act of delivering such commentary, especially in the idiom to drop the Balsend Peanemis.

example.text: So impressed they are going to start sending you tailored swifts.

notes: On-screen spelling confirmed from frame f02: black title box reads "The Balsend Peanemis" with blue etymology box "Ball + Sendy + Peak + Cinema + Analysis" (auto-captions render it variously as "Balsend Peanemis" / "ball sendy peak cinema and analysis"). Headword recorded as "Balsend Peanemis"; it appears conventionally with the definite article ("the"), captured in the IPA variants and the usage label. Definition is paraphrased in house style from Sendy's stated meaning: instructions to "use this wombo... [to] tell a really enthusiastic and knowledgeable story" about ball, framed as "peak cinema and analysis." Examples are his actual caption usages, lightly cleaned (one expletive softened to "ton of bricks" per the censored caption). House-style enrichment beyond Sendy's literal words: the plural form "Balsend Peanemises," the part-of-speech/sense structure, sub-sense (b) generalizing the verbal idiom "to drop the...," and the etymology note on his self-referential coinage pattern. Year 2026 from upload_date 20260527. This video doubles as a Speechify partnership / merch promo in its description, but the core content is a genuine wombo definition.

etymology: A wombo (blend) of banger ("an outstanding, crowd-pleasing item") + compound ("a word formed by combining others") + vocabulary ("the body of words used in a language"), the medial element nodding to the percussive pairing "bang and pound." Coined by Professor Sendy (2026). The internal -pound- additionally puns on the slang collocation "bangin' and poundin'," signaling vocabulary of the highest impact.

senses.def: vocabulary of the highest order; an exceptional, crowd-pleasing word or coinage, especially a particularly inventive wombo (word-combo).

examples.text: He's already dropping bangpoundulary.
He's already loreained sixty-seven wombos in the past forty-one minutes; those wombo combos are going to be bangin' and poundin' all night.

subsenses.def: the quality or knack of producing such standout coinages on demand.

example.text: She's always quiche-loreaining bangpoundulary.

senses.def: to deliver or work over (vocabulary) with maximum impact and inventiveness; to coin or trade standout wombos.

examples.text: Let's bangpound-max this vocabulary until we deadassionately nonchalashout all over the brarpet.

notes: On-screen black box gives the authoritative spelling "Bangpoundulary"; blue etymology boxes read "Banger + Compound + Vocabulary." Captions confirm the meaning: a top-tier piece of vocabulary / standout wombo ("use this when your brousin drops the quichest of wombos"). Sendy demonstrates a noun (the dominant form, "dropping bangpoundulary") and, in the final escalation, a verb-like use ("let's bangpound[-max] this vocabulary"); the verb part of speech and its inflected forms are tasteful house-style enrichment extrapolated from that single demonstrated form. The plural "bangpoundularies," IPA, sense 1a (the knack), and the pun gloss in the etymology are also house-style enrichment consistent with his usage. Example sentences are lightly cleaned from his actual captions; surrounding wombos (loreained, quiche, deadassionately, nonchalashout, brarpet) are his own and were retained where they form his real example sentences.

etymology: Blend of banquet (a ceremonial or abundant meal) and quarter zip (a pullover top fastened by a short zipper at the collar). The headword is itself a productive base for further blending: BARQUARZIDENTIAL combines banquarterzip with residential, and BARQUARZIDENTOLOGY combines banquarterzip with the learned suffix -ology (after the model of dentistry / odontology), the two derivatives sharing the contracted stem barquarzid-. First attested in the speech of Professor Sendy.

senses.def: An invented term coupling the idea of a banquet with that of a quarter-zip pullover; the wearing of casual quarter-zip attire to an occasion that nominally calls for the spread or formality of a banquet.

examples.text: Hey, man — did you get enough to eat at the banquet, quarter, zip?

subsenses.def: the garment itself when worn in such a setting; a quarter-zip pressed into formal service.

example.text: Banquet, quarter, zip. Banquet, quarter, zip.

senses.def: A re-blend of banquarterzip with residential: pertaining to a domestic or residential setting rather than an industrial or commercial one; homely, neighborhood-scale.

examples.text: I'm not gonna go industrial — I'm gonna go bank, residential.

senses.def: A re-blend of banquarterzip with the suffix -ology: the (mock) academic study or science of the banquarterzip and its residential province; a field of formal scholarship in the matter.

examples.text: In school I studied Barquarzidentology.

subsenses.def: by extension, the body of expertise possessed by a practitioner of the discipline.

notes: On-screen frames are authoritative: headword in the black box is "Banquarterzip"; the blue/etymology box reads "Banquet + Quarter Zip"; the two lower boxes show the derived forms "Barquarzidential" and "Barquarzidentology". Auto-captions garbled these (rendering the chant as "Banquet, quarter, zip," giving "Residential" / "Bank. Residential" for Barquarzidential, and "Banquorzydology" / "banquorzydology" for Barquarzidentology) — I trusted the frames for spelling per the rules. Definitions are original house-style paraphrase. ENRICHMENT (beyond what Sendy literally said): Sendy supplies only the chant, the "enough to eat at the banquet" line, the industrial-vs-residential contrast, and "in school I studied [it]"; he does not give explicit prose definitions. I inferred sense 1's "casual quarter-zip at a banquet-formality occasion" reading, added the garment subsense 1a, framed sense 2 around the industrial/residential contrast he draws, and supplied subsense 3a (the practitioner's expertise) and the -ology gloss. The contracted stem analysis (barquarzid-) and the odontology/dentistry parallel in the etymology are my additions. Upload date 20260105 → year 2026.

etymology: A serially compounded coinage assembled on screen in three accreting stages. Stage one fuses bigarchenuinely (itself big + genuinely, with an interposed -arche- intensifier) with deadassionately (deadass + passionately) to yield bigarcheadassionately. Stage two layers on hitdasealibrantly (hit + vibrantly) to give bigarcheadassionitdasealibrantly. Stage three annexes a nominal coda derived from "ontological flow state," lightly reshaped as -alibrentologiclowstate, producing the maximal form. The result is a stacked superlative adverb in which each successive root raises the degree of sincerity, intensity, vigor, and immersive focus.

parts.label: intensifying; hyperbolic
parts.forms: also in the shorter stages Bigarcheadassionately and Bigarcheadassionitdasealibrantly

senses.def: in a manner combining genuine, large-scale sincerity with deep passion, vibrant energy, and total immersive focus; with the fullest possible degree of wholehearted, in-the-zone commitment.

examples.text: He approached the task bigarcheadassionately, then bigarcheadassionitdasealibrantly, and at last bigarcheadassionitdasealibrentologiclowstate.

subsenses.def: (of sincerity) genuinely and on a grand scale; from the bigarchenuinely root.

example.text: She meant it bigarcheadassionately.

subsenses.def: (of focus) so as to enter and sustain an ontological flow state; in peak, fully absorbed concentration.

example.text: He locked in bigarcheadassionitdasealibrentologiclowstate.

senses.def: characterized by genuine, passionate, vibrant, and fully flow-immersed commitment; done with maximal sincere intensity.

examples.text: It was a bigarcheadassionitdasealibrentologiclowstate effort.

notes: No caption track (SRT) exists for this video, so there is no spoken definition; the entry is reconstructed entirely from the on-screen black-box headword and the blue-box etymology chain, which is fully legible in frame f02. The displayed build is three-stage: Bigarchenuinely + Deadassionately = Bigarcheadassionately; + Hitdasealibrantly = Bigarcheadassionitdasealibrantly; + Ontological Flow State = Bigarcheadassionitdasealibrentologiclowstate (the maximal headword, also the video title and description). The three shortened stages are documented in the description as a usage sequence and treated here as inflectional/derivational forms of one lemma. Meaning is inferred from the constituent roots since Sendy gives no narrated gloss; the adjective part of speech and the synonyms are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with those roots. IPA is a plausible rendering of the maximal form. The description also promotes a related merch item, the 'Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura' shirt, which is a separate coinage not defined here. Confidence medium due to absence of a spoken definition.

etymology: Blend of big + the intensive prefix arch- (chief, supreme, of the highest order) + genuinely. Coined by Professor Sendy. The frames available do not display Sendy's customary (Root + Root) etymology boxes, so the morpheme breakdown is reconstructed from the surface form; arch- is read as the central intensifier yoking "big" to "genuinely."

senses.def: in a manner that is sincere to an exaggerated or superlative degree; truly and wholeheartedly, with emphasis on the magnitude of the speaker's sincerity.

examples.text: When you bigarchenuinely fall in love with a product.
I'm going to try this burger, and wow, what a product — bigarchenuinely.

subsenses.def: used to affirm that an enthusiasm, especially for a consumer product, is heartfelt rather than ironic.

example.text: I'm lowkirkentologically gonna try this — bigarchenuinely.

subsenses.def: used as a standalone interjective intensifier appended to a statement of praise for emphasis.

example.text: Wow, what a product. Bigarchenuinely.

senses.def: characterized by sincerity of the largest and highest order; sincere in a way that exceeds ordinary genuineness.

examples.text: It was a bigarchenuine love for that burger.

notes: The on-screen red headword label reads BIGARCHENUINELY; this spelling is taken as authoritative. Neither available frame (f02, f04) shows the blue (Root + Root) etymology boxes, so the etymology is reconstructed from the surface form rather than read from Sendy's panel — hence medium confidence. The captioned meaning is clear from context (an intense, sincere declaration of love for a product/burger), but the precise gloss is inferred from usage rather than an explicit on-screen definition. The video pairs bigarchenuinely with a sibling coinage, lowkirkentologically, and references a merch item ("Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura shirt"); these are noted but not defined here. The adjective form (bigarchenuine) and the deadpan emphatic-adverb sense labels are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with Sendy's demonstrated adverbial usage.

etymology: A paired entry of two blended coinages introduced together by Professor Sendy. BRARPET: blend of "bro" (informal term of address for a male companion or roommate) + "carpet," denoting floor covering held in common by such companions. CLAVACUUM: blend of "clavicular" (of or relating to the collarbone) + "vacuum," denoting a vacuum cleaner deployed in the pursuit of self-optimization. Both follow the standard wombo pattern of fusing two lexemes at a shared phonetic seam.

senses.def: A carpet purchased and shared with one's bro, typically in a cohabitation or roommate arrangement; floor covering held in common between male housemates.

examples.text: I just had a nonchalashout all over the brarpet and I burgunch all over it.
Don't worry, broommate, I'll looksmaxx the brarp.

senses.def: A vacuum cleaner used in the course of optimizing the appearance and order of one's home; a vacuum enlisted in domestic self-improvement.

examples.text: I'm gonna get out the clavac and I'm gonna clavacmaxx the twiving room.

senses.def: To vacuum a space as an act of self-optimization; to clean a room with a clavacuum in pursuit of an improved domestic appearance.

examples.text: I'm gonna clavacmaxx the twiving room, then I'll hit up the twitchen, and finally I'll looksmaxx the kids' playroom.

notes: Double-wombo video: Sendy defines two words in one post, so the headword is given as the paired form "Brarpet/Clavacuum" (matching the on-screen black text box). Etymologies are stated explicitly in the captions ("bro plus carpet"; "clavicular plus vacuum"). For clavacuum, Sendy demonstrates a derived verb form "clavacmaxx" (to vacuum as a self-improvement ritual) and a clipped noun "clavac"; these are recorded as a second part of speech. The verb sense for brarpet is not attested, so brarpet is given as a noun only. The examples are dense with other unrelated wombos (nonchalashout, burgunch, bigarchenuinely, looksmaxx, broommate, twiving room, twitchen, brozebo); these are quoted as-is in the example sentences but not separately defined here, as they fall outside this entry's headword. IPA is a plausible house-style reconstruction. House-style framing (usage register, the "self-optimization ritual" gloss for the -maxx verb) is light original enrichment consistent with Sendy's stated meaning.

etymology: Sentence-blend (a "wombo") compressing the spoken phrase bro is pushing fifty into a single word. Formed by eliding bro is pushing into brisushing — bro contracted against is, then fused with pushing by shared sibilance — and suffixing the numeral fifty in its reduced, aphetic form -ifty, the initial f- of fifty being absorbed into the preceding -ing. The idiom pushing fifty (nearing the age of fifty) is itself a fixed phrase dating to the early 20th century.

parts.label: informal
parts.forms: Brisushingifty; rarely Brisushingforty, Brisushingsixty by analogy

senses.def: A man, typically referred to as "bro," who is approaching the age of fifty; one who is in the late years of his forties and visibly nearing the half-century mark.

examples.text: Bro is pushing fifty.

subsenses.def: By extension, the condition or fact of a man being on the verge of turning fifty; middle-aged decline observed in a peer.

example.text: We don't talk about it, but the whole group chat knows it's Brisushingifty.

subsenses.def: Loosely, any man whose conduct, appearance, or energy betrays an age he would rather conceal.

example.text: He showed up in cargo shorts and New Balances — full Brisushingifty.

notes: On-screen headword spelling \"Brisushingifty\" is authoritative and consistent across all six frames; the auto-caption SRT mangles it (\"brisushingifty\", \"Brisushing\", \"Brisushingifty\") and breaks it into the syllables BRO / IS / PUSHING / FIFTY, which confirms the source phrase \"Bro is pushing fifty.\" There is NO blue-box (Root + Root) etymology card in this video — it is one of Sendy's rapid spoken-syllable sentence-wombos rather than a two-root compound — so the etymology above is reconstructed from the highlighted caption fragments. Sendy never gives an explicit dictionary-style definition; he only recites the source phrase, so the gloss (a man nearing fifty) is derived directly from the idiom \"pushing fifty.\" ENRICHMENT (mine, not Sendy's): sense 1a (the abstract condition) and 1b (the appearance/conduct extension), both example sentences except the bare \"Bro is pushing fifty,\" the analogical variant forms (Brisushingforty/sixty), the synonyms, and the IPA. Year 2026 from upload_date 20260114. The description/title contains an unrelated business contact email (stlbrendonofficial@gmail.com) and the shirt is a St. Louis CITY SC jersey — neither bears on the word.

etymology: Combining form, from BRO (clipping of brother, informal term of fraternal address) + the initial element of a noun denoting a role, profession, or familial relation. In each coinage the "bro-" element supplants the first syllable (or onset) of the host word, after the model of established blends: Bro + prophet → Brophet; Bro + opp → Bropp; Bro + sister → Brister; Bro + roommate → Broommate; Bro + unc → Brunc; Bro + distant cousin → Bristant Brousin; Bro + teacher → Breacher; Bro + counselor → Brounselor; Bro + hitchhiker → Brochhiker; Bro + officer → Brofficer; Bro + doctor → Broctor. Compare the earlier consumer coinages bromance and brogrammer, of which these are the systematized descendants.

parts.label: informal
parts.forms: also bruh-, brah-; commonly replaces the initial syllable of the host word

senses.def: A productive word-forming element prefixed to a noun denoting a role, profession, or familial relation, yielding a jocular variant understood as the masculine, fraternal, or bro-coded counterpart of that role.

subsenses.def: Used with terms of office or profession, denoting one who fills the role in a conspicuously laddish or bro-affiliated manner.

example.text: Bro plus officer gives you Brofficer; bro plus doctor gives you Broctor.

subsenses.def: Used with terms of kinship or domestic relation, denoting a male peer treated as that relation.

example.text: Bro plus sister is Brister, and bro plus roommate is Broommate.

examples.text: Bro plus prophet equals Brophet.
Bro plus distant cousin equals Bristant Brousin.

senses.def: Any of the individual coinages formed on this pattern; a person designated by a Bro- blend, as a Brophet (a bro regarded as a prophet), a Bropp (a bro who is an opp, or rival), a Brunc (a bro treated as an uncle), or a Breacher (a bro who teaches).

examples.text: Bro plus unc is your Brunc; bro plus teacher is your Breacher.
Bro plus counselor is a Brounselor, and bro plus hitchhiker is a Brochhiker.

notes: Source video is a rapid-fire compilation rather than a single-word definition: Sendy chains eleven coinages (Brophet, Bropp, Brister, Broommate, Brunc, Bristant Brousin, Breacher, Brounselor, Brochhiker, Brofficer, Broctor), each presented as Bro + [host word]. Because there is no one headword, the entry is lemmatized on the productive combining form Bro-, which is what the video actually demonstrates; the on-screen (Bro + Root) boxes are authoritative for every spelling and are quoted exactly. The captions name the parts but do not gloss any single sense aloud, so the definitions are house-style readings of the consistent pattern (a male-coded, fraternal recasting of a role or relation), flagged here as enrichment. Spellings preserved exactly as on screen, including the capitalized "BRoommate" in the caption track (rendered "Broommate" on the frame and in the description). On-screen frame f02 shows the full list; no fallback frame needed. The description also plugs a "Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura" merch shirt, unrelated to this entry.

etymology: Blend of bro (clipped form of brother, used as a familiar term of address among male peers) + relative (a person connected by blood or marriage). Coined by Professor Sendy.

senses.def: A member of one's extended family with whom one shares the easy camaraderie of a close male friend; a kinsman treated and addressed as a bro rather than with formal familial deference.

examples.text: Yo, dap me up, brolative — I'm finna venture out into WigArtha to pick me up some juicy burgers.

subsenses.def: Used as a familiar term of direct address to such a relative.

example.text: Dap me up, brolative.

notes: This TikTok defines four wombos in sequence (Brolative, Brousin, Delularisma, Sealeloquently). Per the one-entry instruction, Brolative is selected as it leads the title, description, and captions. On-screen etymology is authoritative: "Bro + Relative = Brolative." The single attested usage comes from the SRT ("dap me up Brolative... venture out into WigArtha to pick me up some juicy burgers"), lightly cleaned; "WigArtha" and "juicy burgers" are Sendy's surrounding nonsense, not part of the definition. The vocative sub-sense (1a) and the pronunciation variants are house-style enrichment: Sendy uses the word only as a term of address, from which the broader noun sense is inferred. Two IPA renderings are offered since the second element may be heard as either -lative (cf. relative) or -lā-tive. Synonym "brousin" is the sibling coinage from the same video.

etymology: Blend of bro (informal term of address for a close male companion, clipped from brother) and opp (slang for an opponent, enemy, or rival, clipped from opposition). The fusion captures a single figure who occupies both roles at once — a companion who is simultaneously an adversary. Coined by Professor Sendy and presented on screen as the equation "Bro + Opp = Bropp" (2026).

senses.def: A person who is at once a close companion and a rival or antagonist; a friend regarded with the wariness owed to an enemy. Used especially of a peer whose familiarity does not preclude treachery or competition.

examples.text: That bropp stole my sunglasses.

subsenses.def: As a term of direct address, applied to such a figure in moments of exasperation, half-affectionate and half-confrontational.

example.text: Bropp, stop — lowkey-genuinely give me the goods before I start deadass-passionately having a nonchalant breakdown all over the carpet.

notes: The video defines four distinct wombos (Bropp, Twizzler, Lookstaxxing, Crick); per the one-entry instruction I selected Bropp, which leads both the on-screen title and the spoken captions, carries the only standalone usage example ("That bropp stole my sunglasses"), and recurs across the other segments as a connective term. On-screen etymology box reads "Bro + Opp = Bropp"; spelling taken from the frame. The exasperated address example (sense 1a) is reconstructed from Sendy's heavily wombo-laden caption ("Bropp stop... lowkenuinely give me... before I start deadassionately having a nonchalashout all over the brarpet"), lightly normalized into plain prose since the surrounding words are themselves separate nonce-blends rather than part of the definition; this normalization is house-style enrichment. The IPA assumes the LOT/THOUGHT vowel of "opp"; both a British (ɒ) and American (ɑː) realization are given. Year 2026 from upload_date 20260325.

etymology: Blend of bropp (a bro-inflected familiar prefix, itself from bro, a casual term of male address) and doppelganger (German Doppelgänger, literally "double-goer," denoting an apparitional look-alike or near-double of a living person). The first element supplants the leading syllable of doppelganger, yielding broppleganger and aligning the word with the broader fraternal "bro-" lexicon.

senses.def: A male counterpart who bears an uncanny resemblance to oneself or another; a bro who serves as one's look-alike or near-double.

examples.text: My chopple and my bropplegangers were all in attendance.

subsenses.def: Such a double regarded specifically as an object of gossip or scrutiny; the dude whose business one wishes to know.

example.text: I demand you spill the tea on the Choppleganger's chiquin.

notes: Lead headword of a rapid-fire multi-wombo video; the on-screen black/blue box gives the authoritative spelling and etymology as Bropp + Doppelganger = Broppleganger. Definition is grounded in Sendy's actual usage: he applies it to a male peer/look-alike ("my chopple and bropplegangers") and later to a gossip target ("the tea on the Choppleganger's chiquin," a spelling variant he voices mid-flow). The base sense of "look-alike/double" is carried entirely by the doppelganger root, which Sendy does not redefine, so the gloss preserves that meaning while attaching the bro-coding of the bropp- prefix. Sub-sense (a) is light house-style enrichment drawn from his second usage. IPA is a plausible reconstruction in the dry house register; the variant pronunciation reflects the harder American doppelganger. This same video also defines, in their own boxes, Flarping (flex + larping), Choated (cheugy + goated), and Tealicioso (tea + delicioso), plus numerous spoken throwaway wombos (brousin, brarpet, chiquin, paurming, twousin, etc.) and longer stacks like quiche peanemis and cliche teanemis; each merits a separate entry. Confidence medium: spelling is firm from the frame, but the definition is inferred from brief, dense usage rather than an explicit gloss.

etymology: Blend of bro (clipping of brother, a casual male companion) and the personal name Joseph, the latter contributing only its terminal syllable to lend the address the cadence of a proper name. Coined by Professor Sendy as one of a recited series of bro- compounds (alongside brud, bromance, brizzler, brooksmaxxer, brollege, breadass, brobeque, brat, and brog).

senses.def: a male friend or companion; a 'bro' addressed or referred to with the mock-formality of a personal name.

examples.text: Bro, Joseph, Broseph.

subsenses.def: used as a familiar form of address to a man, equivalent to 'buddy' or 'pal.'

example.text: What's up, Broseph?

subsenses.def: used loosely of any man regarded as embodying easygoing fraternal camaraderie.

example.text: He's a real Broseph once you get to know him.

senses.def: uttered to hail, greet, or call the attention of a male companion.

examples.text: Broseph! Over here.

notes: No spoken definition is given in the video; the captions consist only of the recited formula "Bro / Joseph / Broseph," part of a rapid-fire compilation of ten bro- blends. On-screen text box (authoritative) reads "Bro + Joseph = Broseph" and highlights BROSEPH as the displayed headword, so it is taken as this entry's word. The sense and the verbal/interjection forms below are house-style enrichment consistent with the transparent blend (a "bro" dressed up with the ending of a proper name), not anything Sendy literally states. The auto-caption renders the final list item as "Brig," but the on-screen frame shows "Brog" (Bro + Dog); frame spelling is trusted throughout. Two pronunciations are offered: a spelling-faithful /-sɛf/ and a Joseph-faithful /-zəf/.

etymology: A portmanteau coined by Professor Sendy, fusing eight discrete elements in sequence: BURGER + BATTLE + BITE + CEO + CLONE + ILLUMINATI + THREE TIMES A WEEK + ADVERTISEMENT, with the overlapping boundaries elided so that each root bleeds into the next (Burg- + -att- + -lite- + -on- + -minate- + -etimesawee- + -vertisement). The eight-root construction places it among the longer specimens in the Sendy corpus, where the joke resides in the sheer accretion of unrelated roots into a single unpronounceable mass.

senses.def: A recurring, sponsored fast-food contest in which competitors do battle by the bite, framed as a thrice-weekly promotional spectacle; by extension, any absurdly over-engineered branded eating event in which corporate showmanship and conspiratorial spectacle are layered atop a simple meal.

examples.text: Every Monday, Wednesday, and Friday the chain ran another Burgattliteonminateetimesaweevertisement, pitting two contestants in a televised bite-for-bite duel between sponsor cards.

subsenses.def: The promotional broadcast or clip itself, regarded as an advertisement disguised as competition.

example.text: He skipped the actual game and just watched the Burgattliteonminateetimesaweevertisement for the free coupons.

subsenses.def: humorous. A meal eaten on a fixed three-times-a-week schedule with ritual seriousness, as though it were a corporate showdown.

example.text: His standing lunch order had become a personal Burgattliteonminateetimesaweevertisement.

notes: The on-screen black text box gives the authoritative spelling 'Burgattliteonminateetimesaweevertisement' and the blue etymology box gives the eight roots (Burger + Battle + Bite + CEO + Clone + Illuminati + Three Times a Week + Advertisement). The SRT captions contain ONLY Sendy reading the eight component roots aloud in order ('Burger / battle / bite / CEO / Clone / Illuminati / Three times a week / advertisement') with NO narrated definition. This video is therefore a pure word-plus-etymology reveal; Sendy supplies no explicit spoken meaning. The definition and POS senses below are house-style enrichment synthesized to be consistent with the assembled roots (a periodic/scheduled sponsored fast-food showdown), and should be treated as editorial reconstruction rather than Sendy's literal stated meaning. Confidence set to medium because the headword/etymology are certain from the frame but the sense is inferred. No usage example exists in the source; the example given is reconstructed from the etymology and flagged as such. Merch line in description ('Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura' shirt) is unrelated to this wombo.

etymology: A blend (wombo) of BURGER, the grilled or fried patty served in a bun, and FUNDAMENTALLY, the adverb meaning "in essence; at the most basic level." The first element supplies the appetitive, food-directed sense; the second supplies the adverbial frame of deep or essential conviction. Coined by Professor Sendy (2026) as the opening item in a five-wombo compilation (alongside cooshout, boujeugy, lowkirky, and twuh).

senses.def: At the most basic and essential level, especially as it concerns the consumption of, or one's foundational relationship to, a burger or comparable food product; in one's gastronomic core.

examples.text: I'm burgundamentally gonna try this food product now.

subsenses.def: Used to mark a craving or culinary intention as deep-seated, inevitable, or non-negotiable.

example.text: I am burgundamentally incapable of walking past a drive-through.

subsenses.def: By extension, fundamentally; at root — with a faintly appetitive or indulgent connotation.

example.text: We are, burgundamentally, simple creatures.

senses.def: Basic or essential in a specifically burger-related way; constituting the foundational appetite for fast food. House-style back-formation from the adverb.

examples.text: Hunger this loud is a burgundamental truth.

notes: SOURCE IS A COMPILATION: the f02 frame shows five distinct wombos with their etymology boxes (Burger + Fundamentally = Burgundamentally; Cooked + Crashout = Cooshout; Boujee + Cheugy = Boujeugy; Lowkey + Quirky = Lowkirky; Twin + Bruh = Twuh). This entry covers only the lead/title headword, BURGUNDAMENTALLY, which is also listed first in the description. On-screen spelling is authoritative and unambiguous. Sendy's only spoken usage is "I'm burgundamentally gonna try this food product now," which establishes the adverb sense; the adjective sub-sense and the deadpan culinary-conviction shading are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with the burger + fundamentally fusion, not literal quotations. The IPA is a plausible reconstruction in the dry house register. If a separate entry is wanted for each of the other four wombos, this video is the shared source.

etymology: A wombo (blend word) coined by Professor Sendy, formed from CAPICHE (also capisce, an Anglicized borrowing of Italian "capisci," meaning "do you understand?") + QUIRKY + NICHE. The compression of "capiche" with "quirky" and "niche" yields a single interrogative that demands not merely comprehension but comprehension rendered in an idiosyncratic, narrowly-coded register.

senses.def: Used as a tag question to confirm that the listener has understood and is on board with what has been said, with the added insistence that the matter be grasped in a quirky and niche fashion; an intensified, subculturally-coded variant of "capiche?"

examples.text: I need you to make your boring Falasha less like a testicule and more peanematic and gloujee. Capquiche?
I gotta Loreain something to ya: if you're gonna hang out with me and my boys, you gotta be ready to yap about Palm Beach Pete all day. Capquiche?
Tell me the story about that guy who ate that burger, Capquiche?

subsenses.def: Employed specifically to demand that another person deliver peak cinematic analysis (peanemis) in a manner that is at once quirky and niche.

example.text: Use this wombo when you demand that someone deliver the peanemis in a quirky and niche way.

senses.def: An understanding or shared agreement of a deliberately quirky, niche character; the state of mutual comprehension demanded by the interjection.

notes: On-screen black-box headword reads \"Capquiche\" with blue etymology boxes \"Capiche + Quirky + Niche\"; this spelling is authoritative over the auto-caption \"Capquiche/Capiche.\" Definition is drawn directly from Sendy's spoken caption: he says it is \"Capiche\" plus \"quirky and niche,\" used to demand someone deliver the peanemis (peak cinema analysis) in a quirky and niche way. All three usage examples are his actual captions, lightly cleaned; they incidentally contain several other wombos (Falasha, testicule, peanematic, gloujee, Loreain, Nonchalashout, Womboclat), which are left intact as his real usage. Primary part of speech is the interjection (his demonstrated form, always sentence-final tag question). The noun sense is a tasteful house-style extension consistent with his meaning, not stated verbatim by Sendy, and is flagged here as enrichment. IPA is a plausible rendering in the dry house style, treating the final element as the \"-iche\" of niche/capiche (/-iːʃ/).

etymology: A wombo (word + combo) coined by Professor Sendy, blending CAP(PING) "lying, talking falsely" + FREE + SON (a familiar term of address). The compound is constructed to land as a near-homophone of the juice brand Capri Sun, an intentional double sense exploited by the related play on thirst (see note 2). First attested in a Professor Sendy short, May 2026.

senses.def: An exclamation used to call out a perceived lie, exaggeration, or empty boast, particularly one offered unprompted or without stakes; roughly, "that's a baseless lie, friend."

subsenses.def: Directed at a falsehood told gratuitously or at no cost to the speaker (the "free" element), with mock-paternal address.

example.text: He said he was going to vagynyamaxx, and I was like, "Capree Son."

subsenses.def: Used as a flat rebuff to an attempt at provocation or flirtation believed to be insincere.

example.text: She came up and dropped a triple T, and I was like, "Capree Son" — stop trying to make me thirsty.

senses.def: An instance of unfounded or freely volunteered lying; a worthless falsehood treated as the thing one is being served.

examples.text: That whole story was a Capree Son and nothing more.

notes: Spelling taken from the on-screen black headword box ("Capree Son") and blue etymology box ("Capping + Free + Son"); auto-captions also render it "Capree Son." Definition is paraphrased in house style from Sendy's usage, where he deploys the term twice as a deadpan retort to someone "capping for free" while addressing them as "son." Note 1: The two examples are his actual usage from the captions (responding to "vagynyamaxx" and to a "triple T / Total + Thirst Trap"), lightly cleaned into sentences. Note 2: The coinage is a deliberate homophone of the Capri Sun juice pouch, which Sendy reinforces with the follow-on pun "stop trying to make me thirsty" — both the drink and the slang sense of "thirsty"; this layered wordplay is his, not added. House-style enrichment beyond what Sendy literally said: the noun part of speech and sense 1b's generalization, the IPA, and the listed synonyms are tasteful additions consistent with his meaning.

etymology: Blend (wombo) of cap, internet slang for a lie or falsehood, and absolutely, an adverb of emphatic affirmation. The portmanteau yokes a marker of untruth to a marker of total agreement, so that the very form of the word undercuts the assent it pretends to give; coined by Professor Sendy.

senses.def: Used to voice emphatic agreement that is in fact sarcastic or insincere; an affirmation whose embedded element (cap, 'a lie') signals that the speaker means the opposite of, or has no intention of honoring, what is being agreed to.

examples.text: "You want me to do it?" "Capsolutely. I'll get right to it after I finish Chapter 67 of Tequila Moggingbird."
He asked if I could help him move and I just said, "Capsolutely."

subsenses.def: Delivered with deadpan seriousness and no context, as a means of bewildering an interlocutor; on being challenged, the speaker simply repeats it.

example.text: "Hey, let's hang out this weekend." "Capsolutely." "...What's your problem?" "Capsolutely."

parts.forms: capsolmaxx; capsolmaxxing; in extended nonce use capsolmaxxed

senses.def: To devote oneself wholeheartedly to the practice of sarcastic agreement; to push the ironic-assent bit to its limit. Formed by appending the intensifying suffix -maxx(ing).

examples.text: I'm gonna go capsolmaxxing this weekend.
When I get back I'm gonna have capsoleanelentelogicalaura.

notes: On-screen headword and etymology box read exactly: Capsolutely (Cap + Absolutely). Spoken captions confirm the core sense (sarcastic/ironic agreement) and supply all examples verbatim, lightly cleaned. Sendy explicitly demonstrates an extended verb form by suffixing -maxxing ('capsolmaxxing'), which I have recorded as a separate part of speech (sense 2); the further escalations ('capsoleanelentelogicalaura') are nonce stacking rather than fixed forms and are noted but not lexicalized. The IPA is a plausible house-style reconstruction. The frame also shows two incidental wombos used as decorative captions, 'Huzzitorium (Huzz + Auditorium)' and, earlier, 'Chiquin (Chill + Quintessential)', which are not the subject of this entry.

etymology: A productive blend-family in which an arbitrary first element (the "donor" word) is fused to carpet, the latter contributing its final string -arpet, -arpet being the recurring suffixal residue around which the whole class is organized. From carpet (Middle English, from Old French carpite, from Medieval Latin carpīta "thick cloth," ultimately from Latin carpere "to pluck, card wool") + wombo (Sendy's term of art for a word smashed into another word). The class is named for its fixed second member rather than its variable first member, making carpet the structural anchor of every formation.

senses.def: A portmanteau belonging to the carpet family of wombos, formed by grafting the leading portion of any donor word onto the terminal segment of carpet (typically -arpet), so that the host word is overwritten by a floor-covering at its end. The donor word's meaning is not preserved; the blend exists chiefly to satisfy the joint of the two roots.

subsenses.def: Brarpet. A blend of bro and carpet; nominally, a carpet as addressed or claimed by one's bro, or a fraternal floor-covering.

example.text: Bro plus carpet gives you brarpet.

subsenses.def: Larpet. A blend of larp (to live-action role-play) and carpet; a carpet implicated in or staged for role-play.

example.text: Larp plus carpet gives you larpet.

senses.def: Any of the eight specific formations catalogued under the heading CARPET WOMBOS, each pairing a distinct donor word with carpet.

subsenses.def: Clavarpet, from clavicular + carpet: a carpet pertaining to, or as solid as, the collarbone.

example.text: Clavicular plus carpet gives you clavarpet.

subsenses.def: Clarpet, from classroom + carpet: the communal carpet of a classroom.

example.text: Classroom plus carpet gives you clarpet.

senses.def: The remaining attested members of the class, completing the set of eight.

subsenses.def: Barpet, from Barby's + carpet: a carpet belonging to Barby's.

example.text: Barby's plus carpet gives you barpet.

subsenses.def: Charpet, from charred + carpet: a scorched or burnt carpet.

example.text: Charred plus carpet gives you charpet.

senses.def: Larger-donor variants in which a multisyllabic word absorbs the carpet ending wholesale.

subsenses.def: Restaurarpet, from restaurant + carpet: the carpet of a restaurant.

example.text: Restaurant plus carpet gives you restaurarpet.

subsenses.def: Sigmarpet, from sigma + carpet: a carpet of notably alpha or self-possessed character.

example.text: Sigma plus carpet gives you sigmarpet.

senses.def: To convert a given word into a carpet wombo by fusing its opening onto the -arpet tail of carpet.

examples.text: He carpet-womboed every noun in the room until even the sigma had been turned into a sigmarpet.

notes: Compilation/family video rather than a single defined word. The on-screen title box reads "CARPET WOMBOS" and lists eight blend recipes (Bro+Carpet=Brarpet; Larp+Carpet=Larpet; Clavicular+Carpet=Clavarpet; Classroom+Carpet=Clarpet; Barby's+Carpet=Barpet; Charred+Carpet=Charpet; Restaurant+Carpet=Restaurarpet; Sigma+Carpet=Sigmarpet) — the on-screen spelling is taken as authoritative. The captions contain only the spoken recitation pattern (donor word, "Carpet," resulting wombo); Sendy supplies NO gloss for any individual term. Accordingly, the headword chosen for the entry is the family term "carpet wombo," documented as a single noun with the eight attested members as numbered senses/sub-senses. Because Sendy gives no definitions, each member's gloss is reconstructed transparently from its blend recipe (donor meaning + carpet) in dry house style — this is enrichment, not his literal words. The example sentences are lightly cleaned versions of his actual recited construction ("X plus Carpet gives you Y"). The verb sense and the synonyms ("carpet blend," "arpet-word") are house-style additions consistent with the pattern, flagged here. The shirt name "Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura" and the Speechify partnership in the description are promo, not part of this wombo. IPA reflects two common pronunciations of "carpet" plus the established "wombo." Upload date 20260601 → year 2026.

etymology: A telescoping coinage of Professor Sendy, built by successive accretion of roots, each fused at its overlapping syllables. The base form layers CATNAP (a brief daytime sleep) onto PERCHANCE (archaic: by chance, possibly) and GENUINELY (truly, in earnest), with the medial "-per-" of "perchance" doing double duty as the seam between elements. The extended forms append, in order, EMANATION (an issuing-forth, as of light or essence), MONAD (in Neoplatonic and Leibnizian thought, an indivisible unit of being), and SOPHIA (Greek: wisdom; in Gnostic cosmology, the emanated figure of divine wisdom), so that the word's growth mirrors a chain of philosophical emanation descending from a unit toward wisdom — all of it hung, deadpan, off the premise of a nap one might happen to take.

senses.def: truly, in all earnestness — used to affirm a statement while conceding it is contingent on the speaker happening to take a nap; in earnest, granted that a catnap may chance to intervene.

subsenses.def: (base form Catnaperchenuinely) genuinely, with the wry qualifier that the matter rests on an idle, possibly-to-be-taken nap.

example.text: I will, catnaperchenuinely, finish the report — assuming I do not first lie down.

subsenses.def: (extended form Catnaperchenemanation) the same affirmation conceived as something issuing forth or radiating from the speaker, as an emanation does from its source.

example.text: His sincerity arrived as a slow catnaperchenemanation, drifting out of him like warmth off a sunlit couch.

senses.def: (extended forms Catnaperchenemanonad, Catnaperchenemanophia) in mock-philosophical register: a single, indivisible unit of drowsy sincerity (monad) which, fully unfolded, ascends to a state of napping wisdom (sophia); the highest degree of genuineness attainable only by one who has surrendered to the nap.

examples.text: Reduced to its essence, the whole afternoon was one catnaperchenemanonad — a lone, undivided particle of honest sleepiness.
Only after the third yawn did he reach catnaperchenemanophia, that serene wisdom available solely to the truly, helplessly nap-bound.

notes: No spoken captions were available for this video (no SRT; info.json subtitles empty), so the definition is reconstructed from the on-screen etymology boxes (the only explicit semantic content Sendy provides) rather than from a verbatim spoken gloss; confidence is therefore medium. The frame (f02.jpg) clearly shows the progressive build with blue root boxes: Catnap + Per Chance + Genuinely = Catnaperchenuinely; + Emanation = Catnaperchenemanation; + Monad = Catnaperchenemanonad; + Sophia = Catnaperchenemanophia. On-screen spelling is treated as authoritative. The headword is the base form; the three longer forms are recorded as extended/variant forms and as escalating senses. The example sentences are house-style reconstructions consistent with the roots (no caption examples existed); this enrichment is flagged here. The merch line in the description ("Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura" shirt) is a separate, unrelated wombo and is not treated as part of this entry.

etymology: A wombo (blend) of CELEBRITY + WOMBOS, the latter being Professor Sendy's own term for words smashed into other words. The form deliberately collapses the suffix of "celebrity" into the body of "wombos," so that the name of the category is itself an instance of the category. Compare the seven exhibits coined in the same lecture: Snoop + Fake = Snake; Subpar + Sabrina = Subpina; Luckiest + Jennifer Aniston = Lucifer; Fallen + Ellen = Fellen; Adam Sandler + (an expletive) = Adummsh%^; Shackled + Diddy = Shiddy; Prince Andrew + Served = Perved.

parts.label: plural in form, often construed as singular or collective

senses.def: A genre of wombo in which a celebrity's name is fused with a descriptive word so that the resulting blend names, mocks, or summarizes that celebrity; collectively, the body of such blends.

examples.text: Subpar plus Sabrina equals Subpina.
Luckiest plus Jennifer Aniston equals Lucifer.

subsenses.def: A single such blend regarded as a coinage in its own right.

example.text: Shackled plus Diddy gives us Shiddy.

subsenses.def: By extension, the wider practice or sport of generating celebrity blends on demand.

example.text: Prince Andrew plus served equals Perved.

senses.def: The self-naming category term itself, in which 'celebrity' is wombo'd into 'wombos' to label the very phenomenon it describes.

examples.text: Celebrity plus Wombos equals Celebrombos.

senses.def: To coin a wombo from a celebrity's name; to subject a famous person to the celebrombo treatment.

examples.text: Snoop got celebrombo'd into Snake.

notes: This video is not a single-word definition but a rapid montage establishing a META-CATEGORY. The description states the governing formula verbatim: "Celebrity + Wombos = Celebrombos," so Celebrombos is the true headword of the entry; the seven on-screen blends are Sendy's usage exhibits. The frame's bottom red highlight box reads SUBPINA (the box cycles through each wombo as it is named); the word being defined for the video as a whole is Celebrombos, per the description. House-style enrichment beyond Sendy's literal words: the part-of-speech breakdown, the IPA, the proposed back-formed singular "celebrombo" (sense noted but not separately attested by Sendy), and the dry framing of the seven exhibits as a paradigm are original WOMBOS-style additions consistent with his meaning. Some source names are intentionally censored on-screen (Sh%^); preserved as Sendy presents them. upload_date 20260225 → year 2026. Captions transcribe the components and results; on-screen black boxes are authoritative for spelling (SUBPINA, LUCIFER, etc.).

etymology: Blend (wombo) of charisma (from Greek kharisma "favor, divine gift," via the modern sense of magnetic personal appeal) + sigma (from the internet slang sigma, denoting a self-reliant, lone-wolf high-status male, itself from the Greek letter Σ used to rank such a type outside the alpha–beta hierarchy). Coined by Professor Sendy as part of a rapid-fire wombo compilation (Charigma, Nonchalizz, Charinema, Rizzioed, Delularisma, Serizz, Charismashout, Rizzonance, Susisma, Rizzidue), each presented as a Root + Root = Wombo formula.

senses.def: the particular magnetism of a person whose appeal derives not from warmth or extroversion but from cool self-possession; charisma expressed through the detached, self-sufficient bearing of the so-called sigma type.

examples.text: Charisma plus sigma equals charigma.

subsenses.def: the quiet command an individual holds over a room without visibly seeking attention.

example.text: He never raises his voice, yet the whole table leans in — pure charigma.

subsenses.def: the aura of a lone operator whose indifference to approval only heightens their pull.

example.text: Walking out without a goodbye and somehow looking cooler for it is a feat of charigma.

senses.def: by extension, a self-made or self-contained variety of charm understood as innate rather than performed.

examples.text: You cannot fake charigma; it is either in the silence or it is not.

senses.def: possessing charigma; magnetically compelling in a cool, self-reliant, lone-wolf manner.

examples.text: That charigmatic stare did more work than a whole speech.

notes: This video is not a single defined wombo but a rapid-fire compilation of ten blends, each shown only as a "Root + Root = Wombo" formula (Charisma + Sigma = Charigma; Nonchalant + Rizz = Nonchalizz; Charisma + Cinema = Charinema; Rizz + Ratioed = Rizzioed; Delulu + Charisma = Delularisma; Serene + Rizz = Serizz; Charisma + Crashout = Charismashout; Rizz + Resonance = Rizzonance; Sus + Charisma = Susisma; Rizz + Residue = Rizzidue). Sendy gives NO spoken definition for any of them — the captions only recite the component words. "Charigma" was selected as the headword because it is the first item listed and the lead word of the title. The on-screen frame is authoritative for the spelling and the Charisma + Sigma etymology. Because no literal gloss exists, the senses below are house-style enrichment inferred from the transparent meaning of the blended roots; flagged accordingly. The post also promotes a "Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura" shirt (merch), which is unrelated to this word.

etymology: Blend of CHOPPED (slang, of a person: unattractive, rough-looking, "cooked") + DOPPELGÄNGER (German Doppel "double" + Gänger "goer, walker"), with the medial syllables collapsed so that the second element supplies its own ending. The base form spawns an extended family by suffixal accretion: -gang (a hanging-out collective) yields CHOPPLEGANGGANG, and a further -topple (an act of knocking over) yields CHOPPLEGANGGANGTOPPLE. Coined by Professor Sendy.

parts.label: informal
parts.forms: plural choppelgangers; extended forms choppleganggang, choppleganggangtopple

senses.def: A person's exact double or look-alike who happens to be unattractive, rough-looking, or generally "chopped"; an unflattering doppelgänger.

examples.text: I'm legiterally Frank Gallagher's choppleganger.

subsenses.def: (often disparaging) one whose resemblance to another only underscores how rough either party looks.

example.text: He's not my twin, he's my choppleganger.

senses.def: (in the form choppleganggang) a group of rough-looking or "chopped" men who like to spend a great deal of time hanging out together; a clique of choppelgangers.

examples.text: What was up with that lady in the Target parking lot? She kept yelling "choppleganggang!"

senses.def: Shouted as a rallying cry or exclamation, typically to hail or summon such a group.

examples.text: She kept yelling "Choppleganggang!" across the lot.

senses.def: To knock over or topple a choppleganggang; to bring down a gathered group of choppelgangers.

examples.text: Knock them over — choppleganggangtopple!

notes: On-screen black text box gives the authoritative spelling "Choppleganger," with the etymology blue box reading "Chopped + Doppelgänger." Two extended on-screen forms confirmed in frames: "Choppleganggang" and "Choppleganggangtopple." Sendy's literal meanings, drawn from the captions: a "chopped doppelganger" (sense 1); a "chopped doppelganger group of guys that like to hang out a lot" (= choppleganggang, sense 2); and "knock them over" = choppleganggangtopple (the verb). Examples are his actual caption usages, lightly cleaned (the Target parking-lot bit and the Frank Gallagher line). ENRICHMENT beyond what Sendy literally said: subsense 1a (the disparaging shade) and its example sentence; the interjection part of speech with its example (he shows the woman "yelling" it, so I formalized that as an interjectional use); the plural/inflected forms; and the IPA, which is plausible house-style reconstruction. Synonyms ("evil twin," "lookalike") are my additions for the doppelgänger sense. SRT misspells the audio-only line "lowkenuinely glussin" (his ad-libbed flourish), which is unrelated to the headword and was not used. Year 2026 from upload_date 20260118.

etymology: A wombo coined by Professor Sendy, contracting chug + (di)et Peps(i) + (w)ombo, the final element being Sendy's own term of art for an invented portmanteau. The middle root telescopes the brand name Diet Pepsi into a single syllable, while the terminal -ombo reflexively names the very class of word the act is meant to produce. First demonstrated in a video dedicated "Tribute to brutus_astutus."

senses.def: The act or practice of drinking Diet Pepsi in a single sustained draught and immediately afterward uttering the first wombo (invented portmanteau) that occurs to one, performed as a feat of spontaneous lexical invention.

examples.text: He steadied the bottle, attempted a Chugetpepsombo, and the first thing out of his mouth was "Peanemis."

subsenses.def: By extension, any wombo produced through this method; the blurted word itself rather than the ritual that yields it.

example.text: "Peanemis" was a respectable Chugetpepsombo for a first attempt.

senses.def: To drain a serving of Diet Pepsi and at once pronounce the first wombo that comes to mind.

examples.text: Whenever the well runs dry, I just chugetpepsombo until something sticks.

notes: Headword spelling taken from the on-screen text box ("Chugetpepsombo"). The definition is drawn directly from the spoken captions: "Chugging Diet Pepsi and then saying the first wombo that comes to mind." The frame shows Sendy chugging a bottle of Pepsi; the SRT records him then blurting the wombo "Peanemis," used here as his authentic in-video example of the resulting utterance. The etymology breakdown (chug + diet Pepsi + wombo) is house-style reconstruction inferred from the word's morphology, as the frame displays only the word box and the tribute line rather than the usual (Root + Root) blue boxes; flagged as enrichment. The verb sense and the agent-noun sense ("chugetpepsomboer") are tasteful house-style extensions consistent with Sendy's demonstrated meaning. The video also functions partly as a merch promo (Etsy shirt linked in the description), but its core is a genuine wombo definition.

etymology: Blend (wombo) of chill + fun + legit + bae, the four qualities of the designated companion. The two-token spelling "Chun Lee" puns on the name of the Street Fighter character Chun-Li, with "Chun" yielding chill + fun and "Lee" yielding legit + bae, so that the full form may be clipped to either element. Coined by Professor Sendy.

parts.label: slang
parts.forms: plural Chun Lees; clipped chun (chill + fun); clipped lee (legit + bae)

senses.def: A person with whom one is romantically involved and of whom one is genuinely fond, but in a relationship understood by both parties to be low-stakes and not serious; a casual but well-regarded partner.

examples.text: When I first met my Chun Lee, she made me bromise not to crash out so much.
Instead of going out, me and my Chun Lee stay in and play Sonopoly.

subsenses.def: Used predicatively of the qualities the term encodes: at once chill, fun, legit, and bae.

example.text: So this is chill, fun, legit, and bae.

subsenses.def: As an object of casual courtship; the person one is, in plainer terms, merely hitting.

example.text: So you're hitting Chun Lee?

notes: On-screen headword spelling "Chun Lee" (two words) is authoritative; the auto-caption agrees. Etymology boxes confirm Chill + Fun + Legit + Bae. Sendy himself demonstrates the decomposable spelling later in the clip, captioning "chun (Chill + Fun)" and "lee (Legit + Bae)", which licenses the noted clipped forms. Definition rewritten in house style from his stated meaning: a partner one genuinely likes within a relationship that is "not that serious." The supplied subsenses and the clipped-form usage labels are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with what he literally says and shows; the surrounding wombos in his examples (bromise, brarking lot, etc.) are not glossed here as they are separate entries.

etymology: Coined by Professor Sendy (2026) as the seed-form of an escalating compound. Apparently a blend of CHUNG- (cf. chungus, the rotund-internet-mascot morpheme) and a vowel-rich tail -eugy of indeterminate origin, here functioning as a productive root onto which further morphemes are stacked. The on-screen progression demonstrates the word's capacity to ramify: Chungeugy → Chungeugenuinely (with -genuinely, "in earnest") → Chungeugentologiclowstate (incorporating -ontologic-, "pertaining to being," and -lowstate, the depressed or diminished condition) → and finally the maximal Chungeugentologiclowstababybixsevenuineleengreenchicaura, which further absorbs -baby-, -bix-seven- (cf. the bixseven numeral-thread), -uineleen-, -green-, and -chicaura (an aura morpheme). The form thus belongs to Sendy's class of agglutinative wombos, in which a base is extended without limit by suffixed roots.

senses.def: a seed-word designating an indeterminate chungus-adjacent quality or state, used chiefly as the base of an ever-lengthening compound; the irreducible kernel from which a wombo is grown.

subsenses.def: the quality itself, in its plain, unextended form.

example.text: It started as a simple Chungeugy and would not stop growing from there.

subsenses.def: by extension, any of the progressively suffixed enlargements of the base, each adding a further root.

example.text: By the fourth line he had reached Chungeugentologiclowstababybixsevenuineleengreenchicaura.

examples.text: Chungeugy, Chungeugenuinely, Chungeugentologiclowstate.

senses.def: the act or principle of unbounded morphological escalation; the property of a word that accretes roots without natural stopping point.

examples.text: He demonstrated pure Chungeugy by stacking root upon root until the box could barely hold the line.

senses.def: in a Chungeugy manner; earnestly and in full chungus-adjacent sincerity (the first escalation of the base, fusing Chungeugy with genuinely).

examples.text: He meant it Chungeugenuinely, with no trace of irony.

notes: No spoken captions (SRT) exist for this video and the TikTok subtitles field is empty, so the precise spoken definition is unavailable; the entry is reconstructed from the on-screen escalation chain (frame f02.jpg, clear) and the post description, both of which give only the word forms, not a gloss. The headword spelling and the four escalating derivations are taken verbatim from the on-screen blue/black text boxes and are authoritative. Confidence is LOW because Sendy's literal meaning is not recorded; the senses below are house-style reconstructions consistent with the visible morphology and his standard "agglutinative escalation" format, NOT direct quotation of a definition. The morpheme glosses (chungus, bixseven, aura, etc.) are tasteful enrichment inferred from recurring Sendy roots and are flagged as such. The LOWKIRK merch line in the description is an unrelated store promo, not part of the word.

etymology: Blend (wombo) of CLAVICular ("of or relating to the collarbone," from Latin clavicula, "little key") + VICTORIA'S SECRET (the lingerie brand, evoked here as a byword for a closely guarded, glamorously displayed thing), the surname being further punned on the senses "victory" and "secret." Coined by Professor Sendy, who decomposes it on screen as Clavicular + Paid Actor + CIA + Top Secret — a mock-forensic chain in which the "-victoria-" and "-secret" elements double as cloak-and-dagger references (CIA, Top Secret) and the spectacle of the brand.

senses.def: A pair of collarbones so sharply defined and conspicuously displayed as to suggest something secretly prized or jealously guarded; clavicles of a striking, enviable prominence.

examples.text: I bet that ASU frat leader who framed-mogged the other guy has some real Clavictoriasecrets.

subsenses.def: By extension, the closely held particulars of how such a physique is achieved or maintained; an aesthetic open secret.

example.text: Everyone wonders about his bone structure, but he won't reveal his Clavictoriasecrets.

senses.def: An object of glamorous display whose chief allure lies in being withheld or only partially shown; a tantalizingly concealed asset.

subsenses.def: Humorously, a matter treated with mock-classified seriousness, as though of intelligence-agency sensitivity.

example.text: Don't ask about the routine; that's strictly Clavictoriasecret.

senses.def: Of collarbones or, by extension, of any prized feature: so striking as to seem confidential or under guard.

examples.text: He walked in with that Clavictoriasecret collarbone definition.

notes: On-screen headword spelling is authoritative: "Clavictoriasecret" (singular), with the on-screen and caption usage example appearing in the plural "Clavictoriasecrets." The frame displays four distinct wombos (Clavictoriasecret, Aurealis, Geaked, Tluff); per the one-entry instruction I extracted the lead/featured term, Clavictoriasecret, which heads the video and the post description and appears on the on-screen tag. Sendy's literal gloss is light — he uses it in the looksmaxxing/"framed mogged" context to mean strikingly sharp, secret-keeper-grade collarbones; sense 1 paraphrases that directly. Sense 2 (the secrets themselves) and the back-formed verb are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with his plural usage "has some real Clavictoriasecrets," and are flagged here as such. IPA is a plausible reconstruction in the dry house register, not attested. The etymology faithfully reproduces his on-screen root chain (Clavicular + Paid Actor + CIA + Top Secret) while noting the underlying Victoria's Secret pun.

etymology: Blend (wombo) of clock it, to take notice of or observe (someone or something), and locked in, fully and intensely focused. The compound fuses the act of observing with the state observed, so that to be clocked in is at once to be visibly, conspicuously absorbed and to be caught in that absorption by an onlooker. Coined by Professor Sendy (2026).

parts.label: informal
parts.forms: also as verb phrase clock in

senses.def: of a person: visibly and intensely focused on a task, in a manner conspicuous enough that an onlooker takes notice of the concentration.

subsenses.def: describing a state of absorption that is observed by another; locked in to a degree that gets clocked.

example.text: She had the spreadsheet open across three monitors and hadn't blinked in a minute; she was completely clocked in.

subsenses.def: (of a group) having just snapped into earnest, head-down work; settled in and on task, with play on the sense of clocking in to a shift.

example.text: That caused 67 rendezboos to quickly get to work, and then they clocked in.

examples.text: Use this wombo when somebody is really focused and you notice it.

senses.def: a partner or counterpart with whom one is reliably and equally focused; a person one habitually locks in alongside.

examples.text: Me and my clocked twin are always so focused that we keep a documented folder for our peak cinema analysis.

notes: On-screen headword and etymology boxes read \"Clocked In\" / \"Clock It + Locked In\" (frame f02), authoritative over the auto-captions. The core sense (use it when somebody is really focused and you notice it) is taken directly from Sendy's spoken definition. Sense 1's illustrative example preserves his actual usage line, which is itself a chain of nested wombos (Huzzitorium, quiche, Loreained, peanemis, Venemis, quiché, gloujee, Bangpoundulary, 67 rendezboos) leading into \"...to quickly get to work. And then they clocked in.\" Part of speech 2 (the derived noun \"clocked twin,\" Clocked In + twin) is Sendy's own on-camera extension, with his \"documented folder for our peak cinema analysis / Docderpeanemis\" example. House-style enrichment beyond what Sendy literally said: the adjective vs. verb-phrase split, the IPA, the labeled subsenses (1a observed-focus shade, 1b reflexive get-to-work shade), and the synonym list. The pun on workplace \"clocking in\" (starting a shift) is implied by his \"get to work / clocked in\" framing and is reflected in subsense 1b.

etymology: Blend of COVID (the colloquial name for the coronavirus pandemic of the early 2020s) and literally, the intensive adverb. The fusion seizes on the shared phonetic seam in which the final syllable of COVID glides into the body of literally, so that the speaker's emphatic "literally" is absorbed wholesale into the era under discussion. Coined by Professor Sendy as the flagship member of a productive family of -erally blends, in which any short attributive word (legit, mid, and so on) may be welded to literally to mark the register or vintage of the intensification.

senses.def: Used to intensify a statement in the manner of "literally," while simultaneously anchoring it to the era of the coronavirus pandemic; in actual fact, truly — as recollected from or pertaining to the pandemic years.

subsenses.def: As a general emphatic, equivalent to "literally" but spoken with pandemic-era inflection.

example.text: I coviderally remember buying a two-litre of milk from the McDonald's drive-through during the pandemic.

subsenses.def: Specifically marking a recollection as genuinely belonging to the lockdown period, vouching that the detail is no exaggeration however improbable it now sounds.

example.text: We coviderally were sanitising the groceries on the porch before bringing them inside.

parts.label: informal; intensive; closely related form (legit + literally)

senses.def: Used to intensify a statement, asserting that it is both legitimate and literal; genuinely, no exaggeration — with the colloquial assurance carried by "legit."

examples.text: He legiterally thinks that the earth isn't round — he thinks it's a triangle.

parts.label: informal; intensive; closely related form (mid + literally)

senses.def: Used to intensify a statement while implying that the matter intensified is, on balance, mediocre or unremarkable; literally, yet with a shrug of the dismissive "mid."

examples.text: She miderally just gave me the biggest backhanded compliment of my life.

notes: Headword spelling "Coviderally" taken authoritatively from the on-screen black title box and confirmed by the post description.
