etymology: A blend (portmanteau) of LARP (live action role-play; broadly, to pretend or perform an identity one does not genuinely hold) and ARTEMIS, the Greek goddess of the hunt and the moon. Coined by Professor Sendy as the flagship member of an extended family of "larp-" wombos, each formed by fusing larp with a second term (carpet, yap, artistry, glow up, locked in, living rent free) to denote a specific mode or flavor of larping.

senses.def: A larp (a feigned or performed identity) carried to a soaring, mythic, or grandiose extreme; pretending so ambitiously that the performance reaches lunar heights. From larp + Artemis.

examples.text: Larptemis larped to the moon.

senses.def: The notional perch or platform from which an especially committed larper performs, invoked chiefly in the imperative to tell someone to stop pretending so strenuously. From larp + carpet.

examples.text: Get off your larpet.

senses.def: To talk on and on while simultaneously larping; to ramble in a way that is both excessive chatter and put-on performance. From yap + larp.

examples.text: Stop yarping so much.

senses.def: The conspicuous display of effort or craft by someone pretending to a skill they have not actually mastered; trying-hard artistry that is more performance than competence. From larp + artistry.

examples.text: They're showing a lot of larpistry.

senses.def: To feign a glow-up; to pretend one has improved or transformed for the better without any genuine change. From larp + glow up.

examples.text: They're glarping up.

senses.def: Pretending to be focused or fully engaged; performing the appearance of being locked in without actually concentrating. From larp + locked in.

examples.text: You're larped in.

senses.def: Occupying another person's thoughts purely through one's larping; a boast that one's pretended persona persists rent-free in someone else's head. From larp + living rent free.

examples.text: I'm living larp free in your head.

notes: This video is a multi-wombo episode rather than a single definition: Professor Sendy stacks seven blends, all built on the root "larp" (to pretend/perform). The on-screen black/blue equation boxes give the authoritative spellings: Larp + Artemis = Larptemis; Larp + Carpet = Larpet; Yap + Larp = Yarp; Larp + Artistry = Larpistry; Larp + Glow Up = Glarp Up; Larp + Locked In = Larped In; Larp + Living Rent Free = Living Larp Free. Because the schema holds one headword, I used the lead/title-first form "Larptemis" as the headword and recorded each of the other six as distinct parts/senses, since they form one cohesive "larp-" family. All definitions are paraphrased into house style; all examples are his actual caption usages, lightly cleaned. Parts of speech (noun/verb/adjective/phrase) and the secondary forms column are tasteful house-style enrichment inferred from the way he uses each term (e.g., "larped in" used predicatively as an adjective, "yarping"/"glarping up" as verbs); Sendy did not explicitly label POS. Year 2026 from upload_date 20260523.

etymology: Blend of the proper noun LeBron (after the basketball player LeBron James) and the adverb genuinely, the celebrity's name displacing the first syllable so that the word both names the subject and supplies the sense "in earnest, truly." Coined by Professor Sendy as the lead member of a productive "[celebrity] + genuinely" template (compare SlimShenuinely, DiCaprenuinely, SubParpenuinely), in which any well-known name may be fused with -nuinely to mark sincere or earnest action by that figure.

parts.label: often facetious
parts.forms: also LeBronuinely; cf. patterned forms SlimShenuinely, DiCaprenuinely, SubParpenuinely

senses.def: In a genuine or earnest manner; truly, sincerely. Used to affirm that the named celebrity (here LeBron James) does or feels something in earnest, the subject's identity being folded into the word itself.

examples.text: He LeBronuinely loves to dribble that ball right into that pizza party.

subsenses.def: Of the productive template generally: serving as a sincerity intensifier in which the relevant public figure's name supplies both subject and emphasis, asserting that the action is no act.

example.text: She was DiCaprenuinely in love with him for like fifteen years.

subsenses.def: Used with mild irony to mark a sincere assessment of a celebrity, frequently a deflating or unflattering one.

example.text: She's SubParpenuinely the biggest industry plant there is.

notes: Source frame f02.jpg is sharp and authoritative for spelling and etymology; it presents FOUR wombos built on one template ("[celebrity] + Genuinely"), so this is a multi-word "wombo family" video rather than a single-word definition. Headword chosen as LeBronuinely because it leads both the on-screen stack (top box) and the post title; the sibling forms SlimShenuinely (Slim Shady), DiCaprenuinely (Leonardo DiCaprio), and SubParpenuinely (Sabrina Carpenter, whose blend also puns on "subpar") are documented in etymology and as patterned subsense examples. Examples are Sendy's actual caption usage, lightly cleaned (spelling normalized to the on-screen forms; SRT auto-captions rendered them as "LeBronuinely/Slimshenuinely/dicaprenuinely/subparpenuinely"). Sendy never literally states a definition; the gloss "genuinely / in earnest" is inferred from the transparent root "genuinely" plus how he deploys each form, which is consistent and unambiguous, hence high confidence. House-style enrichment beyond his literal words: the part-of-speech label "adverb," the usage labels "often facetious" / "mild irony," subsenses (a) and (b), the IPA, and the framing of the construction as a productive template. The post description also plugs unrelated "LOWKIRK" merch (Etsy STLBrendonStore); ignored as promo. Upload date 20260216 yields year 2026.

etymology: A wombo (word combo) blending legitimate + accurate + speculate, coined by Professor Sendy. The compound fuses the credibility of "legitimate," the precision of "accurate," and the forward-looking guesswork of "speculate," yielding a single verb for prediction that proves uncannily correct. The noun legaculation is formed on the model of speculate → speculation.

senses.def: to speculate or predict in a manner that proves shockingly accurate; to make a forecast that turns out to be clairvoyant or borne out by events.

examples.text: My quiche peak-cinema analysis legaculated that the six-or-seven o'clockalypse was lowkey genuinely twinevitable and twinescapable.
I'm gonna legaculate that my twins are gonna go larping for rizzdom, but when they get to the huzzitorium they're gonna get all sigma-introverted and talkward.

subsenses.def: to predict a future event correctly and well in advance, especially through analysis later vindicated.

example.text: Her review legaculated the rise of fruit Love Island months before anyone else saw it coming.

subsenses.def: (broadly) to assert a speculative claim that subsequently turns out to be both legitimate and accurate.

example.text: I legaculated that the sequel would flop, and somehow I was right.

senses.def: an instance of speculation that proves remarkably accurate; a prediction or piece of analysis vindicated by later events.

examples.text: That quirky-niche banger-compound vocabulary is a legaculation which shockingly predicted the rise of fruit Love Island and its eventual chill-hilarious, nonchalant crashout.

notes: On-screen text box confirms the spelling "Legaculate" and etymology "Legitimate + Accurate + Speculate"; auto-captions render it identically. Sendy uses both the verb (past tense "legaculated"; future "I'm gonna legaculate") and the noun "legaculation," so two parts of speech are given. Examples are his actual caption usages, lightly cleaned and stripped of the nested wombos he stacks in for comic effect (Peanemis, quiche, Twuhs, etc.); the bracketed glosses preserved where they aid sense. House-style enrichment: the IPA, the sub-sense (1b) generalizing from prediction to any vindicated claim, and the listed synonyms are editorial additions consistent with his stated meaning of a forecast that turns out shockingly clairvoyant. The post is also a merch promo (a "Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura" shirt), but the video itself is a genuine wombo definition.

etymology: Blend (portmanteau) of literally and figuratively, fusing the two contraposed adverbs into a single hedging term. The intended sense exploits the long-standing colloquial slippage by which literally is used to mean its opposite, figuratively; liguratively splits the difference, denoting a statement meant only partway in earnest. Coined by Professor Sendy (2026).

senses.def: to a partial or intermediate degree; in a manner one means only halfway, neither wholly literally nor purely figuratively.

examples.text: I liguratively think that he's gonna glow-taper-fade on this twearhouse.
I liguratively think that a vivolution is starting.

subsenses.def: used as a softening qualifier to mark a claim as serious-but-not-entirely, hedging the speaker's commitment to it.

example.text: I liguratively think a revolution is starting — I'm pretty sure they did everything genuinely.

subsenses.def: (loosely) used as an intensifier of indeterminate force, conveying that something is true to some real but unspecified extent.

example.text: Grab the work huzz — it's liguratively about to be the six-or-seven-o'clock-alypse.

senses.def: of or relating to a statement meant only halfway; neither strictly literal nor merely figurative.

examples.text: That was a ligurative threat at best — he sort of meant it.

notes: On-screen black box gives the authoritative spelling "Liguratively"; the blue etymology box reads "Literally + Figuratively". Auto-captions render it variously as "liguratively" and the heard examples nest other wombos (glow-taper-fade, twearhouse, vivolution) which are not defined here. The core sense (a wombo for when you "halfway mean something literally") is taken directly from Sendy's captions. Enrichment beyond what he literally said: subsense 1b (intensifier reading, supported by his "six-or-seven-o'clock-alypse" example), the derived adjective "ligurative" with its illustrative sentence, and the second IPA variant; all are house-style extensions consistent with the blend. Example sentences are his actual caption usages lightly cleaned (e.g., "vivoluton" normalized to "vivolution", added the genuine/revolution framing he speaks aloud). Description promotes an unrelated merch shirt ("Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura"), not the subject of this entry.

etymology: A blend of LOGIC (reasoning, sound inference) and LOW (a diminished or depressed level), coined by Professor Sendy. The word fuses the two roots to name a downturn or trough in one's reasoning faculties. On-screen the term is presented in a series alongside Deadamente, Undereppy, Lemoniminal, and Flow.

senses.def: A temporary dip or trough in one's capacity for clear reasoning; a stretch during which logical thinking runs at a diminished level.

examples.text: Logiclow.

notes: No caption file (.srt) exists for this video, so Sendy's spoken definition could not be verified; confidence is therefore low. The two read frames (f02, f04) show NOT a single-word etymology card but a vertical LIST of five wombos in blue boxes: Logiclow, Deadamente, Undereppy, Lemoniminal, and Flow. There is no black headword box or blue (Root + Root) etymology breakdown for any individual word, so the standard single-entry layout is absent. I selected "Logiclow" as the entry because it heads both the on-screen list and the post description. The sense ("a dip in reasoning") and the LOGIC + LOW etymology are HOUSE-STYLE INFERENCES from the surface morphology, not transcribed from Sendy — they should be confirmed against the audio before publishing. The description also references a long compound merch word, "Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura," on an Etsy shirt, which embeds "logiclow" and confirms the spelling. The on-screen spelling "Logiclow" is treated as authoritative.

etymology: Blend of lore (the accumulated backstory, mythology, and continuity of a fictional or fandom world) + explain. Coined by Professor Sendy. The base verb "loreain" yields the past participle "loreained" (lore + explained), which appears on screen as the focal form; the agentive/output noun sense ("a loreain") follows naturally from the verb. The video frames it as the third element of a wombo chain, "The Quichest Peanemis Ever Loreained," alongside the companion blends quichest (quirky + nichest) and peanemis (peak + cinema + analysis).

senses.def: to explain the lore of something; to lay out the accumulated backstory, mythology, or continuity of a fictional world, franchise, or fandom so that it can be understood.

examples.text: You don't just loreain something all the time; sometimes you just loreain it.
All the peanemises have been loreained, but I want to know which one is the quichest.

subsenses.def: to deliver such an explanation casually or in passing, as a quick, offhand clarification rather than an exhaustive account.

example.text: Sometimes you just loreain it.

subsenses.def: to subject a piece of media to a thorough explanatory breakdown, especially of its hidden or deep-cut continuity.

example.text: Every peanemis worth its salt eventually gets loreained.

senses.def: an instance or finished product of explaining lore; an explanatory account of a world's backstory or continuity.

examples.text: That was the quichest loreain I've ever seen.

notes: On-screen etymology box reads LORE + EXPLAIN; the focal on-screen word is the past-participle form LOREAINED. Sendy speaks the base form ("Loreain is Lore + Explain") and demonstrates several inflections in the captions: bare infinitive ("you don't just Loreain something"), transitive present ("sometimes you just Loreain it"), and past participle ("Peanemises have been loreained"). House-style enrichment: the noun sense (a finished act/piece of lore-explanation) is extrapolated from his verb usage and flagged here as original. The video is a chained-wombo bit; two sibling blends appear in the title — quichest (quirky + nichest) and peanemis (peak + cinema + analysis) — but only loreain/loreained receives an explicit etymology box and a spoken definition, so it is treated as the headword. The "quiche contest / most quiche" closing is wordplay riffing on quichest, not part of loreain's meaning. Upload date 20260503 → year 2026.

etymology: Blend of *low cortisol* (the medical phrase for reduced levels of the stress hormone cortisol, popularized in 2020s wellness discourse) and the masculine given name *Kirk*, the latter substituting for the *-cortisol* element to yield *Kirkisol*. The compound is conventionally preceded by *low*, mirroring the source phrase. Coined by Professor Sendy.

senses.def: Characterized by an extreme absence of stress or tension; supremely relaxed, untroubled, and at ease.

examples.text: I'm living the low Kirkisol life, with my days by the pool spent eating looksmaxxed hors d'oeuvres full of claviar.

subsenses.def: Of a person or lifestyle: marked by serenity and freedom from anxiety.

example.text: When you're living the Kirkisol lifestyle, you attract a lot of admirers.

subsenses.def: Of a setting, situation, or experience: producing no stress whatsoever; thoroughly calming.

example.text: Afternoons by the pool are pure low Kirkisol.

senses.def: A state of profound calm and freedom from stress; the condition or lifestyle of being entirely unbothered.

examples.text: He committed to the Kirkisol lifestyle and never worried again.

notes: On-screen headword is "Low Kirkisol" with etymology shown as "Low Cortisol + Kirk"; this spelling is authoritative over the auto-captions ("Low Kirkisol" / "Low Kirkisol life"). Core meaning ("extremely stress free") is taken directly from Sendy's captions and rewritten in house style. Sendy demonstrates both attributive adjectival use ("low Kirkisol life," "low Kirkisol lifestyle") and a bare-noun-style use ("Kirkisol lifestyle"), so both an adjective and a noun part of speech are given; the noun sense and subsense (b) are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with his usage, as he literally only voices the adjectival "life/lifestyle" forms. The single primary example sentence is built from his actual caption ("I'm living the low Kirkisol life. My days by the pool are spent with...") with one of his surrounding wombos (claviar, looksmaxxed) retained for flavor; the others are themselves separate Sendy coinages, not part of this entry's meaning. IPA reflects the dry house style and both US/UK realizations of the "-sol" of cortisol. Upload date 20260513 fixes the year at 2026.

etymology: A blend (wombo) of "low-key" + "carbon," after the pattern of the element carbon. Coined within Professor Sendy's "Lowkirk" cosmology, in which the low-key (chiefly clipped to "low") is treated as a fundamental substance of the universe and given its own quasi-chemical apparatus. The on-screen element symbol is rendered "Lk." Its constituent particles are themselves wombos: the lowtron (low-key + proton/electron suffix -tron), the keytron (key, from low-key, + -tron), and the orbiting kirktron (Kirk, of Lowkirk lore, + -tron).

senses.def: A fictitious chemical element regarded as the carbon-analogue of the low-key, the basic building substance of all that is understatedly cool; the element from which low-key matter is composed.

examples.text: We've got a lowkarbon atom here.
This element is highly unusual, because there are sixty-seven kirktrons in its third orbital.

subsenses.def: A single atom of this element, having in its nucleus one positive lowtron and one negative keytron, and surrounded by orbiting shells of kirktrons.

example.text: In the nucleus we have a positive lowtron and a negative keytron.

subsenses.def: By extension, any quantity of the substance so constituted; lowkarbon considered in bulk.

example.text: Most lowkarbon you encounter in the wild is faintly unstable.

senses.def: An atom of this element considered with respect to its remarkable instability, most of its isotopes being short-lived and, in Sendy's account, prone to decay into a purely ontological state rather than a physical one.

examples.text: This particular element has a lot of isotopes, but most are unstable, and more often than not, they're ontological.

parts.label: chiefly attributive; the subatomic particles of lowkarbon

senses.def: lowtron: the positively charged particle of the lowkarbon nucleus; the low-key analogue of the proton.

examples.text: In the nucleus we have a positive lowtron.

senses.def: keytron: the negatively charged particle paired with the lowtron in the nucleus; the residual 'key' of the low-key, bound at the atom's core.

examples.text: In the nucleus we have a negative keytron.

senses.def: kirktron: the particle occupying the orbitals of a lowkarbon atom; the low-key analogue of the electron, described as existing around the nucleus as a swirling ball of aura. A normal atom carries two kirktrons in the first orbital and eight in the second.

examples.text: Surrounding this nucleus is the first orbital of kirktrons.
These kirktrons exist around the nucleus as a swirling ball of aura.

notes: Headword spelling taken authoritatively from the frames: the black title box and the whiteboard both read "Lowkarbon," with element symbol "Lk" shown in the box. Frame on-screen labels confirm the three particle terms (Lowtron, Keytron, Kirktron) and the nucleus diagram (a positive lowtron "L+" and a key-marked "K" keytron at the core, surrounded by starred kirktron shells). The SRT spellings (lowtron/keytron/kirktron) agree with the frames. Sendy's literal claims preserved: positive lowtron + negative keytron in nucleus; kirktrons in orbitals as a "swirling ball of aura"; 2 in the first orbital, 8 in the second, an anomalous 67 in the third; many isotopes, mostly unstable and "ontological." Enrichment beyond his exact words, in house style: the etymology's parsing of each blend (low-key + carbon; the -tron particle suffixes; the Kirk/Lowkirk derivation) is my reconstruction from his Lowkirk lore and the on-screen symbol, not stated verbatim; sense 1b (bulk-substance reading) and its illustrative example are original house-style extensions; the IPA is plausible in the dry register. The "Lowkirk" cosmology and the low-key-as-fundamental-substance framing are inferred from the description's merch ("Lowkirk shirt") and the wombo pattern; flagged here as interpretation.

etymology: Blend of low-key (understated, subtle, not drawing attention) + aura (in the slang sense, a person's intangible charisma, presence, or vibe). Coined by Professor Sendy. The extended adverbial form lowkauratologically further incorporates -(on)tologically, from ontology (the philosophical study of being), so as to denote charisma operating at the level of one's fundamental existence. The compound noun lowkauratologicalowup nests a third element, glow-up (a marked improvement in one's appearance or condition), within the established stem.

senses.def: an understated or quietly radiated personal charisma; presence or magnetism that asserts itself without overt display, perceptible chiefly to the attentive observer.

examples.text: Bro in that oversized pink blazer has some mad lowkaura.

subsenses.def: the quality of being subtly impressive; a vibe that is felt rather than announced.

example.text: She never says much in meetings, but the lowkaura is undeniable.

senses.def: in a manner pertaining to understated charisma; so as to radiate subtle presence at the level of one's very being, esp. through calm, deliberate, or contemplative conduct.

examples.text: We did that short meditation lowkauratologically.

senses.def: a glow-up of the quiet variety; an improvement in one's appearance or aura that is significant yet understated, registering as elevated presence rather than overt transformation.

examples.text: After Coachella last year, I had a real lowkauratologicalowup.

notes: On-screen spelling is authoritative: headword box reads "Lowkaura" with etymology box "Low Key + Aura"; lower captions confirm the extended forms "Lowkauratologically" and "Lowkauratologicalowup". The auto-caption SRT renders the stem variously as "lowkaura"/"Lowkauratological(owup)" and misreads "-tologically" as the spoken cue "Ontologically" / "Glow up" that Sendy uses to build each compound; I trusted the frames. Sendy's literal content: the base noun (understated aura, demonstrated with the pink-blazer example), the adverb built on an ontology pun (the meditation example), and the triple-stacked noun incorporating "glow up" (the Coachella example). Enrichment beyond what Sendy literally said: subsense 1a of the noun and its illustrative sentence; the synonyms; the gloss tying "-tologically" to ontology in the etymology; and the IPA, all in house style and consistent with his meaning. Definitions are original paraphrase, not his wording. Example years = upload year 2026 (upload_date 20260110).

etymology: Blend (wombo) of LOWKEY ("quietly, subtly, in an understated way") and OKAY ("acceptable; all right"). Coined by Professor Sendy. The construction fuses the modifier of restrained intensity with the marker of casual assent, yielding a single term for approval delivered with deliberate nonchalance.

senses.def: used to register mild, understated approval; an acknowledgment that something is quietly acceptable or fine without rising to enthusiasm.

examples.text: That's lowkay - I support Ohiolence, as long as I get the testiculedrop first.

subsenses.def: expressing that a situation, despite appearances, sits within the bounds of the agreeable.

example.text: Someone nonchalashedout on your brarpet and you deadassionately punched him - that's lowkay.

subsenses.def: expressing grudging or conditional tolerance of something one does not wholeheartedly endorse.

example.text: I support Ohiolence as long as it's lowkay.

senses.def: subtly acceptable; quietly fine or permissible. Often used predicatively to ask or assert whether an action passes muster.

examples.text: Is it lowkay to leave your glazed bronuts at home?

senses.def: in a manner that is quietly or unremarkably acceptable; passably, in an understated way. (house-style extension of the blend)

examples.text: It all went lowkay, so nobody made a fuss.

notes: On-screen headword and etymology boxes read clearly: 'Lowkay' / 'Lowkey + Okay'. The auto-captions render it variously as 'Lowkay' and 'lowkay'; the black-box spelling is authoritative. The captions' two example sentences are dense with OTHER wombos (nonchalashedout, clavacmaxxed, brarpet, deadassionately, testicules, Ohiolence, testiculedrop, glazed bronuts, clock in, gluteous maximus) used as scenery to frame 'Lowkay' as the operative term ('That's lowkay'; 'is it lowkay [to] leave your... at home'); I extracted his actual usage of LOWKAY itself and lightly cleaned the surrounding nonsense into readable sentences. Sense 1 (interjection/adjective) is what he literally demonstrates: a verdict that something is quietly/subtly acceptable. Sub-sense 1b (something tolerated rather than enthusiastically endorsed, per 'I support Ohiolence as long as...') and the adverb part of speech are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with his blend of 'lowkey' + 'okay'; flagged here as such. IPA given in the dry house style. Year 2026 from upload_date 20260510.

etymology: Blend of low-key (adv., "in a restrained or understated manner; quietly, subtly") and genuinely (adv., "truly, sincerely, in earnest"), the shared medial syllable of low-key and the gen- of genuinely fused into a single portmanteau adverb. Modeled on the broader "low-key X" family of intensifiers in which low-key functions as a hedging or softening qualifier paired with a sincere adverb. Compare the nonce-adverb lowkirkentologically (low-key + a mock-scholastic -ontologically formation, associated with the LOWKIRK merch line) used by Sendy in the same address.

parts.label: informal
parts.forms: comparative more lowkenuinely; related adjective lowkenuine

senses.def: In a quiet or understated yet sincere manner; truly but without making a show of it. Used to mark a statement as honest while downplaying its intensity.

examples.text: What is lowkenuinely happening here?

subsenses.def: Used as a softening intensifier to register mild, deadpan disbelief or bewilderment at an absurd situation.

example.text: He stares at the smart speaker and asks what is lowkenuinely happening here.

subsenses.def: As a sentence adverb, prefacing an admission or confession one is half-reluctant to make.

example.text: Lowkenuinely, I do not understand how we got to this point.

notes: NOT a formal wombo definition video. All six frames are a skit-format bit (Sendy asking Alexa how many days until Halloween, then Christmas, with Alexa absurdly claiming there are no Christmas holidays in 2026) overlaid with the caption banner "What is lowkenuinely happening here?". There is NO black headword box and NO blue (Root + Root) etymology cards in any frame, and Sendy never verbally defines the word — so I set confidence to medium and treated the entry as inferred from context plus his established "low-key + X" usage pattern, rather than from a stated definition. Headword spelling is authoritative: "lowkenuinely" appears on-screen in all frames AND in the post title/description, so I trust it over the auto-caption SRT, which mis-transcribes the spoken throwaway adverb as "lowkirkentologically" (a separate nonce-word tied to his LOWKIRK Etsy merch). ENRICHMENT (mine, not stated by Sendy): the full etymology and IPA; both sub-senses (1a deadpan-disbelief intensifier, 1b sentence-adverb confession use); sense 1b's example sentence; the related adjective form "lowkenuine"; the comparative form note; and the synonym list. Sense 1a's example derives directly from his actual on-screen caption. The two IPA variants reflect the ambiguity of whether the blend collapses the /dʒ/ of genuinely or not.

etymology: A blend (wombo) of lowkey (slang, "in a subdued, understated, or quietly emphatic manner") and okey-dokey (informal alteration of "okay," expressing easy assent), the two fused at their shared internal -key. The phonetic overlap of the final and initial syllables (-key / okey) yields a single seamless compound. In escalated use the form is run through a Flanderized -ily/-y suffix pattern, producing Lowkilydokily, after the singsong cadence of cheerfully diminutive, neighborly speech.

senses.def: Used to express casual, unbothered agreement or acceptance; a relaxed affirmative that downplays one's enthusiasm while still consenting.

examples.text: Lowkey and okey dokey come together as lowkeydokey.

subsenses.def: Signaling that a matter is settled without fuss, and that one is quietly fine with the outcome.

example.text: He shrugged, said lowkeydokey, and let it go.

subsenses.def: In escalated, singsong delivery (Lowkilydokily), an exaggeratedly cheerful and neighborly assent, often delivered with mock earnestness.

example.text: And then it goes all the way to lowkilydokily.

notes: Headword spelling taken from the authoritative on-screen black box: "Lowkey + Okey Dokey = Lowkeydokey," with the escalated form "Lowkilydokily" shown in the lower caption box and confirmed in the post description ("Lowkey + Okey Dokey = Lowkeydokey = Lowkilydokily"). The auto-caption SRT misheard the final form as "Loki Doki," which is ignored per the frame-is-authoritative rule. Sendy's spoken content is brief (just the build-up "lowkey -> okey dokey -> [escalated form]"); he gives no explicit definition, so the senses are house-style original inference from the meanings of the two roots. ENRICHMENT added beyond what Sendy literally said: the gloss as an "unbothered/casual agreement" interjection, sub-senses 1a and 1b, the synonyms, the IPA for both forms, and the etymological note on the shared -key overlap and the Flanderized -ily/-y suffix pattern behind Lowkilydokily (the -ily cadence is the recognizable "okely-dokely" neighborly speech pattern). Confidence high on spelling and roots; the precise definitional shading is inferred.

etymology: Coined by Professor Sendy as the final link in a four-step chain of compounded slang. The chain proceeds: TWIN (close companion, twin-flame) + BRUH (bro, dude) yields TWUH; HOT + AWKWARD + TWIN + BRUH yields HAWKTWUH; BRUH + UNC (uncle, an elder peer) yields BRUNC; and finally LOWKEY (quietly, on the down-low) + BRUNC yields LOWKRUNC. The headword thus telescopes three earlier wombos (twuh, brunc) and four base roots into a single term, blending "lowkey" with "brunc." Compare the in-video diagnosis "wombosis," Sendy's mock-clinical name for the condition of compulsively producing such combinations.

senses.def: an eccentric, advanced-in-years man who keeps a low profile while quietly embodying the qualities of a brunc (a bro-uncle); a discreet or unassuming elder oddball who blends in until his peculiarity is noticed.

examples.text: You know that really old guy you saw in the grocery store, aisle 67, with the wizard hat on? That guy straight up lowkrunc.

subsenses.def: by extension, anyone who is covertly or understatedly a brunc, revealing the trait only on closer inspection.

senses.def: to conduct oneself as a lowkrunc; to be quietly, unmistakably the eccentric-elder type.

examples.text: That guy straight up lowkrunc.

notes: Multi-word build-up video: the on-screen blue etymology boxes chain four wombos that each feed the next (Twin+Bruh=Twuh; Hot+Awkward+Twin+Bruh=Hawktwuh; Bruh+Unc=Brunc; Lowkey+Brunc=Lowkrunc). LOWKRUNC is the culminating headword; the three precursor wombos (twuh, hawktwuh, brunc) are entered here as related parts of speech / synonyms since each is demonstrated with its own usage line. The merch description sells a "LOWKIRK" shirt, but the authoritative on-screen spelling of the defined word is LOWKRUNC; LOWKIRK appears to be a separate/alternate product spelling and is not used in the captions. Definitions are paraphrased into house style from Sendy's spoken examples. House-style enrichment: the verb sense and the noun sense distinction for lowkrunc, plus IPA, are reasoned from his usage rather than stated verbatim; the term "wombosis" is his and is noted in the etymology. Examples are his actual caption lines, lightly cleaned. Confidence high: frame is sharp and all four etymologies plus the spoken usage are legible.

etymology: Blend of matcha (the powdered green tea, here in the variant spelling "macha") + sigma (internet slang for a self-possessed, independent, alpha-adjacent male). The compound fuses the wellness-coded aesthetics of matcha culture with the lone-wolf posturing of the "sigma male," denoting the man who performs masculine self-sufficiency through conspicuous green-tea consumption.

senses.def: A man who fuses the lone, self-reliant posture of the so-called sigma male with a conspicuous devotion to matcha; one whose performance of stoic, independent masculinity is expressed chiefly through the ritual consumption of green tea.

subsenses.def: Used as a term of address or commendation for such a person.

example.text: Hey, man, you're a real machigma.

subsenses.def: More loosely, any practitioner of the wellness-coded sigma lifestyle, regardless of literal tea intake.

examples.text: Matcha sigma.

senses.def: To pursue the machigma ideal to its utmost; to maximize one's standing as a matcha-drinking sigma through dedicated, often ostentatious, effort.

examples.text: Matcha sigma maxing.
That bro's just machigmaxxing.

senses.def: A premises or establishment dedicated to the practice of machigmaxxing; an emporium where matcha sigmas gather to maximize the lifestyle, conceived as part tea house, part communal arena of sigma self-improvement.

examples.text: Matcha sigma maxing emporium.
Hey, man, let's go down to the machigmaxsorium later on.

notes: On-screen black box gives the headword as "Machigma" with etymology boxes "Macha + Sigma" (note Sendy's variant spelling "Macha" rather than "Matcha"). The two lower frame boxes give the authoritative spellings of the derived forms: "Machigmaxxing" (double x) and "Machigmaxsorium" (the noun-of-place). Auto-captions render these phonetically/loosely as "Machigmaxing" and "Machigmaxorium" and spell out the components ("Matcha Sigma," "Matcha Sigma Maxing Emporium"); per the frames-are-authoritative rule I used the on-screen spellings for the headwords and Sendy's spoken phrasings (lightly cleaned) for the examples. All examples are his actual usages from the captions. ENRICHMENT (original, house-style, not literally stated by Sendy): the matcha/sigma gloss in the etymology; sense 1b of the noun (looser application); the bare verb lemma "machigmaxx" and a regular preterite "machigmaxxed"; the plural forms; the elaboration that the machigmaxsorium is conceived as part tea house, part communal arena; and the IPA. Sendy only demonstrated the noun, the -xxing verb form, and the -xsorium place-noun; he did not define them in prose, so the definitions are inferred from the comedic context and his examples.

etymology: A wombo (word + combo) blending the first-person pronoun me with the intensifier AF (colloquial abbreviation of "as f---," denoting an extreme degree) and the adverb lowkey ("in an understated or quietly emphatic manner"), the latter pair repeated to yield the compacted fusion me + AF + lowkey + AF. The doubled structure is phonologically smoothed so that the terminal "-key AF" collapses into the closing "-loaf," producing a form that punningly evokes the dish meatloaf. Coined by Professor Sendy (2026).

parts.label: slang
parts.forms: comparative more meafloaf; variants loafmeaf, meloaf, lomeaf, loafaf, meafaf

senses.def: Of a thing, experience, or preference: profoundly and personally relatable while remaining conspicuously understated; capturing one's own essence in a quiet, unshowy way.

subsenses.def: Resonant with the speaker's identity to a near-defining degree; "so me" that it feels like an extension of the self.

example.text: Arby's Meat Mountain is very meafloaf.

subsenses.def: Quietly intense; emphatically lowkey, such that its appeal is felt rather than announced.

example.text: When something's actually meafloaf, you'll know it in your twintuition.

examples.text: So this is me AF, and lowkey AF.

senses.def: An opinion or preference held to be quintessentially one's own and quietly held; chiefly in the phrase meaf goonion ("good opinion"), denoting a personal take one is prepared to defend.

examples.text: That's my meaf goonion, and I'll stand by it.

notes: On-screen headword spelling "Meafloaf" and etymology boxes "Me + AF + Lowkey + AF" are authoritative (from frame f02). Sendy's stated meaning: a thing one strongly and personally identifies with while it remains conspicuously lowkey/understated. His real usage example is Arby's "Meat Mountain" sandwich being "very meafloaf." He also coins, in the same video, the related noun "meaf" (Me + AF) and several jokey synonym variants (loafmeaf, meloaf, lomeaf, loafaf, meafaf), which I record as a noun part of speech and as synonyms respectively. The two IPA renderings reflect his oscillation between a "mee-" and "may-" onset; this is house-style enrichment as he does not pronounce it deliberately. Senses 1 (adjective) and the noun "meaf" are grounded in his captions; sub-sense 1b (intensified relatability) is a light house-style shading consistent with the doubled-intensifier etymology. Adverbial/intensive uses (e.g. "loafmaxxed") drift into his other wombos and are noted but not given full treatment here.

etymology: Blend of melancholy (a pensive, low-spirited sadness; ultimately from Greek melankholia 'black bile') and nonchalant (coolly unconcerned; from French nonchalant, present participle of nonchaloir 'to lack warmth or care'). The on-screen etymology gives the formula Melancholy + Nonchalant. In Professor Sendy's serial demonstration the term is further compounded by successive suffixation — + idiot savant, + confidant, + debutante, + restaurant — yielding the extended forms melanchalantidiotsavant, melanchalantidiotsavantfidant, melanchalantidiotsavantfidantbutante, and melanchalantidiotsavantfidantbutantaurant.

parts.label: informal
parts.forms: comparative more melanchalant; superlative most melanchalant

senses.def: of a person or mood: quietly sad yet utterly unbothered; carrying a low, pensive gloom with such studied indifference that the sorrow registers as composure rather than distress.

examples.text: He stared out the rain-streaked window with a melanchalant shrug, mournful but entirely unbothered.

subsenses.def: wistful or wearily downcast in affect.

example.text: A melanchalant sigh escaped her, soft and resigned.

subsenses.def: cool, casual, and conspicuously unconcerned about that downcast feeling.

example.text: He delivered the bad news in a melanchalant tone, neither alarmed nor cheered.

senses.def: a person characterized by a habitual blend of low-grade melancholy and breezy indifference; one who is sad about things but cannot quite be moved to mind.

examples.text: Every coffee shop has its resident melanchalant nursing a cold espresso.

notes: No spoken captions exist for this video (no SRT file), so no verbal gloss was available; the definition is reconstructed in house style strictly from the on-screen etymology boxes (Melancholy + Nonchalant). The frame f02.jpg was clear and authoritative for spelling. The senses, the related adjective/noun split, and the serial-compound forms (melanchalantidiotsavant through melanchalantidiotsavantfidantbutantaurant) are read directly from the on-screen black/blue boxes; the precise shades of meaning for each extension are not verbally defined by Sendy and are presented here as the documented chain rather than discrete glosses. The example sentences are tasteful house-style enrichment built around the literal root meaning, since Sendy supplied no spoken usage example — flagged accordingly. Confidence is medium because the headword and etymology are certain but the intended sense is inferred from roots rather than a spoken definition.

etymology: A wombo blending melancholy (deep, settled sadness) + nonchalant (studiedly unbothered or indifferent) + crashout (an emotional or behavioral breakdown). The three roots are telescoped so that the sadness (melon-) and the false ease (-chala-) collapse directly into the breakdown (-shout), enacting the very sequence the word names. Coined by Professor Sendy.

senses.def: An emotional collapse that occurs when a person who is in fact deeply sad insists on appearing carefree or unbothered, until the suppressed feeling breaks through into a visible breakdown.

examples.text: Use this wombo when somebody's really sad but they try to act carefree and crash out because of it.

parts.label: slang
parts.forms: melonchalashouts; past and past participle melonchalashedout; present participle melonchalashoutting

senses.def: To break down emotionally after struggling to mask genuine sorrow behind a pose of indifference; to crash out specifically because the effort to seem unbothered has failed.

examples.text: My cousin lost her job at the carpet factory, and she melonchalashedout after delivering a chill, peeved announcement to the manager, who promptly terminated her in shock.

senses.def: In the -maxxing construction, to lean fully and deliberately into the melonchalashout state; to maximize one's quietly-sad-then-crashing-out behavior as a chosen mood or activity.

examples.text: Let's go Melonchalashoutmaxxing this weekend.

notes: On-screen headword spelling "Melonchalashout" and the etymology boxes (Melancholy + Nonchalant + Crashout) are taken directly from frame f02; captions misspell the word and so the frame is treated as authoritative. The core definition follows Sendy's literal gloss (sad, acts carefree, then crashes out). Enrichment beyond his literal words, flagged here: (1) the part-of-speech split into noun, verb, and intensified -maxxing verb is inferred from the forms he actually demonstrates in the video (the base "Melonchalashout," the past-tense "Melonchalashedout," and "Melonchalashoutmaxxing"); (2) the verb's stated inflected forms and the IPA are house-style reconstructions; (3) the listed synonyms are editorial. The first usage example is rebuilt from his cousin/carpet-factory anecdote, in which he stacks several other wombos (twousin, brarpet, chilleanemis, sherminated) — I have rendered those into plain English ("cousin," "carpet factory," "chill, peeved," "terminated... in shock") so the citation reads as a clean sentence illustrating Melonchalashout itself. The merch/shirt mentions in the description are promotional and not part of the definition.

etymology: Coined by Professor Sendy (2026) as a clipping of Mesopotamian, after a viewer's remark that his manner of speaking word-combos resembled an attempt to summon an ancient Mesopotamian curse. Adopted by Sendy as the proper name for the art form of word-combination itself.

parts.label: mass noun; the proper name of an art form

senses.def: The art form, named in 2026, consisting of the deliberate fusing of words into compound coinages; the practice and resulting body of word-combinations regarded as a creative and expressive medium in its own right rather than as ordinary speech.

examples.text: The new art form for humanity is called Meso; word combos are Meso.

subsenses.def: Used as a mass noun denoting the activity itself, chiefly in the construction 'do some Meso'.

example.text: Let's go to the conference and do some Meso.

subsenses.def: The medium or register in which such coinages are uttered, esp. in the phrase 'speak in Meso'.

example.text: You all can come to the conference, and we can speak in Meso together and create new words.

senses.def: Expressly distinguished by its originator from rapping; though delivered with cadence, it is held to be a separate discipline whose aim is the invention, communal proposal, and ratification of new words.

examples.text: So this is not rapping. It's 2026.

senses.def: Of an utterance or coinage: belonging to or exemplifying this art form; constituting a word-combo.

examples.text: Word combos are Meso.

notes: On-screen headword is unambiguously "MESO" across all frames (black title box reading "New Artform / MESO", subtitle box "Word Combos are Meso"); the auto-caption mangling around timestamp 3 ("lowkirkentologic...") is ignored per the frame-authoritative rule. This is a meta-entry: rather than the usual (Root + Root) etymology boxes, Sendy names the entire wombo art form itself. No blue etymology boxes appear; the etymology (clipping of Mesopotamian) is given verbatim in the captions via the viewer-comment anecdote. Taken directly from Sendy: that Meso is the new art form / "word combos are Meso," the Mesopotamian-curse origin, that it is "not rapping," the mass-noun usage "do some Meso," "speak in Meso together," and the conference where people create and vote on new words. His explicit rejection of the verb form is recorded ("It's not mesoing. It's called doing some Meso"). ENRICHMENT (original, house-style, consistent with his meaning): the predicative adjective sense 1 is extrapolated from his line "Word combos are Meso"; the formal mass-noun and predicative-adjective labels, the IPA, and the synonyms list ("wombos") are added editorially. Two IPA variants are offered because the spelling supports both a long-vowel reading and a Mesopotamian-derived short-vowel reading.

etymology: Coined by Professor Sendy as the diminutive companion to his term "peanemis" (peak cinema analysis), itself a wombo of PEAK + CINEMA + ANALYSIS. The on-screen etymology renders the full chain as WORD + COMBO + MICROSCOPIC + PEAK + CINEMA + ANALYSES: the leading "wombo" stock (WORD + COMBO) is fused to a MICRO- prefix (from MICROSCOPIC) and the plural ANALYSES, yielding a "microscopic peak-cinema analysis" — a batch of very small, rapid-fire wombo definitions delivered in a single sitting.

senses.def: A compact, rapid-fire batch of miniature wombo definitions; a 'microscopic peak-cinema analysis' in which a string of very short coinages is glossed in quick succession rather than examined one at a time.

examples.text: Today's wombo micropeanemises: a quick run of tiny words, each defined in a single breath.

subsenses.def: quiche loraine: the act of relating someone else's backstory to a third party, or more loosely the act of simply telling somebody something or throwing an object.

example.text: He pulled a quiche loraine and told everyone my whole backstory.

subsenses.def: micropeanemis: a singular, bite-sized instance of a genuinely cool story.

example.text: That was a real quiche peanemis, a really cool story.

senses.def: Loosely, any of the individual micro-wombos catalogued within such a batch; a single small coinage presented as part of the rapid-fire set.

subsenses.def: brarpet: a carpet one stands on together with a 'bro,' the term 'bro' extending to a girlfriend or wife.

example.text: We were just standing on the brarpet together.

subsenses.def: wombaclat: an epithet hurled at someone in anger, or exclaimed aloud when one is deeply upset.

example.text: You absolute wombaclat!

subsenses.def: nonchalashout: the condition of appearing outwardly calm while inwardly melting down.

example.text: She gave a nonchalashout, serene on the surface and falling apart underneath.

subsenses.def: bropp: a false friend; a backstabber.

example.text: Turns out he was a bropp the whole time.

parts.label: internet
parts.forms: third-person singular micropeanemises; present participle micropeanemising; past micropeanemised

senses.def: To deliver a batch of miniature wombo definitions in rapid succession; to gloss a string of tiny coinages back to back.

examples.text: He micropeanemised through nine words before the clip even hit thirty seconds.

notes: On-screen title is the authoritative spelling: "Wombo Micropeanemises" with etymology boxes reading "Word + Combo + Microscopic + Peak + Cinema + Analyses". The video is a rapid-fire compilation rather than a single definition, so the headword chosen is the umbrella term "Micropeanemises" — the bite-sized batch format itself — which Sendy frames at the top of the clip. Auto-captions also spell the singular as both "peanemis" and "peanemis"/"micropeanemis"; the screen plural governs. The numbered senses below capture the umbrella concept (sense 1) and, as illustrative subsenses, the individual micro-wombos Sendy defines in the run: quiche loraine, brarpet, wombaclat, nonchalashout, bropp, analvision, testicule, mysterpeanemis, and clavacuum. Per house style these are paraphrased from his spoken captions, not copied. Light enrichment: the gloss of "micropeanemis" as a singular bite-sized cool-story unit is extrapolated from his line "a quiche peanemis is a really cool story" and the micro- framing; flagged here. Description confirms the running gag and a Speechify partnership/merch plug (non-definitional).

etymology: A seven-fold blend coined by Professor Sendy from mysterious + enigmatic + misunderstood + sus + peak + cinema + analysis, the final three elements being themselves contracted into the recurring formant -peanemis (peak + cinema + analysis). The opening cluster Mysteren- compresses mysterious and enigmatic, -miss- carries misunderstood, and -sus- preserves sus intact before the terminal -peanemis. Compare Sendy's companion fragment peanemis, used independently of the full word, and its variant susqueanemis (sus + queanemis).

senses.def: An account, anecdote, or piece of media that is at once genuinely impressive and so far-fetched as to strain credulity; a story prized for its appeal yet quietly suspected of being untrue.

examples.text: Use this wombo when someone tells you a really cool story that's also highly unbelievable.
Could you not drop the Mysterenmissuspeanemis? I want a regular peanemis with quiche.

subsenses.def: In reduced form, the terminal element alone, denoting a story or analysis that is merely peak, cinematic, and worth dissecting, without the connotation of doubt.

example.text: I want a regular peanemis, not the whole Mysterenmissuspeanemis.

senses.def: To devote oneself, intensively and at length, to producing or consuming such cool-but-unbelievable stories; to lean fully into the mysterious-yet-implausible mode.

examples.text: I'm gonna go Mysterenmissuspeanemismaxxing this weekend.

notes: On-screen headword spelling is authoritative: Mysterenmissuspeanemis, glossed in the blue etymology box as Mysterious + Enigmatic + Misunderstood + Sus + Peak + Cinema + Analysis. Sendy's stated meaning: a tag for a story that is both really cool and highly unbelievable. The verbal/gerund form Mysterenmissuspeanemismaxxing is his own demonstrated usage (suffixed with the intensifier -maxxing). The detachable element peanemis (peak + cinema + analysis) and the further blend Mysterenmissusbrarpeanemis (inserting brarpet) are also his, shown as he stacks wombos; recorded here as house-style enrichment of the secondary noun sense and synonym. IPA is a plausible rendering in the dry house style, not transcribed from a pronunciation Sendy gives. The post description's garbled spelling 'Mysterenmissuspeanemis' is consistent with the frame.

etymology: A wombo (portmanteau) blending MYSTERIOUS + PEAK + CINEMA + ANALYSIS, as displayed on screen. The four roots fuse into a single noun naming a piece of media so impressive ("peak cinema") that its excellence is inseparable from the questions it raises ("mysterious analysis"). First attested in the lexicographic videos of Professor Sendy, 2026.

senses.def: A work of media or a moment regarded as both genuinely excellent and bewildering; something really cool that happens yet leaves the audience with a great many unanswered questions.

subsenses.def: The acclaimed-yet-baffling work itself, especially a film, scene, or piece of content.

example.text: Could you drop the Mysterpeanemis?

subsenses.def: The act or delivery of presenting such a work; the demanded reveal of something at once peak and unexplained.

example.text: I demand the delivery of the Mysterpeanemis.

examples.text: Use this wombo when describing something really cool that happened that also left you with a lot of questions.
Could you drop the Mysterpeanemis melonchalashout all over the clavarpet?

notes: On-screen spelling 'Mysterpeanemis' is authoritative (frame f02). Etymology boxes read 'Mysterious + Peak + Cinema + Analysis'. Definition derives directly from Sendy's spoken gloss: describing something really cool that happened that also left you with a lot of questions. All three usage examples (the polite request, the emphatic version, and the demand) are his actual caption text, lightly cleaned. House-style enrichment beyond Sendy's literal words: the breakdown into noun sub-senses (the work itself vs. the act of delivering it), the speech-act usage labels (in requests / emphatically / as a demand), the part-of-speech treatment, and the IPA are original. Sendy explicitly disclaims a negative back-formation, 'Mysteranalvisions' (mysterious + analysis + revisions); noted but not elevated to a separate entry. The emphatic example also nests two further wombos he coins in passing ('melonchalashout' = melancholy/nonchalant + crashout; 'clavarpet' = clavicular + carpet), preserved verbatim in the example.

etymology: Blend (wombo) of nervous + types, the latter clipped and fused so that the second element supplies the plural-looking ending. The resulting form deliberately echoes the disease name "herpes," lending the coinage its undertone of an undesirable condition one can "catch" or "get." Coined by Professor Sendy.

senses.def: A state of acute nervousness and uncertainty, conceived of as a condition one can contract or be afflicted with; jittery, unsure energy.

subsenses.def: the quality itself, especially as something undesirable that may rub off on a person from association with anxious company

example.text: When it comes to friends, I don't wanna get nerpes.

senses.def: Used collectively or with reference to people: nervous, unsure types; individuals who give off such anxious energy that others keep their distance.

examples.text: We went to the party with some nerpes and no one would come near us.

senses.def: To embrace or lean fully into a state of nervous uncertainty; to maximize one's nerpes as a deliberate mode of being.

examples.text: Let's go nerpesmaxxing this tweekend.

notes: On-screen text box confirms spelling "Nerpes" and etymology "Nervous + Types"; captions confirm the meaning ("really nervous and unsure"). Sendy treats the word both as a quality/condition one can "get" (example 1, leaning on the herpes pun) and as a collective term for the nervous, unsure people themselves (example 2). House-style enrichment beyond what he literally states: the verb form nerpesmaxxing is his (caption: "let's go nerpesmaxxing this tweekend"), but I have framed it as a formal derived part of speech and supplied the -maxx gloss; the two numbered noun senses (condition vs. people) and the IPA are editorial structuring of his single demonstrated meaning. Confidence high on spelling, etymology, and core sense.

etymology: Blend (wombo) of niche + literally, coined by Professor Sendy. The headword is formed by overlapping the second syllable of "niche" with the opening of "literally," fusing the sense of obscure specificity ("niche") with the intensifying truth-claim of "literally." On-screen etymology presented as the two roots "Niche + Literally."

senses.def: Used to assert that one means something both literally and in a narrowly specific, obscure, or insider sense; truly, but with respect to a niche; in a way that is at once literal and esoteric.

examples.text: I nichiterally think that brarpet is the most deadassionate wombo ever loreained.

subsenses.def: As an intensifier of a verb of opinion or belief, signalling earnest conviction about a niche matter.

example.text: I nichiterally think it's peak.

subsenses.def: As a sentence adverb prefacing a statement of fact that is literally true yet too specialized for a general audience to appreciate.

example.text: Nichiterally, this is the most quiche take you'll hear all week.

senses.def: The quality of being literally true while remaining of niche or specialist interest; earnest specificity.

examples.text: The nichiterality of his analysis was unmatched.

notes: On-screen headword spelling "Nichiterally" with etymology box "Niche + Literally" is authoritative; auto-captions match. Sendy's stated meaning: use the word when you literally mean something niche. The adverb (sense 1) and its sentence-modifier shade (sub-sense 1b) are directly demonstrated in his usage ("I Nichiterally think that brarpet is the most deadassionate wombo ever loreained"). The noun part of speech (sense 2, the quality/register of being nichiterally) is a tasteful house-style extension consistent with his usage but not explicitly named by Sendy. The example sentences are drawn from his actual captions, lightly cleaned; the second is part of a deliberately maximalist wombo-stacked sentence and is reproduced in condensed form. IPA is a plausible house-style reconstruction.

etymology: A blend (wombo) of Nickelodeon (the children's-television network) + background check (a vetting of a person's history), coined by Professor Sendy. The portmanteau folds the two together to yield a form homophonous with the rock band Nickelback, the surface joke being that the made-up word sounds like the band while meaning something entirely unrelated. Compare the band name Nickelback, popularly held to derive from the nickel ("five cents") returned in change. First recorded in Sendy's "New Words: Millennial Edition," 2026.

senses.def: A vetting of an adult's background undertaken specifically because that adult is to be entrusted with children; a child-safety screening of the kind a children's-entertainment employer might require.

examples.text: Before he could volunteer at the kids' network, the studio ran a full Nickleback on him.

subsenses.def: By extension, any precautionary investigation of a person presented in a deceptively wholesome or family-friendly context.

example.text: The smiling cartoon host turned out to need a Nickleback of his own.

senses.def: Humorously, the band one's friend insists is the best band ever; used in the deadpan misnaming of the rock group Nickelback.

examples.text: Hey, man, let's go to the Nickleback concert; they're the best band ever.

notes: This is a COMPILATION video ('New Words: Millennial Edition') covering four wombos in 37 seconds. The single authoritative on-screen black headword box, shown across all six frames, is 'Nickleback' with etymology 'Nickelodeon + Background Check =' — so I made that the headword entry per the rule that the on-screen black box is authoritative. SPELLING NOTE: the on-screen wombo is spelled 'Nickleback' (no second 'e'); the auto-caption SRT renders it 'Nickelback' (the real band's spelling) in the usage line. I trusted the frame for the headword spelling and used the SRT line as the example. The other three words in the compilation, listed in the green on-screen contents box and walked through in the SRT (none of which got their own black etymology box on screen), were: (1) AQUEMINI = Aquarius + Gemini, his example 'Did you hear Aquemini? It's so good — but it's not as good as Silk, the Shocker's album'; (2) ADULTING = Adele + insulting, his example 'I'll stop adding people on my MySpace and start adulting'; (3) PARAMORE = paranormal + Mount Rushmore, his example 'Let's take the rotor-copter out to Paramore.' These could each be split into their own entries if desired. ENRICHMENT I ADDED beyond what Sendy literally said: sense 1 and sub-sense 1a (the child-safety-screening reading) are my house-style inference from the Nickelodeon + Background Check etymology — Sendy himself only voices the band-name gag (sense 2), so I derived the literal blended meaning and a tasteful extension. The plural form and synonyms are also my additions. IPA reflects the standard pronunciation of the homophonous band name. Confidence is medium because Sendy never spells out a prose definition for this word; only the etymology blend and the band joke are explicit.

etymology: Blend of the slang assertion no cap ("no lie; in all honesty") and apathetically ("in a manner showing indifference"), the two welded at the shared element -ap-. Coined by Professor Sendy as the base term of a progressive lexical stack, each successive coinage grafting a further root onto the tail of the last: + eloquent yields Nocapathetiloquent; + kirkenuinely (itself a Sendy formation on genuinely) yields Nocapathetiloquirkenuinely; and + quantum mechanics yields Nocapathetiloquirkentumechantics.

parts.forms: Nocapathetically; stacked forms Nocapathetiloquent, Nocapathetiloquirkenuinely, Nocapathetiloquirkentumechantics

senses.def: in a manner that is at once wholly sincere and conspicuously indifferent; truthfully yet without any show of caring.

subsenses.def: of an utterance: delivered as honest fact while affecting total nonchalance.

example.text: No cap, apathetically, I could not care less, and that is the truth.

subsenses.def: by extension, used as an intensifier of candour, asserting that what follows is plainly true and offered with a shrug.

example.text: Nocapathetically, the meeting changed nothing.

senses.def: (in extended stacked forms) honestly indifferent and, successively, eloquently so, genuinely so, and to a degree approaching the quantum-mechanical; used to escalate the same sentiment to absurd length.

examples.text: Nocapathetiloquent: indifferent, but expressed with eloquence.
Nocapathetiloquirkenuinely, I mean every uncaring word of it.

notes: On-screen text in f02 is authoritative for all four spellings: Nocapathetically (No Cap + Apathetically), Nocapathetiloquent (+ Eloquent), Nocapathetiloquirkenuinely (+ Kirkenuinely), Nocapathetiloquirkentumechantics (+ Quantum Mechanics). The SRT captions only voice the component roots ("No cap," "apathetically," "Eloquent," "Kirkenuinely," "Quantum mechanics") and give no spoken prose definition, so the sense is inferred from the roots Sendy fuses rather than from an explicit gloss — hence confidence medium. The core meaning (sincere + indifferent) follows directly from no cap + apathetically. Enrichment beyond Sendy's literal words: numbered senses, sub-senses 1a/1b, the IPA, and the example sentences are house-style reconstructions built on his stated roots; the year (2026) is from upload_date 20260315. The description repeats the four stacked forms and promotes an Etsy merch shirt, consistent with the on-screen build. "Kirkenuinely" is itself a prior Sendy coinage (on genuinely) reused here as a root.

etymology: Blend (portmanteau) of nonchalant + crashout. The headword fuses the studied indifference of nonchalant with crashout (an emotional breakdown or loss of composure), naming the contradiction between a placid exterior and an interior breakdown. Coined by Professor Sendy (2026).

senses.def: A state in which one maintains an outwardly composed, indifferent demeanor while privately experiencing an emotional collapse; the act or condition of appearing nonchalant on the surface while crashing out within.

examples.text: When you look nonchalant on the outside but you're crashing out on the inside, that's a nonchalashout.

subsenses.def: The calm exterior itself, considered as a deliberate mask over inner distress.

example.text: She kept a perfect nonchalashout through the whole meeting.

subsenses.def: A person who habitually presents such a facade.

example.text: He's a real nonchalashout once you know what to look for.

senses.def: To present a calm, unbothered exterior while internally crashing out; to mask an emotional breakdown beneath an air of indifference.

examples.text: I was nonchalashouting the entire time but nobody could tell.

senses.def: Outwardly unbothered while inwardly in turmoil; characteristic of a composed surface concealing an inner collapse.

examples.text: That was a very nonchalashout reaction to getting fired.

notes: No spoken caption file (SRT) exists for this video; the entry is derived entirely from the on-screen text, which clearly displays the headword, the etymology blocks (Nonchalant + Crashout), the segment label "On a Brarpet," the "Demonstration" tag, and a burned-in defining caption. Spelling taken from the black headword box and is authoritative. Sendy's literal on-screen gloss is "When you look nonchalant on the outside but you're crashing out on the inside"; the numbered senses and the derived adjectival and verbal forms (nonchalashouting) are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with that meaning. The merch tie-in ("Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura" shirt) and the "On a Brarpet / Demonstration" framing are recurring channel furniture, not part of the word's meaning.

etymology: Blend (wombo) of nonchalant (from French nonchalant, present participle of nonchaloir "to lack warmth, be unconcerned," ultimately from Latin non "not" + calēre "to be warm") and defenestrate (back-formation from defenestration, from Latin de- "out of" + fenestra "window," after the 1618 Defenestration of Prague). Coined by Professor Sendy.

senses.def: to eject or be ejected through a window (or comparable doorway or threshold) while one party maintains a studied, unbothered air, so that an act of violent expulsion is framed as a matter of complete indifference.

subsenses.def: of the one expelled: to be thrown out and to play the ordeal off as cool, affecting unconcern despite probable injury.

example.text: Twuh, I just got nonchalenestrated; I think I'm twinjured, but honestly I'm cooler than a sixty-seven-degree evening.

subsenses.def: of the one doing the throwing: to pitch a person out of a window casually, with no change in demeanor.

example.text: The bouncer just nonchalenestrated him without so much as blinking.

senses.def: an instance of nonchalenestrating; an episode in which someone is tossed out of a building yet conspicuously tries to act unfazed, typically with a poor result.

examples.text: Did you see the nonchalenestration? He hopped and then flopped all over the sidewalk after getting tossed out of that cafe, and still tried to play it off all cool.

notes: On-screen spelling is authoritative: Nonchalenestrate, etymology shown as (Nonchalant + Defenestrate). Sendy demonstrates a transitive verb (chiefly in passive: \"I just got Nonchalenestrated\"), a past-participial adjective, and a related noun nonchalenestration; these forms are extrapolated from his own usage, not separately defined by him. The verb senses (the thrower's nonchalance vs. the victim's) are house-style sub-senses teasing apart the single comic image he gives. His example sentences are studded with unrelated wombos (twinjured, Brousin, twevening, deadassionately, Nonchalashout, Brarking lot, cafestaurant), preserved here only as quoted context. Year from upload_date 20260331.

etymology: Blend of NONCHALANT (unconcerned, studiedly casual; from French nonchalant, present participle of nonchaloir "to lack warmth, be indifferent") + CONTEST (a competition or trial of skill; from Latin contestari "to call to witness, bring an action"). Coined by Professor Sendy as the designation for a deliberately low-key, follower-milestone competition. The medial -ant of nonchalant is elided so that the word resolves cleanly into -contest.

senses.def: A competition staged with conspicuous casualness, especially one held to mark a social-media milestone, in which entrants submit material and a host selects a winner without ceremony or evident exertion.

examples.text: In celebration of nearly reaching 100,000 followers, the 100,000 Followers Nonchalontest invites viewers to send in their best wombos.

subsenses.def: Specifically, such a contest soliciting audience-submitted coinages, the most quirky and niche of which is chosen for fuller treatment.

example.text: Among all the peanemises sent in from around the world, the nonchalontest crowns whichever wombo is the quichest.

senses.def: By extension, any informal solicitation of entries from one's audience, conducted as an offhand gesture of gratitude.

examples.text: He marked the milestone with a quick nonchalontest, asking only that submissions be the quichest peanemis ever loreained.

senses.def: To run or hold such a contest; to invite submissions in a studiedly unbothered manner.

examples.text: Rather than make a fuss, he simply nonchalontested the milestone and waited for the wombos to roll in.

notes: This video is a follower-milestone celebration and call for submissions rather than a conventional single-word definition. The authoritative on-screen headword, set in the black title box across both frames (f02 and f04), is "Nonchalontest" (NOT the auto-caption-style spelling; confirmed by frame). No (Root + Root) blue etymology box is shown for it, so the Nonchalant + Contest derivation is inferred from the word's evident structure and the contest framing in the captions. Sendy does not give a formal spoken definition of "Nonchalontest," so the senses below are house-style reconstruction consistent with its use as the title of his casual 100k-follower wombo contest; flagged as enrichment. The captions and description are rich in OTHER incidental wombos not selected as the headword: "peanemis"/"peanemises" (Peak + Cinema + Analyses), "quichest" (Quirky + Nichest), "amaxxing" (Amazing + Maxxing), "loreained" (Lore + Explained), and the merch-shirt blend "Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura" in the description. The verb sense (to nonchalontest) is original house-style extension. Examples are drawn from his actual caption usage and the on-screen title, lightly cleaned. Year 2026 from upload_date 20260503.

etymology: Blend of NOURISHMENT and INCREDIBLE (with overlap on the shared -edible / -edib- segment, also evoking EDIBLE), coined by Professor Sendy in 2026 as the lead entry of a rapid-fire "MESO Lesson" series. The lesson chains two productive root families: the NOURISH- family (Nourishment fused with incredible, city boy, and neurodivergent) and the RU-/RUR- family (Rural fused with aura, aurora, and jury+aura). Each wombo is built by speaking the two source words in sequence and then collapsing them at their shared sounds.

senses.def: A blend of "nourishment" and "incredible," joined at their shared central sounds; the lead specimen of Professor Sendy's MESO Lesson, exemplifying the fusion of a nutrition term with a superlative (and punning on "edible").

examples.text: Nourishment, incredible — Nourishedible.

subsenses.def: By extension, any member of the NOURISH- family demonstrated in the same lesson, in which "nourishment" is collapsed into a second word.

example.text: Nourishment plus city boy yields Nourish*tyboy; nourishment plus neurodivergent yields Nourisheurodivergent.

senses.def: More broadly, a wombo produced by the MESO method: speaking two source words aloud and then eliding them into a single portmanteau at their overlapping syllables.

examples.text: Rural plus aura gives Ruaura; rural plus aurora gives Rurora.

subsenses.def: A triple-stacked instance of the same method, fusing three source words.

example.text: Rural, jury, aura — Rururaura.

senses.def: To merge a nourishment term with another word in the MESO manner; to coin a wombo by overlapping shared sounds.

examples.text: He nourishedibled his way through six blends in a single lesson.

notes: This is not a standard single-headword definition video; it is a "MESO Lesson" montage in which Professor Sendy demonstrates six wombos in rapid succession, speaking the two source words and then the blend. On-screen spelling (authoritative) lists: Nourishedible, Nourish*tyboy, Nourisheurodivergent, Ruaura, Rurora, Rururaura. The description repeats these (with a typo'd Ruraura/Rururaura and an extra 'Rururaura'). The captions confirm the source pairs: Nourishment+Incredible, Nourishment+City boy, Nourishment+Neurodivergent, Rural+Aura, Rural+Aurora, Rural+Jury+Aura. I selected Nourishedible as the headword (first and least problematic entry) and recorded the full series as numbered senses, since the schema is single-word. Sendy supplies no glosses — these wombos are presented as pure sound-blends with no stated meanings — so the definitions below are house-style descriptions of each blend's construction rather than invented semantics (flagged as enrichment). Nourish*tyboy is shown self-censored with an asterisk on screen and in the caption (he says "city boy"); kept censored as displayed. "Rururaura" is the on-screen spelling; the caption renders it "Ruruaura." MESO appears to be Sendy's branding for this lesson format. IPA is plausible reconstruction in the dry house register.

etymology: A wombo of OPP (slang clipping of OPPONENT or OPPOSITION, denoting an enemy, rival, or adversary) and POPULAR. The first syllable of POPULAR is supplanted by OPP, fusing the notion of widespread renown with that of antagonism. Coined by Professor Sendy (2026) as one entry in a rapid-fire series demonstrating the productivity of the root element -OPP-, in which OPP is interpolated into a host word so that the word both names and contains an adversary. Compare the companion coinages BROPP (bro + opp), SIOPP (sis + opp), OPPLEGANGER (opp + doppelganger), BABY BOPP (baby boo + opp), TWOPP (twin + opp), OPPICER (officer + opp), PROPPFESSOR (professor + opp), FROPP (friend + opp), and TWIN FLOPP (twin flame + opp).

senses.def: A person who is at once widely liked or socially prominent and, beneath that standing, an opponent or adversary; a popular figure who is secretly or simultaneously one's opp.

subsenses.def: One whose popularity is itself the source of, or cover for, the rivalry; a celebrated rival.

example.text: She isn't just well known around here; she's an oppular, beloved by everyone and against me in everything.

senses.def: Enjoying broad popularity while functioning as an opp; admired and adversarial at the same time.

examples.text: Watch out for the oppular ones at the party; everybody loves them and they're still your opp.

senses.def: A bound element, from OPP (enemy, rival), interpolated into a familiar word so that the resulting term names a person of that relation who is also an opponent; used by Sendy to generate an open-ended family of coinages.

examples.text: Run a bro through it and you get a bropp; run an officer through it and you get an oppicer.
Even your professor can be a proppfessor, and your twin flame a twin flopp.

notes: Source video is a montage: Professor Sendy supplies no single spoken definition, instead rattling off ten OPP-based formulas while the on-screen black box lists each as ROOT + ROOT = WOMBO. The red caption box highlights OPPULAR at the chosen frame, so it is treated as the headword; the full family is recorded in the etymology and the wider -opp- pattern in sense 2. Spellings are taken verbatim from the on-screen box (note the on-screen list renders 'Bro + Opp = Bropp' though the audio also voices 'Bropp'). Because no explicit gloss is given, the definitions are house-style reconstructions inferred from the consistent build pattern (a familiar word reframed so that the person it names is secretly, or additionally, an opp); flagged as enrichment. 'Opp' is established slang (opponent/enemy); the wombo move is Sendy's. IPA is a plausible house rendering. Confidence medium owing to the absence of a verbal definition. Sense 2 (the bound combining form -opp-) and the verb part are tasteful house extrapolations consistent with the demonstrated pattern.

etymology: Blend of PEAK (slang, of the highest quality; superlative) + MACHO (aggressively or admirably masculine) + NEPHEW (a brother's or sister's son; here in its affectionate slang use as a term of endearment for a younger male). The form and stress deliberately echo Pikachu, the Pokemon mascot, an allusion reinforced in use by the franchise catchphrase "gotta catch 'em all." One of four wombos coined in a single segment alongside Nihilisticpenguenuinely, Mundainrot, and Senduminati.

senses.def: A young man regarded as a prize or top-tier specimen; an admirable, impressively masculine fellow held up as one of the best of his kind.

examples.text: That tough little fellow over there is a glistening Peakachew.
When it comes to the Wombos, he's gotta catch them all.

subsenses.def: (by extension) any person or thing prized as a collectible or sought-after find, framed in the manner of a creature to be acquired and amassed.

example.text: When it comes to the Wombos, he's gotta catch them all.

notes: Headword spelling taken authoritatively from the on-screen black box ("Peakachew"); auto-captions render it variously ("Peakachew", "Peakachew"). On-screen etymology boxes read "Peak + Macho + Nephew." Sendy's only literal usage is the single sentence "That really tuff twuh over there is a glussin Peakachew," paired with the Pokemon allusion "he's gotta catch them all"; the gloss "tuff twuh / glussin" is rendered in house prose as "tough little fellow / glistening." Sense 1 paraphrases what he demonstrates (a praiseworthy male specimen). Sub-sense 1a is tasteful house-style enrichment, inferred from the explicit "catch 'em all" Pokemon framing rather than stated outright. The plural form and dry IPA are house additions. The video bundles four distinct wombos; this entry covers only Peakachew per the one-entry brief. Upload date 20260323 sets the year to 2026.

etymology: Blend of peak + cinema, the initial syllable of "peak" fusing with "cinema" to yield "Peanema." The headword serves as the seed of a progressively stacked compound: Peanema + Regina George + Mean Girls + Lost Queen Bee Status yields the extended form "Peanereginageorgeangirllostqueenbeetus," to which "+ Lord of the Rings" is further appended to produce "Peanereginageorgeangirllostqueenbeetusordoftherings." Each accreted root names another emblem of cinematic greatness, the word lengthening in proportion to the magnitude of acclaim asserted.

senses.def: The highest attainable standard of filmmaking; cinema at its absolute apex, regarded as the summit of the art form.

examples.text: That sequence wasn't merely good film — it was Peanema.

subsenses.def: By extension, any work, scene, or moment held to be transcendently great, whether or not strictly cinematic.

example.text: Three rewatches in and the verdict stands: Peanema.

senses.def: A superlative formed by accretion, in which each additional cinematic or pop-cultural root appended to the base raises the asserted tier of greatness; the longer the resulting compound, the higher the praise.

examples.text: Regina George reigning as the lost queen bee of Mean Girls is pure Peanereginageorgeangirllostqueenbeetus.
Add the One Ring and you have ascended to Peanereginageorgeangirllostqueenbeetusordoftherings.

subsenses.def: In its first extended form (Peanereginageorgeangirllostqueenbeetus), peak cinema as embodied by Regina George, the archetypal lost queen bee of Mean Girls.

example.text: Her downfall arc is textbook Peanereginageorgeangirllostqueenbeetus.
