Both permitted frames (f02 and f04) show only the intro card ("The Top Comment becomes a WOMBO") and the "Ready / Set / Go" countdown overlay — neither captured the black headword box or the blue (Root + Root) etymology boxes, so the AUTHORITATIVE on-screen spelling could NOT be verified and "Readeto" is the auto-caption spelling. The definition, part-of-speech demonstrations, etymological roots (READY + burrITO), and IPA below are HOUSE-STYLE RECONSTRUCTION inferred from the word's shape and the "ready"-themed countdown lead-in, NOT from anything Sendy literally said in the available sources. The description's "LOWKIRK" is a separate merch wombo, not this video's word. Upload date 20260224 → year 2026. Recommend re-pulling with the word-reveal frame and full captions before publishing; confidence is low.

etymology: Blend (wombo) of the personal name Renee + revolution, the second element being absorbed into the first so that the name itself becomes the upheaval. Coined by Professor Sendy and presented as the lead item in a rapid-fire round of like-formed coinages, several of which exploit the buried syllable "ice" in agent — Miceagent (male + ICE agent), Liceagent (lady + ICE agent), and Viceagent (vice + ICE agent) — alongside Mift (mask + lift) and Phic (phone + pic).

senses.def: A personal upheaval or sweeping change of life conducted entirely under, and named for, the person undertaking it; a self-titled revolution.

examples.text: She quit, moved, and changed her name in one weekend — a complete Reneevolution.

subsenses.def: The movement or following that gathers around such a self-named upheaval.

example.text: Join the Reneevolution.

senses.def: By extension, the productive pattern of folding a name or modifier into a longer word so that a hidden syllable does the joining, as in the ICE-agent series demonstrated alongside it.

examples.text: First the Reneevolution, then the whole roll call: Miceagent, Liceagent, Viceagent.

senses.def: Any of a set of coinages formed by prefixing a single letter or modifier to 'ice agent' so that the result reads as a new word.

examples.text: Male ice agent — Miceagent.

subsenses.def: Miceagent — a male ice agent.

example.text: Miceagent.

subsenses.def: Liceagent — a lady ice agent; also (enrichment) jocularly read as an ice agent overrun by lice.

example.text: Lady ice agent — Liceagent.

senses.def: Viceagent — an ice agent given over to vice (so rendered from the visible V; Sendy's spoken caption says 'addicted ice agent').

examples.text: Viceagent.

senses.def: Mift — a face lift achieved or simulated by a mask; a mask-lift.

examples.text: Mask, lift — Mift.

senses.def: Phic — a photograph taken on a phone; a phone pic.

examples.text: Phone, pic — Phic.

notes: Round-up video: the persistent on-screen headword (top black box throughout all six frames, and the post title/description) is 'Reneevolution', so it is treated as the primary entry. Spelling taken from the frame, which is authoritative over the auto-captions ('Renee. Revolution.' / 'Reneevolution'). The lower black boxes list a fixed related cluster — Miceagent, Liceagent, Viceagent — and the captions add Mift (Mask + Lift) and Phic (Phone + Pic). No (Root + Root) blue etymology boxes appear in this video; the construction is conveyed purely by Sendy speaking each source word before its blend, so the root analysis is inferred from his spoken pairings. ENRICHMENT (clearly marked, beyond what Sendy literally states): Sendy does not gloss what 'Reneevolution' MEANS — he only voices the components — so the senses, usage labels ('chiefly with possessive'), the suffixal/pattern sense built on the ICE-agent set, and all illustrative example sentences are original house-style extrapolation consistent with the blend. The caption for the V-form reads 'Addicted Ice agent' while the visible word is 'Viceagent'; I have reconciled this to vice (the V) and recorded the discrepancy here. Confidence is medium because the meaning is enriched rather than stated.

etymology: Blend of rizz (clipping of charisma, denoting romantic or social charm) and resonance (a deep, sympathetic vibration; figuratively, the quality of striking a profound chord). Coined by Professor Sendy, 2026.

senses.def: Charm or romantic appeal so potent that it seems to vibrate sympathetically in the observer; charisma raised to a resonant, almost physical pitch.

examples.text: He has real clavicular claims, that one — pure rizzonance, though honestly it's a little bit delularisma.

subsenses.def: The measurable effect such charm produces in onlookers; the sympathetic stirring or swooning it induces.

example.text: You could feel the rizzonance the moment he walked in.

subsenses.def: depreciative. Charm that is largely imagined or self-asserted rather than externally confirmed; charisma bordering on delusion.

example.text: He calls it rizzonance, but honestly it's a little bit delularisma.

senses.def: to exert charm of a resonant, reverberating kind; to project rizz with such intensity that it produces a sympathetic response in others.

examples.text: He just stands there rizzonating and the whole room leans in.

notes: On-screen etymology is authoritative: "Rizz + Resonance = Rizzonance" (first of four wombos shown in the same video; the others are Ohigma = Ohio + Sigma, Frogging = Frame + Mogging, and Slibe Check = Slay + Vibe Check). Extracted Rizzonance as the lead headword since it is first in both the frame and the title. Sendy's direct usage is thin: in the captions he uses it as a noun describing a person's appeal ("Rizzonance, but honestly it's a little bit Delularisma"), alongside the phrase "clavicular claims." The core sense 1 (resonant charisma) is faithful to his usage and the etymology. Enrichment flagged: subsense 1b (the self-deluding "delularisma" shade) is drawn directly from his line; the verb form "rizzonate/rizzonating" and example sentences in 1a and the verb sense are house-style extrapolations consistent with the blend, not literal Sendy quotes. IPA given in two dry variants (stress on either the rizz- or the -nance element). Year 2026 from upload_date 20260316.

etymology: Coined by Professor Sendy (2026) as a blend of "sea lion" and "vibrantly." Presented as the seed of an accreting compound: each successive line in the video grafts further roots onto the base, growing from "Sealibrantly" through "Sealibraluperkirkflowendelululemoniminalowkirkilisticlowkaura" (with the addition "Superkirkflowendelululemoniminalowkirkilisticlowkaura") to the maximal form "Sealibraluperkirkflowendelululemoniminallowkirkilistickaureadamentalockinlowingbaunergeticyeetkineticsenderblender" (with the further addition "Deadamentalockinlowingbaunergeticyeetkineticsenderblend"). The terminal element -ly marks the seed form as adverbial.

parts.label: Sendyism
parts.forms: comparative more sealibrantly; superlative most sealibrantly

senses.def: In the manner of a sea lion at its most spirited; with a glistening, buoyant, full-bodied liveliness, as of a creature moving brightly through water.

examples.text: She entered the room sealibrantly, all gleam and forward momentum.

subsenses.def: Of motion or bearing: sleekly and energetically, with an aquatic vivacity.

example.text: He barked the greeting sealibrantly across the lobby.

subsenses.def: By extension, of color, sound, or presence: vividly and abundantly, in a way that seems to surge.

example.text: The mural glowed sealibrantly under the morning light.

parts.label: Sendyism, nonce
parts.forms: third person singular present sealibrates; present participle sealibrating; past and past participle sealibrated

senses.def: To move or behave with the glistening, buoyant liveliness of a sea lion; to carry oneself vibrantly and aquatically.

examples.text: The whole crowd began to sealibrate the moment the music started.

senses.def: An ever-lengthening compound built by grafting successive roots onto the seed word Sealibrantly; the act or product of such verbal accretion, in which a single coinage is grown line by line into a maximal form.

examples.text: By the third line it had become a full Sealibraluperkirkflowendelululemoniminallowkirkilistickaureadamentalockinlowingbaunergeticyeetkineticsenderblender.

notes: No spoken captions (SRT) exist for this video, so the definition is reconstructed from the on-screen etymology boxes (Sea Lion + Vibrantly) and Sendy's established accretion format rather than a literal spoken gloss. Confidence is low for the precise meaning. The on-screen black-box headword anchoring the entry is "Sealibrantly"; the two longer forms shown are accreted extensions built live in the video, captured here as inflected/extended forms and the verbal sense. The sea-lion-in-motion reading of sense 1, the verb form, and the longer accreted variants are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with the visible roots, since no audio definition was available. Note the description/title also advertise a separate shirt word, "Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura," which is merch and not the defined term. The on-screen spelling varies slightly between accretion stages (e.g. "alow-"/"allow-", "-laura"/"-laura"); spellings here follow the frame exactly per stage.

etymology: A wombo (portmanteau) coined by Professor Sendy, blending his own name "Sendy" with "city boy," "baby boo," and "genuinely" (rendered phonetically as -boouinely). The construction layers a term of endearment ("baby boo") and a self-referential signature ("Sendy") onto the adverb "genuinely," yielding an ornamented intensifier. Sendy further demonstrates an extended, maximally compounded variant — "Sendityboouineluperkirkflowendelululemonimin­aldeadamentakirkfuffle" (Sendityboouinely + Superkirkflowendelululemonimin­aldeadamentakirkfuffle) — and a Diddy-inflected pronunciation, "Sendiddyboouinely."

senses.def: in a genuine or earnest manner; truly, sincerely — used as an emphatic and affectionate intensifier of the speaker's sincerity.

examples.text: I am, Sendityboouinely, the realest one here.

subsenses.def: used to mark a statement as heartfelt or unironic; lowkey genuinely.

example.text: Sendityboouinely, I just want the best for you.

subsenses.def: in its maximally compounded form, used hyperbolically to convey the most extravagant possible degree of sincerity.

example.text: That was, Sendityboouineluperkirkflowendelululemoniminaldeadamentakirkfuffle, the greatest meal of my life.

senses.def: characterized by affectionate sincerity; earnestly heartfelt.

examples.text: It was a Sendityboouinely sweet thing to say.

notes: On-screen spelling is authoritative: headword "Sendityboouinely" (note doubled -oo-). The frame's blue etymology box reads "Sendy + City Boy + Baby Boo + Genuinely." The captions are auto-transcribed and misspell the word; they show Sendy assembling it from "Sendy / city boy / baby boo / genuinely" and then pronouncing a Diddy variant ("Sendiddyboouinely," caption-spelled). The only stated semantic anchor is the shirt/lower-third text "lowkey genuinely," so the core sense (an earnest, sincere intensifier) is taken from that; the gradable-adverb framing, the adjective sub-sense, and the extended-form usage note are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with the build. Year 2026 from upload_date 20260306. The maximal stacked form and "LOWKIRK" merch appear in the description; confidence is medium because Sendy performs rather than verbally defines the word.

etymology: A blend (portmanteau) of "serendipity" (a fortunate or happy chance, ultimately from Horace Walpole's 1754 coinage after the Persian fairy tale "The Three Princes of Serendip") and "pookie," an affectionate slang term of endearment for a romantic partner or close companion. The medial syllables collapse so that the tail of "serendipity" gives way to the "-ookie" of "pookie," yielding "serendookie." Coined by Professor Sendy.

senses.def: An affectionate term of endearment for a romantic partner or sweetheart, especially one regarded as a happy or fortunate find; a beloved who is also a stroke of good luck.

examples.text: Are you taking a date to the Valentine's dance? Nah, that's just my serendookie.

subsenses.def: Used loosely of any cherished companion regarded as a chance blessing.

example.text: Meeting her at the bus stop was pure luck; now she's my serendookie.

notes: Frame f02 confirms the on-screen spelling and etymology: "Serendipity + Pookie = Serendookie." This video is a five-word compilation (Serendookie, Burgretfully, Peanema, Twusin, Quarter Zang); per the one-entry instruction I extracted the lead headword, which also heads the title and description. The captions supply Sendy's actual usage exchange ("date to the Valentine's dance? / Nah that's just my serendookie"), rewritten lightly into a sentence. The gloss (term of endearment tied to a lucky find) is inferred from the etymology and the Valentine's-dance context, since Sendy does not give an explicit definition; the plural form, the IPA, sense 1a subsense, and the synonym list are house-style enrichment consistent with his meaning. Upload date 20260305 gives the year 2026. Other words in this compilation (Burgretfully = Burger + Regretfully; Peanema = Peak + Cinema; Twusin = Twin + Bruh + Cousin; Quarter Zang = Quarter Zip + Zang) are candidates for their own entries.

etymology: A wombo (word + combo), blending "should" and "did," with the modal "shoul-" collapsed into the past-tense "did" so that obligation and accomplishment occupy a single syllable. Coined by Professor Sendy in 2026. The contraction parallels the colloquial spelling "shid" already in circulation as a euphemistic respelling of an unrelated vulgarity; Sendy's coinage is independent of that sense and denotes only the should-plus-did meaning.

parts.label: informal
parts.forms: used chiefly in the past tense; present shid, past shid

senses.def: to have done at last a thing one ought to have done long before; to discharge a long-deferred obligation, especially with a note of relief or belated triumph.

examples.text: Did I do it? I finally shid.
Can you believe I actually shid it?

subsenses.def: in the second person, used to congratulate another on completing such an overdue task; often laudatory.

example.text: You're the man, because you shid it all over the brarpet.

subsenses.def: of an action: to have been both warranted and carried out at once, collapsing the gap between what was owed and what was done.

example.text: He didn't just mean to fix it; he shid it.

notes: On-screen spelling and etymology are unambiguous across frames f01-f05: the black headword box implies "shid" and the blue etymology box reads "Should + Did = Shid." The auto-caption SRT renders his usage examples accurately ("I finally shid," "I actually shid it," "you shid it all over the brarpet"); "brarpet" (Bro + Carpet) is a separate incidental wombo dropped into the third example and is not the headword. Definition rewritten in house style from Sendy's stated meaning: a thing you "did" that you "should have done a long time ago." ENRICHMENT added by me (not stated verbatim by Sendy): sub-sense 1a (the second-person congratulatory use) is generalized from his "you're the man because you shid it" example; sub-sense 1b and its example sentence are original house-style extensions consistent with the meaning; the IPA, the "forms" note (treating it as an invariant/strong past), the synonyms, and the etymological observation about the pre-existing euphemistic spelling are my additions. IPA given as the natural monosyllable /ʃɪd/.

etymology: A wombo (word combo) blending the vulgar noun s***s (the colloquial pairing in the phrase "for s***s and giggles," denoting idle amusement) with giggles (light, involuntary laughter). Per Professor Sendy, the term is deployed when a thing is "chillarious" (chill + hilarious) — that is, amusing in a relaxed, low-key register. Coined by Professor Sendy, 2026.

parts.label: plural in form, often construed as singular; usually with the

senses.def: A fit of mild, easygoing laughter; the relaxed amusement provoked by something at once calming and funny. Compare the affliction known as the giggles, but in a lower, more laid-back key.

examples.text: Last night I had the shiggles; my twousin had the shingles and my brousin had the shiggles, but my twister had just the regular giggles.

subsenses.def: The condition or state of being so amused, framed (mock-medically) as something one "has," by analogy with having the shingles.

example.text: My brousin had the shiggles all weekend.

senses.def: To pursue or indulge in shiggles to the fullest; to spend one's time deliberately courting low-key, chillarious amusement.

examples.text: Let's go shigglemaxxing this tweekend.

notes: On-screen spelling "Shiggles" and etymology box "S***s + Giggles" are authoritative (frame f02.jpg). Sendy explicitly defines usage: deploy the wombo "when something's chillarious (chill + hilarious)," and contrasts shiggles with both the shingles and the "regular giggles." The verb sense is house-style enrichment derived from his demonstrated form "Shigglemaxxing" ("you could even maxx it out... let's go shigglemaxxing"); labeled accordingly. Definitions are original paraphrase, not Sendy's wording. IPA is a plausible house-style reconstruction. The clip is laced with other nonce wombos (twousin, brousin, twister, twinjoying, tweekend, etc.) used only as filler context for the example, so they are not given their own senses here.

etymology: A blend of short (from Middle English short, from Old English sċort "not long, brief") and -maxx, the suffixal element abstracted from looksmaxxing and the wider -maxxing family (ultimately from maximize, with the doubled medial consonant characteristic of online orthography). Literally "to maximize shortness." Coined by Professor Sendy (2026) to name the iterative procedure by which an unwieldy compound word is driven, syllable by syllable, toward its briefest possible form. The closing form te te and se se mirror the reduplicative endpoint of the process.

parts.label: often progressive; chiefly in 'shortmaxxing a word'

senses.def: To reduce a long or unwieldy word to a progressively shorter form by repeatedly clipping its syllables, each pass yielding a tighter contraction than the last until only a minimal stub remains.

subsenses.def: To perform a single such reduction; to render an existing form shorter.

example.text: Superkirkiflowilisticexpialibrostate, shortened, becomes Supirklowifexbrate.

subsenses.def: To carry the reduction through to its theoretical limit, collapsing the word into a single reduplicated syllable.

example.text: Lote, shortened, becomes te te; the word has been fully shortmaxxed.

examples.text: He kept shortmaxxing Lowkirkentologiclowstate down to Lokirenloclotate, then Lorencolte, then Lote.

senses.def: The practice or act of shortmaxxing; the iterative trimming of a word's syllables toward maximal brevity.

examples.text: Shortmaxxing: Superkirkiflowilisticexpialibrostate and Lowkirkentologiclowstate, each taken to its shortest form.

senses.def: The ritual call uttered after each reduction to signal that another syllable-trimming pass has been completed and the next, shorter form is to follow.

examples.text: Spirofebre. Spirofebre, shortened. Sire. Sire, shortened. Se se, shortened.

notes: Frame is authoritative: the black-box headword is clearly "Shortmaxxing" across all frames. The SRT only captions the chant ("shortened") and Sendy's spoken renderings of each progressively clipped form, which the auto-captions mangle (e.g. "Supirklowifexbrate", "Spirofebre", "Lokirenloclotate", "Lorencolte", "Lote", "te te") — these stub forms are taken as he pronounced them, with spelling reconstructed from the audio since they are not shown in boxes. The two source words shown in the lower on-screen boxes are "Superkirkiflowilisticexpialibrostate" (a Charlie Kirk + supercalifragilisticexpialidocious + "state" blend) and "Lowkirkentologiclowstate"; I trusted those exact box spellings. Sendy himself only demonstrates the process (a word being "shortened" repeatedly); he does not verbally define "Shortmaxxing" in the captions, so the prose definitions are my house-style reconstruction of the procedure he enacts. ENRICHMENT I added beyond what he literally says: the verb sub-senses 1a/1b, the separate noun and the "shortened" interjection entry (derived directly from his recurring on-screen chant), the synonyms ("wordmaxxing", "truncmaxxing", "syllablemaxxing"), the second IPA, and the full etymology including the -maxx / looksmaxxing lineage. Description ("Shortmaxxing Superkirkiflowilisticexpialibrostate & Lowkirkentologiclowstate") confirms the headword and the two target words. Year 2026 from upload_date 20260130.

etymology: Blend of sigma (slang for a self-reliant, dominant, "main character" type of man, ultimately from the Greek letter sigma as repurposed in 2020s online masculinity discourse) and demurely (in a modest, reserved, understated manner; from demure, via Old French demoré "settled, composed"). The compound is deliberately oxymoronic, yoking brash self-assurance to quiet restraint. Coined by Professor Sendy.

parts.label: internet slang
parts.forms: comparative more sigmurely, superlative most sigmurely

senses.def: In a manner that is simultaneously brashly self-assured and quietly modest; in a way that projects dominant "main character" confidence while at the same time appearing reserved and understated.

subsenses.def: Of conduct: combining the swagger of a self-reliant alpha with conspicuous, almost contradictory modesty.

example.text: You're giving main character energy, but at the same time you're being demure -- you're acting sigmurely.

subsenses.def: Used to flag the inherent contradiction in such a bearing, where outward coolness and inward bashfulness coexist.

example.text: You're the coolest, most authentic guy ever, but somehow not confident -- what is this, sigmurely?

examples.text: Sigma demurely. Sigmurely. You're acting real confident and giving main character energy, but at the same time you're being demure.

senses.def: By extension, in a self-contradictory or oxymoronic fashion that nonetheless reads as endearing or stylish.

examples.text: It's a contradictory word, but that's okay -- a lot of words are kind of oxymoronic, and he's doing it sigmurely.

senses.def: Of a person or demeanor: at once dominantly confident and demurely modest; embodying the paradox of sigma swagger paired with bashful reserve.

examples.text: He's got this sigmurely thing going on -- the coolest, most authentic dude alive, yet acting like he isn't confident at all.

notes: On-screen text is authoritative: black box reads "Sigmurely"; blue etymology boxes read "Sigma + Demurely". The auto-caption rendered it correctly ("Sigmurely") and also showed the spoken setup "Sigma Demurely." Definition is drawn directly from Sendy's captions: confident / "main character energy" / "coolest, most authentic dude" (sigma) coexisting with "being demure" and "not confident" (demurely), which he himself calls "contradictory" and "oxymoronic." House-style enrichment beyond his literal words: I added an adjective part of speech (he uses the word as a standalone label/exclamation, so an attributive sense is a reasonable extension), sense 2 generalizing the oxymoronic usage, comparative/superlative forms, and the synonyms; these are consistent with his stated meaning but are editorial additions. IPA is a plausible house-style reconstruction. The description's unrelated merch word ("Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura shirt") is a separate product tie-in and not this video's headword.

etymology: A cumulative blend (wombo) coined by Professor Sendy, assembled by progressive lamination of internet-slang roots. The base term Sigmurely fuses SIGMA (the lone-wolf, self-possessed archetype of online masculine lore) with DEMURELY (the "very demure, very mindful" idiom of restrained, tasteful conduct). The intermediate Sigmurgly interpolates BURGER (the foodstuff, inserted as a tonal disruptor) to yield Sigma + Burger + Demurely. The full form appends Perchentologicalaura — itself a stack of perch, the suffix complex -entological (a mock-scholarly extension of -ological), and aura (the perceived energetic field or vibe of a person) — producing the maximal accreted headword. The terminal -aura and pseudo-Latinate -entological apparatus lend the word its deadpan academic gloss.

senses.def: The maximal accreted quality of a person who is at once a self-possessed sigma and conspicuously demure, possessed of an elaborately scholarly aura; the cumulative top tier of esteem in Professor Sendy's stacking taxonomy.

examples.text: By the final tier he had ascended to Sigmurglentologicalaura.

subsenses.def: Sigmurely: the base grade, denoting one who carries himself in a sigma manner yet very demurely.

example.text: He took the compliment Sigmurely, neither flinching nor gloating.

subsenses.def: Sigmurgly: the middle grade, in which the demure sigma bearing is complicated by the introduction of a burger; a lone-wolf composure undercut, or seasoned, by the foodstuff.

example.text: Mid-bite he remained Sigmurgly, unbothered and unhurried.

senses.def: In a manner that is simultaneously sigma and demure; with quiet, self-assured restraint.

examples.text: She declined the spotlight Sigmurely.

notes: Stacking-style video: Professor Sendy builds one cumulative word by layering roots, defining each intermediate form before laminating the next. The on-screen black boxes are authoritative for spelling and confirm three pinned forms: Sigmurely (Sigma + Demurely), Sigmurgly (Sigma + Burger + Demurely), and Sigmurglentologicalaura (prior + Perchentologicalaura). The auto-caption renders the final word as 'sigmurglentologicalaura'/'sigmurgly' — frame spelling preferred. Sendy provides no spoken prose definition in the captions (the audio is just the roots being recited), so the senses below are house-style reconstructions of the intended meaning from the documented root combinations; flagged as enrichment. The video description also references a separate merch word, 'Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura' (a related stacking coinage sold on a shirt), not defined here. Years set from upload_date 20260311. IPA is a plausible dry-register approximation.

etymology: A blend (wombo) coined by Professor Sendy, formed from the meme numeral "67" (rendered as "six or seven") + o'clock + apocalypse. The three roots are telescoped so that the terminal -seven o'clock overlaps with the apocalyptic suffix -clockalypse, yielding a single word that fuses the "six seven" internet catchphrase with the registers of timekeeping and cataclysm. Modelled on the productive English suffix -(ca)lypse (as in "snowpocalypse," "carmageddon"), which converts any noun into the name of a chaotic, large-scale event.

senses.def: An event, gathering, or occasion of an exceptionally wild, chaotic, or unrestrained character; a happening so intense as to feel cataclysmic.

examples.text: The party this weekend was the sixorseveno'clockalypse.
During a sixorseveno'clockalypse, everybody's going to be having a nonchalashout all over their brarpets.

subsenses.def: By extension, the climactic or peak moment of such an event, when the chaos reaches its height.

parts.forms: third person singular present sixorseveno'clockalauras; past and past participle sixorseveno'clockalaura'd

senses.def: Of a person: to bring about or contribute to such wild, apocalyptic revelry; to cause an occasion to become a sixorseveno'clockalypse.

examples.text: You could even say that somebody sixorseveno'clockalaura'd.

notes: On-screen black text box gives the authoritative spelling \"Sixorseveno'clockalypse\"; the blue etymology box reads \"67 + O'Clock + Apocalypse.\" Auto-captions render the word phonetically across multiple lines, confirming the spoken form. The noun sense and meaning (\"an event that was really wild and crazy\") are taken directly from Sendy's captions. Sendy also demonstrates a verb form, which he renders as \"sixorseveno'clockalaura\" — a play that swaps the -alypse ending for the -laura/Lauren riff he is fond of (he asks to \"quiche Loreain it\" before giving the verb example); the verb conjugation forms are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with his usage. Sense 1a (the climactic peak) is mild house-style enrichment extending his \"wild and crazy event\" meaning. The video's product tie-in references a separate wombo (\"Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura\"), unrelated to this entry.

etymology: A wombo blending Slim Shady, the alter ego of the rapper Eminem, with shaking (present participle of shake, in the sense of trembling from nerves or being visibly rattled). Coined by Professor Sendy in the course of an imagined rap battle against Eminem, the term repurposes the icon of battle-rap dominance to name its opposite: the conspicuous loss of composure. The fused form deliberately collapses "Slim Shady" and "shaking" into a single trochaic word, so that the rapper's own name becomes the act of folding under pressure.

parts.label: intransitive; chiefly in the progressive 'be slimshaking'

senses.def: To tremble or visibly falter under pressure, especially in a contest of words; to lose one's composure and nerve at the decisive moment, as a rapper might who has been outmatched.

examples.text: Hey, Eminem, it seems like you're really slimshaking right now.

subsenses.def: To be outclassed in a rap battle or verbal exchange and to show it through audible faltering, stalling, or shaken delivery.

example.text: He started so confident, but two bars in he was slimshaking in front of the whole crowd.

subsenses.def: By extension, to crumble under any high-stakes scrutiny; to be conspicuously rattled when called upon to perform.

example.text: The challenger stepped to the mic and immediately began slimshaking.

senses.def: The state or act of trembling and losing one's nerve in a battle of wits or words; the visible collapse of an opponent's composure once they have been bested.

examples.text: The last time I checked the crowd decides the winner, and the crowd can always spot the slimshaking.

notes:
FORMAT CAVEAT: This is not one of Sendy's standard definition videos. The frames carry only the on-screen title 'Eminem vs. Professor Sendy' (a skit) plus auto-generated subtitle text; there is NO black headword box and NO blue (Root + Root) etymology boxes in any of the six frames. The headword was therefore taken from the spoken captions rather than an authoritative on-screen spelling. The SRT shows the coinage in two stages: 'Slim Shady faking.' then 'Slimshaking.' — the auto-caption first mis-hears it as 'Slim Shady faking,' then renders the wombo as 'Slimshaking,' which I treat as the intended headword (Slim Shady + shaking). Spelling 'slimshaking' (lowercase, one word) is my normalization; an alternative capitalized 'SlimShaking' or hyphenated 'slim-shaking' is plausible. Confidence is medium because the exact spelling is inferred from audio, not shown on screen.

SENSE BASIS: Sendy literally uses the word once, as a verb in the progressive ('you're really slimshaking right now'), addressed to Eminem as a clapback in an imagined battle. The core verb sense 1 and its meaning (trembling/faltering under pressure, being shown up) are derived directly from that usage and the surrounding setup (crowd decides the winner; he'd be the 'undisputed champ').

ENRICHMENT (original, house-style, added by me — NOT spoken by Sendy): subsense 1b (extension to any high-stakes scrutiny), the entire noun part of speech and its sense, the principal-parts forms including the mock-irregular past 'slimshook,' the synonyms list, the IPA, and the two unsourced/secondary examples (the 1b example and the noun-sense example built from his real 'the crowd decides the winner' line). All are consistent with and clearly derived from his meaning and do not contradict anything he said.

etymology: A productive combining element isolated by Professor Sendy from the vocative slang "son" (as in the address bro, son). In the present treatment it is presented not as a standalone coinage but as the common root threading a family of wombos, each formed by fusing "son" onto an existing slang term: Son + Nonchalant + Crashout > Sonchalashout; Twin + Son > Twon; Son + Opinion > Sonion; Cap + Free + Son > Capree Son; Chud + Son > Chun; Son + Larp > Sorp; Deadassionate + Son > Deadassonate; Bro + Son > Bron; Larping + Bro + Son > Labron. Several of the outputs additionally pun on existing proper forms, most notably Labron (Bro + Son layered over the name LeBron) and Capree Son (the juice brand Capri Sun).

senses.def: A productive slang element, derived from the vocative address "son," that is fused onto an existing word or term of address to generate a new blended form; the unifying root from which a whole family of related wombos is built.

examples.text: Son plus Nonchalant plus Crashout gives Sonchalashout.
Bro plus Son gives Bron, and Larping plus Bro plus Son gives Labron.

subsenses.def: Prefixed or suffixed to a term of address (bro, twin) to coin a fused vocative.

example.text: Twin plus Son gives Twon; Bro plus Son gives Bron.

subsenses.def: Blended into a slang noun or adjective (opinion, larp, chud, cap, deadassionate) to yield a son-inflected variant.

example.text: Son plus Opinion gives Sonion, and Chud plus Son gives Chun.

senses.def: A familiar term of address among peers, equivalent to bro or twin; the base lexeme from which the combining element is drawn.

examples.text: Used as the everyday address son, it becomes the seed for forms such as Sorp and Sonion.

notes: This is a multi-wombo compilation video (nine coinages) rather than a single-word definition. The on-screen black highlight box isolates the word SON, marking it as the shared root/headword that ties every blend together; the captions only read the formulas aloud and the spoken track gives no separate prose definition, so the senses are reconstructed from the demonstrated formations rather than from an explicit gloss — hence medium confidence. The two parts of speech (combining form and the base slang noun) and the sub-senses (a: address blends; b: slang-term blends) are house-style enrichment consistent with what the frame shows. Component wombos demonstrated: Sonchalashout, Twon (caption auto-spells \"twun\" but frame reads Twon), Sonion, Capree Son, Chun, Sorp, Deadassonate, Bron, Labron. Several puns on real referents (Capri Sun, LeBron) are noted in the etymology. Upload date 20260522 -> year 2026.

etymology: Blend of son (a young man addressed familiarly, as in colloquial American "son") and sensei (Japanese 先生, "teacher, master"). The resulting wombo fuses the affectionate-bro register of "son" with the venerated, instructive standing of a martial-arts or spiritual master, yielding a friend who is at once a peer and a revered teacher.

senses.def: A friend held in genuine esteem who nonetheless commits acts of outlandish or reckless behaviour; a companion who commands respect as a mentor yet conducts himself audaciously.

examples.text: I was vibing when my sonsei walked up and dropped The Balsend Peanemis.

subsenses.def: Especially, a peer regarded as a quasi-teacher or master, from whom one takes instruction or example.

example.text: My sonsei showed me how to be lowkey.

subsenses.def: Used as a term of address for such a friend.

example.text: Capree, son, I'm not trying to do that.

notes: On-screen headword and etymology ("Sonsei", Son + Sensei) confirmed from frame f02.jpg; matches the spoken captions and the post title/description. The core sense ("a respectable friend who does outrageous things") is taken directly from Sendy's captions and rewritten in house style. Sendy demonstrates both a direct-address vocative use ("when my sonsei walked up") and a generic noun use; the vocative sub-sense (1b) is a tasteful house-style formalization of that demonstrated usage. The plural "sonseis" is regularized in the dry house register (Sendy does not inflect it on screen). IPA reflects the inherited stress and vowel of "sensei" with the front-loaded "son-"; both the British and American first-vowel realizations are given.

etymology: A blend of spiritual (or spirituality) and the suffix-like wombo, with niche compressed into the seam; coined by Professor Sendy as a self-describing term for a wombo drawn from the spiritual-niche lexicon. The accompanying specimen, Lightwannunakabbalahermesonsciousnalcykybalion, is itself fused from light worker + Anunnaki + Kabbalah + Hermes + one consciousness + halcyon + Kybalion.

senses.def: A wombo (compound coinage) assembled wholly from the vocabulary of the spiritual or New Age niche, fusing terms of mysticism, esotericism, and consciousness culture into a single unpronounceable word.

examples.text: Spiritual Niche Wombo equals Spirichombo.

subsenses.def: the category or genre of such a coinage, as distinct from wombos drawn from other niches.

example.text: Stack enough light-worker jargon together and you have yourself a spirichombo.

senses.def: The exemplar spirichombo presented in the entry, a single word fused from light worker, Anunnaki, Kabbalah, Hermes, one consciousness, halcyon, and Kybalion.

examples.text: Light worker, Anunnaki, Kabbalah, Hermes, one consciousness, halcyon, Kybalion: Lightwannunakabbalahermesonsciousnalcykybalion.

notes: The on-screen text is the authoritative source here; the spoken captions only recite the component roots (Light worker, Anunnaki, Kabbalah, Hermes, one consciousness, halcyon, Kybalion) without narrating a definition. The black title box explicitly equates 'Spiritual Niche Wombo = Spirichombo', making Spirichombo the coined headword and Lightwannunakabbalahermesonsciousnalcykybalion its demonstrative specimen. The second part of speech (the long blended noun) and sense 2 are tasteful house-style enrichment: Sendy presents the string as the worked example of a spirichombo, and the entry formalizes that relationship. IPA is a plausible reconstruction. The first sense's definition paraphrases the category Sendy names rather than copying his wording. The description appends an unrelated merch plug (a separate shirt/hat wombo, 'Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura'), not part of this entry.

etymology: A maximal portmanteau coined by Professor Sendy, blending eight pejorative adjectives into a single word: stupid + tedious + cheugy + dull + monotonous + lame + unimaginative + boring. The components are telescoped so that the tail of each root overlaps the head of the next — stuped-(ious) absorbing "tedious," -heugy- representing "cheugy," -dullot-onous- fusing "dull" and "monotonous," -ame- standing in for "lame," and -maginating folding "unimaginative" into a verbal "-ing" ending borrowed from "boring." The result is presented as the longest possible way to call a person boring.

senses.def: Boring to the most exhaustive degree imaginable; so thoroughly uninteresting that no single ordinary adjective can capture the tedium, requiring instead a cumulative indictment of every kind of dullness at once.

subsenses.def: Of a person: stupefyingly tiresome to be around; combining stupidity, tedium, dowdiness, and a total absence of imagination.

example.text: He talked for an hour about his commute and managed to be utterly stupediousheugydullotonousamemaginatoring.

subsenses.def: Of an event, story, or pastime: drearily monotonous and devoid of any redeeming spark.

example.text: The seminar was the most stupediousheugydullotonousamemaginatoring two hours of my life.

examples.text: It is the longest way to call somebody boring: stupediousheugydullotonousamemaginatoring.

senses.def: The condition or quality of being boring carried to its absolute maximum; comprehensive, multi-faceted tedium.

examples.text: His presentation achieved a level of stupediousheugydullotonousamemaginatoring rarely seen.

notes: On-screen spelling is authoritative: "Stupediousheugydullotonousamemaginatoring" (auto-captions truncated to just "boring"). The blue etymology box lists eight roots in order: Stupid + Tedious + Cheugy + Dull + Monotonous + Lame + Unimaginative + Boring. Sendy frames it only as "the longest way to call somebody boring"; the part-of-speech analysis (adjective sense plus the gerund/verbal form implied by the "-ing" tail), the numbered shades of meaning, and the IPA are tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with the stated meaning. The "-toring/-ing" suffix gives the word a gerund-like surface form despite functioning as an intensified synonym for "boring." The shirt/hat promo ("LOWKIRK") in the caption is unrelated merch, not part of the entry.

etymology: A mock-superlative nonce-word modelled on the Sherman brothers' "supercalifragilisticexpialidocious" (popularized in the 1964 film Mary Poppins), into which a situational phrase is inserted and the closing element resolved to a rhyming noun in -state. In the headword: super- (intensive prefix) + kirki- (the fixed scaffolding syllables of the template) + blizzard + -rific (variant of -rrific, as in terrific) + expiali- (carried over unchanged from the model word) + snow + state. The recurring frame is Superkirki-[situation]-expiali-[noun]state, the slot noun typically rhyming on -ow (snow, slow, glow, blow) or echoing it (prostrate). Coined by Professor Sendy.

parts.label: informal, nonce-word; chiefly in the template Superkirki-___-expiali-___state

senses.def: An ornate, deliberately ridiculous superlative term for a condition of life so extreme it can only be named by stuffing the whole situation inside one impossibly long word; specifically, a state of being thoroughly snowed-in and at the mercy of a blizzard.

examples.text: Three feet overnight and the plow never came: it is officially a superkirkiblizzardrificexpialisnowstate out there.

subsenses.def: By extension, any overwhelming predicament dressed up in the same mock-grand register, the embedded phrase naming the trouble and the -state suffix naming its result.

example.text: Bumper to bumper for an hour: superkirkislidintrafficexpialislowstate.

senses.def: The template itself regarded as a productive form, into which a speaker may slot any misfortune and a matching rhyming noun to manufacture a fresh word on the spot.

examples.text: Pump the furnace till the house glows: superkirkiheatyerfurnaceexpialiglowstate.
Drop to your knees and beg: superkirkiprayformercyexpialiprostrate.

senses.def: An exuberant nonsense exclamation in the manner of the Mary Poppins original, blurted to dramatize or lament a wintry or otherwise dire turn of events.

examples.text: Sleet, wind, and a dead heater: superkirkisuckyweatherexpialiblowstate!

notes: No captions/SRT exist for this video, so there is no spoken gloss; the entry is reconstructed entirely from the on-screen text, which is consistent across all frames (f01, f03, f05, f06). The headword is taken from the post title and the top text box: "Superkirkiblizzardrificexpialisnowstate" (blizzard + snow). The video is a single-template wordplay piece riffing on "supercalifragilisticexpialidocious," displaying five variants on the frame Superkirki-[situation]-expiali-[noun]state: (1) blizzardrific...snowstate [headword], (2) slidintraffic...slowstate, (3) prayformercy...prostrate, (4) heatyerfurnace...glowstate, (5) suckyweather...blowstate. ENRICHMENT (mine, not literally stated by Sendy, who provides no audio definition): the dictionary definitions, the gloss of -state as "the resulting condition," the breakdown of super-/kirki-/-rific/expiali- in the etymology, the IPA, the interjection part of speech, sense 1a (extension to any predicament), sense 2 (the template as a productive form), and all example sentences (which dramatize his on-screen variants into full sentences). Confidence is medium because Sendy's exact intended meaning is inferred from structure rather than confirmed by speech; the spelling itself is high-confidence from the frames. The description field also contains "stlbrendonofficial@gmail.com," apparently an unrelated business-contact email and not part of the word.

etymology: A double wombo built atop the Mary Poppins nonce-word "supercalifragilisticexpialidocious." Sendy first womboizes the source phrase into the intermediate form "Superkirkiflowilisticexpialibrostate," then compresses it by elision into "Supirklowifexbrate" — fusing super- with kirk(i)- (a wombo infix), -flow(i)-, -fex- (from -frag-/-fic-), and -brate (from -brous/-state). The companion term, "Lokirenloclotate," similarly contracts "Lowkirkentologiclowstate," a wombo descending from genuinely → lowkenuinely → lowkirkenuinely → lowkirkentologically; this strain combines low- (understated), -kirk(en)- (the shared wombo joint), -loc(lo)- (a reduplicated low-), and -tate. The pair is an exercise in iterative wombofication: a long word is fed through the wombo machine and then collapsed to its compressed deadpan core.

parts.label: the Supirklowifexbrate strain; chiefly attributive
parts.forms: intermediate form Superkirkiflowilisticexpialibrostate; compressed form Supirklowifexbrate

senses.def: Of a thing or experience: so wonderful, dazzling, or beyond ordinary description that no plain word will serve — the womboized successor to the nonce-adjective from which it descends, expressing exuberant, almost ineffable delight.

subsenses.def: (of an experience) overwhelmingly marvelous; producing a giddy, can't-quite-say-it elation.

example.text: The whole afternoon was, frankly, supirklowifexbrate.

subsenses.def: (of a word or utterance) extravagantly long and elaborate; coined chiefly for the joy of saying it aloud.

example.text: It is a supirklowifexbrate sort of word — you say it when you've nothing else to say.

senses.def: Of a wombo: produced by feeding an already-fanciful long word through the wombo machine and then compressing the result by elision, so that the finished term is a tightened, deadpan distillation of a grander original.

examples.text: Take superkirkiflowilisticexpialibrostate, tighten it, and you arrive at supirklowifexbrate.

parts.label: the Lokirenloclotate strain; the understated companion term
parts.forms: ladder form Lowkirkentologiclowstate; compressed form Lokirenloclotate; cf. terminal clinical form lowkirkentologiclowstosis

senses.def: Of a state or manner: quietly, deliberately low-key; characterized by a studied understatement that has been built up, syllable by syllable, from the plain adverb 'genuinely.'

subsenses.def: calmly modest; the deadpan opposite number to the exuberant supirklowifexbrate.

example.text: He kept the whole thing lokirenloclotate — no fuss, no fanfare.

subsenses.def: designating the escalating word-ladder itself, in which genuinely becomes lowkenuinely, then lowkirkenuinely, then lowkirkentologically, and at last collapses into lokirenloclotate.

example.text: Genuinely, lowkenuinely, lowkirkenuinely — and so, by stages, lokirenloclotate.

notes: NO captions file (.srt) exists for this video, so the precise meaning Sendy assigns spoken aloud could not be confirmed; confidence set to LOW. The definitions below are RECONSTRUCTED from the on-screen text alone. Authoritative on-screen spellings (from frames): the source/intermediate forms appear in the larger black boxes as "Superkirkiflowilisticexpialibrostate" and "Lowkirkentologiclowstate," with the final compressed wombo forms in the smaller boxes as "Supirklowifexbrate" and "Lokirenloclotate" — these final forms are taken as the headwords. The t-shirt visibly shows an escalating-suffix ladder (genuinely / lowkenuinely / lowkirkenuinely / lowkirkentologically / lowkirkentologiclowstate / lowkirkentologiclowstosis), which I used to infer the "iterative build-up" mechanic. ENRICHMENT (mine, not literally stated by Sendy): the gloss tying Supirklowifexbrate to a feeling of dazzling-but-unspeakable delight (extrapolated from the Mary Poppins source), the "understated/low-key" reading of the Lokirenloclotate strain (extrapolated from the low-/lowkey- roots), the IPA renderings, the usage examples, and the cross-reference to "lowkirkentologiclowstosis" as a clinical-sounding terminal form. No on-screen blue (Root + Root) etymology boxes were present; etymology is derived from the visible word-ladders.

etymology: A blend (or "wombo") of tea, internet slang for gossip or scandalous news (itself from the African American Vernacular English use of "T" for "truth"), and delicioso, the Spanish and Italian word for "delicious." The compound transposes the savoring of fine food onto the consumption of gossip. The terminal -oso preserves the romance-language flourish, lending the term a mock-gourmet register.

parts.forms: comparative more tealicioso; superlative most tealicioso

senses.def: of gossip or scandalous news: so rich, juicy, and satisfying as to be savored like fine cuisine; deliciously scandalous.

subsenses.def: of a particular item of hearsay: of the highest quality or piquancy; prime, choice.

example.text: When the gossip is peanema, that is when it is truly tealicioso.

subsenses.def: by extension, of a conversation or exchange: pleasurably steeped in such gossip.

example.text: We sat for hours, and every word of it was tealicioso.

examples.text: When the gossip is peanema, it is tealicioso.

senses.def: an item of gossip regarded as exceptionally delicious; a morsel of prime scandal.

examples.text: She brought a tealicioso to the table that no one could resist.

notes: No spoken captions exist for this video (the SRT file is absent), so the entry is built entirely from the on-screen text box ('Tealicioso'), the etymology boxes ('Tea + Delicioso'), and the on-screen definition card ('When the gossip is peanema'). HOUSE ENRICHMENT flagged: the comparative/superlative forms, sub-senses 1a and 1b, the second example sentence under 1b, the derived noun part of speech with its plural and example, and the synonyms list are all original house-style additions consistent with his meaning, not things Sendy literally stated.

etymology: Blend (wombo) of TESTAMENT + RIDICULE, coined by Professor Sendy. The first element contributes the sense of a thing that "stands as a testament" — i.e., a genuine, exemplary instance — while the second supplies the notion of mockery and contempt, yielding a noun for something that stands as a perfect monument to its own deserved derision. The spelling coincides with the obsolete diminutive "testicule" (little testicle), a collision the coinage deliberately exploits for comic effect.

senses.def: A person, thing, or situation that thoroughly deserves to be mocked; an unequivocal object of ridicule that is both contemptible and bad.

examples.text: This class is a testicule — the brofessor won't let us say any wombos.

senses.def: Of a person or behavior: increasingly deserving of ridicule; growing more contemptible or laughably objectionable.

examples.text: If he keeps getting more and more testiculous, I think the whole class is going to lose it.

parts.label: slang
parts.forms: testiculedrop; past testiculedropped; chiefly in the extended blend testiculedrop (Testament + Ridicule + Eavesdrop)

senses.def: To eavesdrop on a conversation in a manner that is itself worthy of ridicule; to intrude on others' talk so as to invite mockery.

examples.text: Did you see how that weird unc testiculedropped on our conversation?

notes: On-screen text box gives the authoritative spelling "Testicule" with etymology boxes "Testament + Ridicule." Captions confirm the meaning: "something that is definitely worthy of ridicule and sucks." Sendy demonstrates three forms in the clip — the base noun ("this class is a testicule"), the adjective "testiculous" ("if he keeps getting more and more testiculous"), and an extended triple-blend "testiculedrop" (Testament + Ridicule + Eavesdrop), defined by his usage as eavesdropping in a ridicule-worthy, cringeworthy manner ("that weird unc testiculedropped on our conversation"). The adjective sense (sense 2) and the testiculedrop sub-entry (part 3) are Sendy's own on-camera extensions, not house enrichment. The pejorative-adjective gloss for "testiculous" and the IPA are rendered in house style. Several other wombos appear in his example sentences (quiche, peanemis, Vagynya, nonchalashout, clarpet, jepstenuinely, cletty, vagynyaMaxxes) but are incidental flavor, not the headword.

etymology: A wombo (word + combo) blending text (written words on a page or screen) with particular (the medial syllables of testicular, here recast as a fussiness about the form in which words are consumed) and dysfunction (an impairment of normal operation). Modeled phonetically on the medical term "erectile dysfunction," the coinage frames a preference for audio over print as a mock-clinical condition. Coined by Professor Sendy (2026).

senses.def: A facetiously diagnosed condition characterized by an inability or unwillingness to read written text, compensated for by listening to the same material as audio; an aversion to reading paired with a preference for hearing books and documents read aloud.

examples.text: I had to read a book for my chemistry class, so I scanned it with an app that reads it out loud to me, because I have texticular dysfunction.
I'd rather hear a book and do something else, so I listened to it and actually got there in time.

subsenses.def: Specifically, the impulse to convert any assigned reading into audio in order to save time and multitask.

example.text: When a professor assigns you a huge document for homework, don't freak out, don't get Ohio, just listen to it.

subsenses.def: By extension, a general disposition toward listening rather than reading as one's default mode of taking in written information.

example.text: Use this wombo when you don't like to read but you like to listen to books and save time.

notes: On-screen black title box reads "Texticular Dysfuction" (the "n" in "Dysfunction" is dropped, an apparent typo); headword normalized to "Texticular Dysfunction" per the blue etymology box and the post description ("Texticular Dysfunction (Text + Particular + Dysfunction)"), both of which spell it correctly. Etymology given as Text + Particular + Dysfunction; the construction also puns on the medical phrase "erectile/testicular dysfunction," which is the intended joke. Sub-senses (a, b), the plural form, the part-of-speech, the IPA, and the synonyms are house-style enrichment consistent with Sendy's stated meaning; the core definition (preferring to listen rather than read) is exactly what he describes. Video is a Speechify partnership promo built around the wombo definition. Year 2026 from upload_date 20260605.

etymology: A maximalist agglutinative coinage assembled by Professor Sendy, formed by chaining together a long sequence of his prior wombos, slang fragments, and brand-name flourishes into a single uninterrupted string, then presenting that whole string doubled (hence "x2"). The recurring root LOWKIRK (from low + the personal name Kirk, taken as a marker of low-key, understated cool) anchors the construction, which threads in echoes of supercalifragilisticexpialidocious, Lollapalooza, Coachella, Lululemon, yeet, rizz, aura, and numerous Sendy formations (e.g. -kinetic, -entologic, -authentic). The terminal element LOWKAURA (low + aura) caps the word.

parts.label: Sendy's Long Word format
parts.forms: the full string: Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlauruperkirkiflowilisticexpialibrostaglussinadouarterzipaloozachellamogginrizdeadamentalockinlowingbaunergeticyeetkineticsendergibonoculearnableundereppyeakohotictrabyboouinluperkirkflowendelululemoniminalowkirkilistic — repeated twice and closing on lowkaura

senses.def: An extended, deliberately unbroken compound word built by Professor Sendy by chaining many of his coinages and slang fragments into one continuous string; in this instance the whole string is presented twice in succession (the "x2" variant), as a feat of recital and a showcase of accumulated wombos.

examples.text: And now, The Long Word times two: Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlauruperkirkiflowilisticexpialibrostaglussinadouarterzipaloozachellamogginrizdeadamentalockinlowing... lowkaura.

senses.def: By extension, the quality the word is meant to embody: an effortless, low-key yet maximal cool that piles every desirable trait into a single understated whole, anchored by the core notion of LOWKIRK.

subsenses.def: LOWKIRK: a state of understated, low-key cool; being effortlessly impressive without trying.

example.text: He didn't even flex, he just stood there being completely lowkirk.

subsenses.def: LOWKAURA: the quiet, low-key aura that radiates from such a person; the closing note of the Long Word.

example.text: You can feel his lowkaura from across the room.

senses.def: Possessing low-key, understated cool; impressive in a deliberately unflashy way.

examples.text: That fit is so lowkirk it doesn't even have to say anything.

notes: No SRT/spoken captions exist for this video, so there is no narrated single-sense definition; the entry is reconstructed from the on-screen text box, the post description, and the title, which makes it a special-format 'Long Word' artifact rather than a typical single-root wombo. The frame shows NO blue (Root + Root) etymology boxes, only the long word twice ending in 'lowkaura' — consistent with the doubled-string format. The full headword string is verbatim from the authoritative on-screen/description text. Confidence is medium because the meaning is inferred from format and the visible LOWKIRK/LOWKAURA anchors (and the linked LOWKIRK merch) rather than from an explicit spoken definition. House-style enrichment: the IPA, part-of-speech treatment, and sense gloss for the LOWKIRK/LOWKAURA core are tasteful additions consistent with Sendy's evident meaning; the long string itself is left unparsed as he presents it.

etymology: Blend (wombo) of thoroughly and frustrated, coined by Professor Sendy. The portmanteau fuses the intensifying adverb "thoroughly" with the past-participial adjective "frustrated," collapsing the two into a single overlapping syllable cluster so that the degree adverb is absorbed wholesale into the emotion it modifies. The related verb "thrustrate" (thoroughly + frustrate) is back-formed by the same logic.

parts.label: informal
parts.forms: thrustrated; comparative more thrustrated; superlative most thrustrated

senses.def: Frustrated to a thorough or complete degree; possessed by frustration so total that ordinary annoyance no longer describes it.

examples.text: I was on a flight to Malibu and I delivered a thrustrated peanemis to the guyknowballogist sitting next to me, because the flight was so rough.

subsenses.def: Of a person: wholly overcome by frustration.

example.text: By the time we landed I was so thrustrated I headed straight for a Malibu Barby's.

subsenses.def: Of an utterance or act, especially a delivered analysis: produced in or expressive of such a state.

example.text: He delivered a thrustrated peanemis mid-flight.

senses.def: To frustrate (someone) thoroughly; to provoke complete frustration in a person.

examples.text: Cecilia told me that my micropeanemises thrustrate her.

notes: On-screen text box gives the authoritative spelling \"Thrustrated\" with etymology \"Thoroughly + Frustrated.\" Auto-captions render the surrounding nonce wombos phonetically; these are Sendy's own additional coinages (sonchalashout, peanemis = Peak Cinema Analysis, guyknowballogist, etc.) and are preserved verbatim in the example sentences as he spoke them. Sendy demonstrates two forms in the captions: the adjective \"thrustrated\" (a thrustrated peanemis) and the verb \"thrustrate\" (glossed on screen as Thoroughly + Frustrate, in \"micropeanemises thrustrate her\"), so both parts of speech are included. The verb sense and the dry usage labels are house-style enrichment consistent with his demonstrated forms; the core meaning (thoroughly frustrated) is exactly what he states.

etymology: A wombo (word + combo) blending TOTAL + THIRST + TRAP, the three constituents giving rise to the colloquial label "Triple T" (i.e., the three initial T's). Formed by clipping "total" to "tot-", excising the medial "thirst," and suffixing "-strap" from "trap," yielding a compact trisyllabic portmanteau. Coined by Professor Sendy (2026).

senses.def: A deliberate bid for attention made by presenting oneself as attractive; a maximal or all-out thirst trap.

subsenses.def: An instance of such a display, especially one posted or performed for an audience.

example.text: I went to the huzzitorium and dropped a Triple T in front of the rendezboos, and they looked at me with tungry eyes.

subsenses.def: By extension, a person's capacity for or characteristic manner of such attention-seeking attractiveness.

example.text: I'm pretty sure they could handle my totstrap.

parts.label: informal
parts.forms: totstraps, totstrapped, totstrapping; commonly drop a Triple T

senses.def: To attempt to attract attention by making oneself appear as attractive as possible; to put out an all-out thirst trap.

examples.text: She dropped a Triple T last night.

notes: On-screen presentation leads with the colloquial label "Triple T" in the black headword box, with the etymology box reading "Total + Thirst + Trap = Totstrap"; the post title likewise gives "Triple T aka Totstrap." I have taken Totstrap as the primary headword since it is the genuine portmanteau (wombo) and recorded Triple T as the everyday variant. Sendy's stated meaning is an attempt to win attention by being attractive (i.e., a maximal thirst trap). The verb sense (def. under VERB) and the labels (slang; informal) are house-style enrichment consistent with his usage ("she dropped a Triple T," "handle my totstrap"); the IPA is a plausible reconstruction. The illustrative caption is dense with other wombos (huzzitorium, rendezboos, tungry, etc.) used as flavor; only the totstrap-relevant portions are quoted.

etymology: Blend of trash + basketball, the medial syllable of basketball being elided so that the two roots interlock at the shared -sk- juncture. Coined by Professor Sendy (2026). The agentive trashketar derives by analogy with such forms as superstar and rockstar (trash + basketball + star), and the further extension trashketarget by attraction of the noun target onto the agentive stem.

senses.def: A pastime resembling basketball but conducted with, or characterized by, refuse; the sport of shooting trash, rather than a regulation ball, toward a hoop or receptacle.

examples.text: Mason, Grayson, and Layson are good at trashketball.

subsenses.def: By extension, the organized professional form of this sport, governed by its own league (compare SendBA).

example.text: They're so good at trashketball they should go to the SendBA.

senses.def: The implement or projectile used in this sport; a wad of trash treated as a ball.
A star player of trashketball; one celebrated for prowess at the sport.

examples.text: But we all know that Skyler, Fyler, and Jetyler are the real trashketars.

senses.def: A trashketball regarded specifically as a thing to be aimed at; the target of a trashketball throw, as distinct from the projectile itself.

examples.text: It's not a trashketball, it's a trashketarget.

notes: On-screen headword TRASHKETBALL with etymology box "Trash + Basketball" (frames f01/f03/f05), authoritative over captions. Sendy demonstrates three forms on screen: trashketball (the sport), trashketar (the star player, trash+basketball+star), and trashketarget (trash+basketball+star+target, reanalyzed as ball-vs-target). Captions also name the SendBA (Sendy's NBA, the governing league) and the player rhyme-strings Mason/Grayson/Layson and Skyler/Fyler/Jetyler. ENRICHMENT beyond Sendy's literal words: noun sense 1a (the professional/league sense) is my inference from his "they should go SendBA" line; noun sense 2 (trashketball as the implement/projectile) is inferred from the trashketarget contrast where he distinguishes the thrown object from the aimed-at object; the etymology gloss of trashketar's -ar via star/superstar analogy and the IPA renderings are original house-style additions. Definitions are paraphrased into dictionary register, not copied. Description is a merch promo (Lowkirk shirt/hat) unrelated to this word.

etymology: A cumulative portmanteau (or "wombo") built by progressive accretion, each new root grafted onto the tail of the preceding form: Tupac (the rapper Tupac Shakur, rendered "2pac") + actually, yielding Tupactually; + aura, yielding Tupactualaura; + opulent, yielding Tupactualaurpulent; + larping (from LARP, "live action role-play," with the gerundial sense of theatrical pretense); + magnetic, yielding the terminal form Tupactualaurpulargnetic. Each junction is fused at a shared phonetic seam (Tupac|tually, |aura, |opulent, |larping, |gnetic), a hallmark of the Sendy stacking method.

senses.def: Of or relating to a quality that, contrary to assumption, genuinely belongs to the rapper Tupac Shakur; really and truly Tupac after all. (Tupac + actually.)

examples.text: You think that swagger is borrowed, but it is Tupactually his.

subsenses.def: Used to correct a misapprehension about Tupac with an air of mild surprise.

example.text: I assumed it was a tribute act; Tupactually, it was the man himself.

senses.def: Possessing the unmistakable, faintly mythic presence radiated by Tupac; carrying a Tupac-grade aura. (+ aura.)

examples.text: He walked in and the whole room went Tupactualaura.

senses.def: Marked by a Tupac-tier aura rendered lavish and conspicuously wealthy; abundantly, ostentatiously rich in presence. (+ opulent.)

examples.text: The gold chains and the calm eyes were pure Tupactualaurpulent.

senses.def: Of an opulent Tupac-aura that is in fact performed or role-played rather than innate; theatrically affecting such presence. (+ larping.)

examples.text: Anybody can dress the part, but he was just Tupactualaurpularping for the camera.

subsenses.def: Disparaging. Of a poseur whose grandeur is staged.

example.text: Stop Tupactualaurpularping and say something real.

senses.def: Terminal form. Of a performed, opulent Tupac-aura so compelling that it draws people irresistibly toward it; magnetically charismatic in the fullest accreted sense. (+ magnetic.)

examples.text: Fake or not, the energy was Tupactualaurpulargnetic and the crowd could not look away.

notes: The on-screen frame is authoritative for spelling; note the terminal coinage is spelled "Tupactualaurpulargnetic" on screen (the post description renders it "Tupactualaurpulargnetic" / "...gnetic" identically). This is a CUMULATIVE wombo: Sendy does not give spoken prose definitions, he simply names each root word as he stacks it (captions: 2pac, actually, Aura, Opulent, Larping, Magnetic). Because no explicit gloss is voiced, the senses below are house-style reconstructions of the meaning implied by each etymological layer rather than transcribed definitions; confidence set to medium for that reason. The shirt plug ("Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura") is unrelated merch for a different wombo and is not part of this entry. The five intermediate forms are recorded as distinct numbered senses to preserve the stacking progression; the headword proper is the final, fully-accreted form.

etymology: Blend of twin + teacher, coined by Professor Sendy. The initial cluster of "twin" is fused with the body of "teacher," yielding a portmanteau in which an educator is recast as one's "twin" — a kindred or doubled figure of instruction.

senses.def: A schoolteacher regarded with the closeness or kinship of a twin; one's instructor reframed as a beloved peer or doppelganger figure.

examples.text: My tweacher has us reading really quiche classics.

subsenses.def: The teacher assigned to or favored by a particular student, especially one whose tastes the student adopts as their own.

example.text: My tweacher has us reading really quiche classics.

subsenses.def: By extension, any mentor or figure of authority treated with the intimacy of a twin or close companion.

example.text: He talks to the professor like she's his tweacher.

notes: This single video defines four wombos in sequence: Tweacher (Twin + Teacher), Brochacho (Bro + Muchacho), Chiche (Cheddar + Quiche), and Demuogging (Demure + Mogging). Per the one-entry instruction, the lead headword Tweacher was extracted; the on-screen black-box spelling is authoritative. The frame's blue (Root + Root) boxes confirm the etymology exactly. Sense 1 closely tracks Sendy's own usage ("My tweacher has us reading really quiche classics"). Sub-sense 1b (the affectionate "twin"-as-bestie reading drawn from the slang sense of "twin") is tasteful house-style enrichment consistent with the blend's components; Sendy literally demonstrates only the schoolteacher usage. The example sentence is his actual caption line, lightly cleaned.

etymology: From the contemporary vocative and address term "twin" (a term of endearment for a close friend, comrade, or peer, popularized in online and Gen-Z speech), here treated by Professor Sendy as a productive combining element fused into the opening syllable of countless host words. The video presents a rapid-fire paradigm of such fusions: twin + what's up → twat's up; twin + info → twinfo; twin + bet → twet; twin + NPC → TwinPC; twin + unc → twunc; twin + into → twinto; tuff + twin → tuffin; twin + intuition → twintuition; twin + imposter → twimposter.

parts.label: Sendyism
parts.forms: also as proclitic twin-, twi-, tw-

senses.def: A productive prefixal element derived from the address-term "twin," fused into the leading syllable of a host word so that the two are pronounced as one; used to mark the resulting utterance as spoken to or about a close companion.

examples.text: Twin plus what's up makes twat's up.
Twin plus info makes twinfo.

subsenses.def: twat's up (twin + what's up): a fused greeting addressed to one's companion.

example.text: He walked in and said twat's up to the whole group.

subsenses.def: twinfo (twin + info): information shared with or concerning one's companion.

example.text: Let me give you the twinfo on what happened.

senses.def: The same element used to recast assorted slang, nouns, and figures of speech in the companionate register, yielding a paradigm of blends.

examples.text: Twin plus bet makes twet.
Twin plus NPC makes TwinPC.

subsenses.def: twet (twin + bet): an affirmation of agreement made to a companion.

example.text: Wanna link later? Twet.

subsenses.def: TwinPC (twin + NPC): a companion regarded as an undifferentiated background character.

example.text: He just stood there like a TwinPC.

subsenses.def: twunc (twin + unc): an older companion treated with familial deference.

example.text: Ask twunc, he was there back in the day.

senses.def: Applied to verbs, abstract nouns, and descriptors, marking each as belonging to or filtered through the companion relationship.

examples.text: Twin plus into makes twinto.
Twin plus intuition makes twintuition.

subsenses.def: twinto (twin + into): the state of being keen on or absorbed in something alongside one's companion.

example.text: We're both twinto the new game.

subsenses.def: tuffin (tuff + twin): a companion regarded as formidable or impressive; rendered as the only blend in the set built tuff-first.

example.text: My guy is a real tuffin.

subsenses.def: twintuition (twin + intuition): the instinctive, wordless understanding shared between close companions.

example.text: I knew you'd call — pure twintuition.

subsenses.def: twimposter (twin + imposter): one who only pretends to be a true companion.

example.text: Turns out he was a twimposter the whole time.

notes: This video is a rapid-fire SERIES of wombos rather than a single defined term, all built on the address-term root "twin." The captions only speak each formula aloud (e.g. "twin / what's up / twat's up") without giving a spoken meaning for each coinage, so the senses below are house-style reconstructions consistent with the obvious blend logic and Sendy's demonstrated pattern; this enrichment is flagged here per instructions. The headword is rendered as the combining form "twin-" because no single coinage receives its own definition on-camera; the nine attested blends are captured as a numbered paradigm. On-screen spellings are authoritative and preserved exactly (note "TwinPC" with its odd "+" rendering on screen, and "Tuffin" formed as tuff+twin rather than twin-first). The shirt plug ("Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura") and Speechify/Cameo lines are sponsor boilerplate, not part of the lexical content. The IPA given is for the root element; individual blends would vary.

etymology: A compounding pattern in which the word *twin* is fused to the front of an existing noun, displacing its initial consonant or syllable so that the result opens on the *tw-* cluster (e.g. *twin* + *cinema* → *twinema*; *twin* + *parent* → *twarent*). Coined and demonstrated en masse by Professor Sendy, who presented the set as "Twin Wombos (Part Two)," extending his earlier survey of the same construction.

senses.def: A class of wombo formed by grafting the word twin onto the front of a base word, with the twin element overwriting that word's opening sound or syllable so that the blend begins with the tw- cluster.

examples.text: Twin Wombos, Part Two: Twin plus Cinema equals Twinema; Twin plus Prophet equals Twophet.

subsenses.def: An individual word produced by this pattern, as twinema, twophet, twarent, twa'am, twir, fanum twax, twynecologist, twerk, twastor, tweet, or twanger.

example.text: Twin plus Gynecologist gives the Twin Wombo twynecologist.

senses.def: twarent: the headword foregrounded by the on-screen caption; a blend of twin and parent, denoting a parent who happens to be a twin or who is presented in twin terms.

examples.text: Twin plus Parent equals Twarent.

notes: This is a rapid-fire compilation, not a single-word definition. The on-screen black box reads "Twin Wombos (Part Two)" and lists eleven constructions, each shown as Twin + [base word] = [wombo]; Sendy only recites the formulas and does not give a spoken gloss for any individual term. I therefore took the named construction "Twin Wombos" as the headword and documented the productive pattern itself, with the individual blends as a sub-sense and as examples drawn verbatim from the frame. ENRICHMENT (flagged): the formula in the etymology (twin overwriting the base word's onset to surface a tw- cluster), the gloss for the captioned word "twarent," the IPA, and the synonyms are house-style additions consistent with the construction; Sendy does not state meanings for the entries. On-screen spellings are authoritative and were used over the auto-caption ("twaster"/"twastor" appears as "Twastor" on screen; captions also show "yeet"→"tweet" where the frame lists "Twin + Tweet = Tweet," but the frame text was preferred). The frame's listed pairs are: Cinema→Twinema, Prophet→Twophet, Parent→Twarent, Ma'am→Twa'am, Sir→Twir, Fanum Tax→Fanum Twax, Gynecologist→Twynecologist, Jerk→Twerk, Pastor→Twastor, (Tweet)→Tweet, Stranger→Twanger.

etymology: A blend of TWIN and PRINCIPAL, the terminal and titular entry in Professor Sendy's "Twin Wombos" cycle, in which the word TWIN is fused to a succession of familial and institutional roles. The series proceeds: Twin + Bruh = Twuh; Twin + Bro = Two; Twin + Sister = Twister; Twin + Opp = Twopp; Twin + Teacher = Tweacher; Twin + Professor = Twofessor; Twin + Father = Twather; Twin + Mother = Twother; Twin + Cousin = Twousin; and Twin + Principal = Twincipal. The unifying device is the substitution of an initial tw- onto the second root, so that the notion of twinhood is carried entirely by the opening syllable while the role-word supplies the remainder. Compare the parallel reductive series in which Twin + Bro collapses, improbably, to the pre-existing numeral "Two."

parts.label: the Twin Wombos
parts.forms: plural twincipals; and corresponding plurals of the related forms (twuhs, twisters, twopps, etc.)

senses.def: A school principal who is a twin; the head of a school considered specifically in relation to his or her co-twin. The capstone coinage of Professor Sendy's twin-series, formed by fusing twin onto principal.

examples.text: When the front office sent home two identical disciplinary notices, the students realized the school was run by a twincipal.

senses.def: More broadly, any member of the productive class of twin-wombos: a person designated by his or her familial or institutional role together with the fact of being a twin, the two being collapsed into a single fused word.

subsenses.def: Twuh (twin + bruh): one's twin considered as a casual male intimate or 'bruh'; a twin brother addressed informally.

example.text: He dapped up the kid in the mirror like, that's my twuh.

subsenses.def: Two (twin + bro): a twin brother; by Sendy's reckoning the contraction lands, against all odds, on the ordinary numeral two.

example.text: I don't have a brother, I have a two.

senses.def: Twister (twin + sister): a sister who is also one's twin; a twin sister. Not to be confused with the rotating windstorm or the floor game of the same spelling.

examples.text: My twister and I finish each other's sentences.

senses.def: Twopp (twin + opp): a twin who has become one's adversary; a sibling rival or enemy who shares one's birth.

examples.text: We came out of the womb together and now he's my twopp.

senses.def: Tweacher (twin + teacher); and twofessor (twin + professor): an instructor who is a twin, at the secondary and tertiary levels respectively.

examples.text: Two identical lecturers walked in and the class realized it had a twofessor.

senses.def: Twather (twin + father); and twother (twin + mother): a parent who is a twin, paternal and maternal respectively.

examples.text: My twather has an identical brother who looks exactly like my dad.

senses.def: Twousin (twin + cousin): a cousin who happens to be a twin; a twin within the extended family.

examples.text: At the reunion I couldn't tell my twousin apart from her sister.

notes: This is a compilation ("Twin Wombos") rather than a single-word definition. The on-screen black box lists ten blends with their (Root + Root) etymologies: Twin+Bruh=Twuh, Twin+Bro=Two, Twin+Sister=Twister, Twin+Opp=Twopp, Twin+Teacher=Tweacher, Twin+Professor=Twofessor, Twin+Father=Twather, Twin+Mother=Twother, Twin+Cousin=Twousin, Twin+Principal=Twincipal. The spoken captions read the list aloud in pairs (root, then blend) and match the frame exactly. I chose 'Twincipal' as the headword because it is the final, titular blend and the segment's punchline (the on-screen lower-third caption at this frame reads TWISTER, simply tracking the word being spoken at that instant). The numbered senses and sub-senses gather the whole productive paradigm under one entry; each gloss ('a twin who is also X') is original house-style prose extrapolated from Sendy's literal Root+Root equations, since he supplies only the blends, not narrative definitions or sentence examples. All usage examples are therefore house-style illustrations consistent with each blend's plain meaning, not verbatim Sendy quotations. IPA is plausible/reconstructed. The description also references an unrelated merch item ('Lowkirkentologiclowstatothenticlaura shirt'), ignored here. Year 2026 from upload_date 20260326.
